topics.title: Cell Membrane and Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cell Membrane and Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Cell Membrane — Formal Academic Definition

The **cell membrane** (also called the *plasma membrane* or *sarcolemma* in muscle cells) is a **~7–10 nm thick, amphipathic, selectively permeable lipid bilayer** that physically encloses the cytoplasm of every living cell. According to the widely accepted **Fluid-Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972), the membrane is composed of:

- A **phospholipid bilayer** forming the structural matrix.
- **Integral proteins** (ion channels, transporters, receptors) embedded within the bilayer.
- **Peripheral proteins** loosely attached to the membrane surfaces.
- **Cholesterol molecules** interspersed between phospholipids, regulating membrane fluidity.
- **Glycoproteins and glycolipids** projecting from the outer surface, responsible for cell recognition and signalling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The membrane is the **fundamental transducer** of every bioelectrical signal. Without an intact, polarized membrane, **no action potential** can be generated or propagated. In biomedical instrumentation, this is why electrodes (e.g., ECG, EMG, EEG) only pick up signals arising from excitable cells (neurons, cardiomyocytes, muscle fibres).

## 1.2 Action Potential — Formal Academic Definition

An **action potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization and repolarization of the cell membrane's electrical potential, lasting approximately **1–2 milliseconds** in neurons and **200–300 milliseconds** in cardiac ventricular myocytes. It is the basic unit of information transfer in the nervous system and the trigger for muscle contraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Nernst/Goldman Reference Values (KTU-Expected Memorization Set):**
> - **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** −70 mV (standard convention: outside positive, inside negative).
> - **Threshold Potential:** approximately **−55 mV** (the "firing point").
> - **Peak Overshoot Potential:** approximately **+30 to +40 mV**.
> - **Hyperpolarization (After-Potential):** dips to roughly **−80 mV** before returning to RMP.
> - **Body Temperature (T):** **310 K (37°C)** — used in all Nernst calculations.
> - **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**.
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Bilingual Security Gate of a Building":**
> Imagine a high-security office building where the cell membrane is the **main security gate**. The gate has **selective turnstiles** (ion channels) — one for **Na⁺ ions** (sodium turnstile), one for **K⁺ ions** (potassium turnstile), and one for **Cl⁻ ions** (chloride turnstile).
> - At rest, the gate is **slightly closed and biased**: K⁺ leaks out more easily than Na⁺ leaks in. The building interior is **negatively charged** (−70 mV) relative to outside.
> - When a stimulus (a key card swipe) reaches the **threshold** (−55 mV), the sodium turnstile **slams open**, flooding the interior with positive charge — this is **depolarization**.
> - Security then slams the sodium gate shut and forces the potassium gate open, pumping positive charge out — this is **repolarization**.
> - The gate **over-corrects** slightly, leaving the building more negative than at rest — **hyperpolarization**.
> - Finally, a **janitor (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump)** uses energy (ATP) to restore the original ionic distribution.
>
> The action potential is the **rolling wave of gate-flipping** that sweeps down the corridor (the axon), carrying the "message" to the next room.

This gate-flipping is, electrically, what an ECG electrode (sensitive to ±1 mV) and an EEG electrode (sensitive to ±10 µV) detect from outside the body.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Does a Resting Potential Exist?

The resting potential arises because the cell membrane is **selectively permeable** and the **intracellular and extracellular ionic concentrations differ dramatically**. The following ionic gradients are established and maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, per ATP hydrolyzed):

| Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] | Equilibrium Potential (Nernst, ~37°C) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **K⁺ (Potassium)** | ~140 | ~4–5 | ≈ −94 mV |
| **Na⁺ (Sodium)** | ~10–15 | ~145 | ≈ +60 to +70 mV |
| **Cl⁻ (Chloride)** | ~5–10 | ~100–110 | ≈ −65 to −70 mV |
| **Ca²⁺ (Calcium)** | ~0.0001 | ~1.2 | ≈ +120 mV |

At rest, the membrane is **~25–30× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**, so the resting potential is dominated by the K⁺ Nernst potential. The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation formally computes this.

## 2.2 The Six Phases of an Action Potential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Sub-Topic:** Students are repeatedly asked to draw and label the AP waveform. Memorize the **five distinct phases** below — they map 1:1 to specific ion channel states.

1. **Phase 0 — Resting State (≈ −70 mV):** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels closed (activation gate shut, inactivation gate open). Voltage-gated K⁺ channels closed. Leak channels maintain RMP.
2. **Phase 1 — Depolarization to Threshold (≈ −70 → −55 mV):** A stimulus (synaptic, mechanical, or electrode-induced) opens some Na⁺ channels. Na⁺ flows **inward** down its electrochemical gradient.
3. **Phase 2 — Rapid Depolarization / Upstroke (≈ −55 → +30 mV):** At threshold, the *regenerative* positive-feedback loop fires: more Na⁺ entry → more depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open. The membrane approaches the Na⁺ Nernst potential (+60 mV).
4. **Phase 3 — Repolarization (≈ +30 → −70 mV):** Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (a separate ball-and-chain inactivation gate). Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open slowly. K⁺ efflux drives the membrane back toward the K⁺ Nernst potential.
5. **Phase 4 — Hyperpolarization / Undershoot (≈ −70 → −80 → −70 mV):** K⁺ channels are slow to close, so K⁺ efflux overshoots. The membrane briefly becomes *more* negative than RMP (the "after-hyperpolarization").
6. **Phase 5 — Return to Resting:** Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores the original ionic distribution using ATP. The membrane is now ready to fire again.

## 2.3 The All-or-Nothing Principle & Refractory Periods

- **All-or-Nothing Law:** Once threshold (−55 mV) is reached, the AP fires to its full amplitude (~+30 mV). A *stronger* stimulus does **not** produce a *larger* AP — it only produces a *larger* frequency of APs (rate coding).
- **Absolute Refractory Period:** During Phase 2–early Phase 3, no stimulus, however strong, can elicit a second AP (Na⁺ channels are inactivated). This sets the **maximum firing frequency**.
- **Relative Refractory Period:** During Phase 3–Phase 4, only a *supra-threshold* stimulus can trigger an AP (K⁺ channels still open, membrane is hyperpolarized).

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Engineering Relevance:** The absolute refractory period is what makes the **cardiac action potential longer than the neuronal one** — the long plateau (Phase 2) in cardiac cells (driven by L-type Ca²⁺ channels) prevents tetanic contraction, ensuring the heart fills and empties rhythmically. This is why an **ECG waveform (P-QRS-T)** is reliable and rhythmic, while a nerve can fire rapid bursts.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these four equations** — they appear in nearly every KTU board question on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Where Used |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Nernst Equation** (single ion) | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ | Equilibrium potential of one ion species |
| 2 | **Nernst Equation (log₁₀ form, 37°C)** | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} \text{ mV}$ | Quick numerical KTU problems |
| 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Membrane potential with multiple permeant ions |
| 4 | **GHK Current Equation (Density)** | $I = P \cdot \dfrac{z^2 F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{[C]_i - [C]_o e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$ | Single-channel ionic current through a permeable membrane |

**Critical Boundary Conditions for the KTU Board:**

- If membrane is **only permeable to K⁺** (i.e., $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$) → GHK collapses to the **Nernst equation for K⁺**, giving $V_m \approx -94$ mV.
- During **peak of AP**, membrane is **only permeable to Na⁺** → $V_m \approx +60$ mV.
- If $V_m = 0$ mV → no net ionic current flows through a perfectly selective channel.
- Standard KTU numerical value at 37°C: $\dfrac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71$ mV, so $\dfrac{RT}{F} \ln(10) \approx 61.5$ mV.

## 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These equations are not academic curiosities. They directly govern the design of:
> - **Defibrillators and Pacemakers** — must time-deliver current during the absolute refractory period of cardiac tissue to avoid inducing ventricular fibrillation.
> - **Neural Stimulators (DBS, cochlear implants, retinal prosthetics)** — must generate supra-threshold stimuli to recruit action potentials in target neurons.
> - **ECG/EMG/EEG amplifiers** — front-end gain and bandwidth are chosen to capture the small extracellular AP derivatives (µV–mV range).
> - **Microelectrode arrays and patch-clamp systems** — used in drug discovery to measure how pharmaceutical compounds shift Nernst/GHK potentials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation (From First Principles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Setup:** Consider a membrane separating two solutions of a monovalent cation (e.g., K⁺). Ion flow across the membrane is driven by two opposing forces: (a) a **chemical concentration gradient** (diffusion) and (b) an **electrical gradient** (drift). At equilibrium, the net work done by these two forces is zero.

### Step 1 — Work done by the chemical gradient

For one mole of ions moving from inside (concentration $[C]_i$) to outside (concentration $[C]_o$), the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{chem}} = RT \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}$$

This is the standard chemical potential difference; positive if the ion moves from low to high concentration (against the gradient).

### Step 2 — Work done by the electrical gradient

If the ion carries charge $zF$ per mole and the membrane potential is $E_{\text{ion}}$ (inside relative to outside), the electrical work to move one mole from inside to outside is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{elec}} = zF \cdot E_{\text{ion}}$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium condition

At electrochemical equilibrium, the **total Gibbs free energy change is zero**. The two forces exactly balance.

$$\Delta G_{\text{chem}} + \Delta G_{\text{elec}} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $E_{\text{ion}}$

$$RT \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i} + zF E_{\text{ion}} = 0$$

$$E_{\text{ion}} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}$$

### Step 5 — Flip the log ratio for the conventional "out over in" form

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}$$

Some textbooks write it as the **out-over-in** form by using $[C]_o / [C]_i$ and **dropping the negative sign** when the ion is a cation. KTU accepts both notations — but the **sign convention must be consistent** (inside minus outside potential). The full Nernst form used universally in physiology is:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (KTU board form)

Using $\ln(x) = 2.3026 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$ and at body temperature $T = 310$ K:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{2.3026 \cdot R \cdot 310}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} \text{ mV}$$

This is the form students must memorize for KTU numerical problems.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

Compute the K⁺ equilibrium potential at 37°C, given $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM.

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{4}{140}$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.02857)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.5441)$$

$$E_K \approx -94.97 \text{ mV}$$

This matches the expected physiological value of ≈ −94 mV, confirming the derivation. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Setup:** The Nernst equation assumes the membrane is permeable to **only one ion**. Real membranes are permeable to **multiple ions simultaneously**. The GHK equation extends Nernst by computing the membrane potential that would simultaneously keep the **net ionic current (I_total) = 0** for multiple permeant ion species.

### Step 1 — Starting from the GHK Current Equation

For a single ionic species, the current density through a membrane with permeability $P$ is:

$$I = P \cdot z^2 \cdot \frac{F^2}{RT} \cdot V_m \cdot \frac{[C]_i - [C]_o \, e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$$

### Step 2 — At steady state, sum of all ionic currents = 0

$$I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Cl} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the GHK current form for each ion

$$P_{Na} \cdot \frac{F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[Na]_i - [Na]_o e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} + P_K \cdot \frac{F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[K]_i - [K]_o e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} - P_{Cl} \cdot \frac{F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[Cl]_o - [Cl]_i e^{+FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{+FV_m/RT}} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the algebra (factor out common terms)

After cancellation of the common prefactor $\dfrac{F^2 V_m}{RT}$, the exponential terms collapse using the identity $\dfrac{1 - e^{-x}}{1 - e^{+x}} = e^{-x}$ pattern. The result is the **GHK Voltage Equation**:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key notational rule for the KTU board:** The **numerator contains the OUTSIDE concentration of cations** (K⁺, Na⁺) and the **INSIDE concentration of anions** (Cl⁻). The **denominator is the reverse**. Sign convention: $V_m$ = inside potential minus outside potential. If your answer comes out *positive*, the inside is more positive than the outside (depolarized); if *negative*, the inside is more negative (polarized/resting).

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

Given:
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $P_K = 1$ (reference)
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 10$ mM, $P_{Na} = 0.04$
- $[Cl^-]_o = 100$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 5$ mM, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$

**Numerator:**

$$N = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(5) = 4 + 5.8 + 2.25 = 12.05$$

**Denominator:**

$$D = (1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(100) = 140 + 0.4 + 45 = 185.4$$

**Membrane potential at 37°C:**

$$V_m = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10} \frac{12.05}{185.4}$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}(0.0650)$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1871)$$

$$V_m \approx -73.0 \text{ mV}$$

This matches the **physiological resting membrane potential of −70 mV** to within physiological tolerance. ✅

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Use This Equation | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single ion dominates permeability | **Nernst** | Two-force equilibrium (chemical + electrical) |
| Multiple ions permeable simultaneously | **GHK Voltage** | Sums weighted contributions by permeability |
| Computing actual current through a channel | **GHK Current** | Gives the *flux*, not just equilibrium potential |
| Threshold-crossing problem | **Nernst for Na⁺** (≈ +60 mV) | At threshold, Na⁺ permeability surges |
| Resting-state check | **Nernst for K⁺** (≈ −94 mV) | RMP is dominated by K⁺ leak |
| Cardiac plateau phase (Phase 2) | **Nernst for Ca²⁺** (≈ +120 mV) | L-type Ca²⁺ channels drive the plateau |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Action Potential Phase Map (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete state machine of the action potential — which channels are open in each phase and the corresponding voltage trajectory. It is designed to be KTU-board-diagram-compliant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 0: RESTING STATE -70 mV NaV closed Kv closed K leak active"] --> B["PHASE 1: STIMULUS APPLIED -70 to -55 mV Few NaV open slow Na entry"]
    B --> C{"THRESHOLD CHECK Vm equals -55 mV"}
    C -- "No return to rest" --> A
    C -- "Yes fire AP" --> D["PHASE 2: RAPID DEPOLARIZATION -55 to plus 30 mV All NaV open Na floods in regenerative"]
    D --> E["PHASE 3: REPOLARIZATION plus 30 to -70 mV NaV inactivate Kv open K flows out"]
    E --> F["PHASE 4: HYPERPOLARIZATION -70 to -80 mV Kv still open overshoot negative"]
    F --> G["PHASE 5: RETURN TO REST -80 to -70 mV NaK ATPase pump restores gradients"]
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Ion Channel State Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

This block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the voltage-gated ion channels during an AP — a topic routinely asked as "draw and label" in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rest["RESTING STATE Vm minus 70 mV"]
        R1["NaV channel: m gate CLOSED, h gate OPEN"]
        R2["Kv channel: CLOSED"]
        R3["K leak channel: OPEN"]
        R4["NaK ATPase pump: ACTIVE 3 Na out 2 K in"]
    end
    subgraph depol["DEPOLARIZATION Vm minus 55 to plus 30 mV"]
        D1["NaV channel: m gate OPEN, h gate OPEN"]
        D2["Kv channel: slowly activating"]
        D3["K leak: OPEN"]
        D4["Driving force: Na in"]
    end
    subgraph repol["REPOLARIZATION Vm plus 30 to minus 70 mV"]
        P1["NaV channel: m gate OPEN, h gate CLOSED inactivated"]
        P2["Kv channel: fully OPEN"]
        P3["Driving force: K out"]
    end
    subgraph hyper["HYPERPOLARIZATION Vm minus 70 to minus 80 mV"]
        H1["NaV channel: reset m closed, h open"]
        H2["Kv channel: slow to close"]
        H3["K efflux overshoots"]
    end
    rest --> depol --> repol --> hyper --> rest
```

## 4.3 Cell Membrane Functional Block Architecture

This is the **Fluid-Mosaic Model** rendered as a Mermaid architecture diagram, suitable for KTU 14-mark "draw the cell membrane" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ext["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE Outside positive"]
        GLYC["Glycoprotein Cell recognition and signalling"]
        REC["Receptor Protein Binds ligands hormones neurotransmitters"]
    end
    subgraph mem["LIPID BILAYER 7 to 10 nm thick"]
        CHOL["Cholesterol Molecule Regulates fluidity"]
        PHOS["Phospholipid Head Hydrophilic outside"]
        TAIL["Pholipid Tail Hydrophobic inside"]
        ICHAN["Integral Ion Channel Na K Ca Cl selective"]
        TRAN["Transporter Protein NaK ATPase pump"]
    end
    subgraph cyto["INTRACELLULAR SPACE Inside negative at rest"]
        CYTOSK["Cytoskeleton Anchor for membrane proteins"]
        ENZ["Peripheral Enzyme Signal transduction"]
    end
    GLYC --- mem
    REC --- mem
    mem --- cyto
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
**Define resting membrane potential. Mention its typical value and the ion primarily responsible for it.**

**Model Answer (KTU Board Key Pattern):**

The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is the steady-state electrical potential difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is not generating an action potential. By convention, it is measured as the **inside potential relative to the outside**.

- **Typical value:** approximately **−70 mV** (inside is 70 mV more negative than the outside).
- **Primary ion responsible:** **Potassium (K⁺)**. At rest, the membrane is **~25–30× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**, so the RMP is dominated by the K⁺ equilibrium (Nernst) potential, which is approximately **−94 mV**. The actual RMP (−70 mV) is slightly less negative because of small but finite Na⁺ and Cl⁻ permeabilities, as computed by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation**.
- The K⁺ gradient itself is maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per ATP).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "RMP = −70 mV" without mentioning **which side is negative** (the inside). Examiners explicitly ask this — and missing the sign convention loses 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
**State the Nernst equation and explain the significance of the term $z$ in it. Calculate the Nernst potential for Ca²⁺ at 37°C if $[Ca^{2+}]_o = 1.2$ mM and $[Ca^{2+}]_i = 0.0001$ mM.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential of a single ion species across a selectively permeable membrane:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

At body temperature (37°C, $T = 310$ K), using $\dfrac{RT}{F} \ln(10) \approx 61.5$ mV:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} \text{ mV}$$

**Significance of $z$:** $z$ is the **valence (charge number) of the ion**, including sign. For K⁺ and Na⁺, $z = +1$. For Ca²⁺, $z = +2$. For Cl⁻, $z = -1$. The factor $z$ in the denominator means **divalent ions** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) produce a **half-magnitude potential** for the same concentration ratio compared to monovalent ions.

**Numerical Calculation for Ca²⁺:**

$$E_{Ca} = \frac{61.5}{+2} \log_{10} \frac{1.2}{0.0001}$$

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times \log_{10}(12000)$$

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times 4.0792$$

$$E_{Ca} \approx +125.4 \text{ mV}$$

This large positive value is why even tiny Ca²⁺ entry drives strong depolarization in cardiac and skeletal muscle cells.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting the **sign of $z$** for anions (Cl⁻ has $z = -1$, NOT +1). For Cl⁻, the equation yields a *negative* $E_{Cl}$ because the concentration gradient pulls Cl⁻ into the cell.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (as per regulation):** Each 14-mark question has **internal choice**. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions — a student answers *one* of them. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation from first principles. Using it, compute the equilibrium potential of K⁺ at 310 K given $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the different phases of an action potential in a neuronal cell. Mention the ion channels involved in each phase.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation:**

Consider a selectively permeable membrane separating two aqueous solutions containing a monovalent cation, e.g., K⁺. At equilibrium, the **net flux of ions across the membrane is zero**. The two opposing forces acting on the ion are:

1. **Chemical (concentration) gradient** — drives net diffusion from high to low concentration.
2. **Electrical gradient** — drives net drift of charged ions along the electric field.

The **change in Gibbs free energy** for moving one mole of ions from inside to outside is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G_{\text{chem}} + \Delta G_{\text{elec}} = RT \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i} + zF E_{\text{ion}}$$

At electrochemical equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$:

$$RT \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i} + zF E_{\text{ion}} = 0$$

$$E_{\text{ion}} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}$$

Equivalently, using the *out-over-in* form (with the negative sign absorbed by sign convention):

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

At $T = 310$ K, using $\ln = 2.3026 \cdot \log_{10}$ and $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02671$ V:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} \text{ mV}$$

**Numerical Calculation for K⁺:**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{150} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03333)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4771) \approx -90.8 \text{ mV}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (7-mark sub-question):**
> - **Step-marking scheme:** Derivation setup = 2 marks, equilibrium condition = 1 mark, final equation = 1 mark, numerical substitution = 2 marks, final answer with correct unit (mV) and sign = 1 mark.
> - **Most common mark-loss:** (i) writing $\ln$ and $\log_{10}$ interchangeably without the 2.3026 factor (lose 1 mark); (ii) forgetting the sign of $z$ for anions in a follow-up part (lose 1 mark); (iii) not writing units (lose 0.5 mark).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Phases of a Neuronal Action Potential** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

| Phase | Voltage Range | Key Event | Ion Channels Involved |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resting** | ≈ −70 mV | RMP maintained; K⁺ leak dominates | K⁺ leak channels, Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump |
| **Depolarization to Threshold** | −70 → −55 mV | Stimulus opens some voltage-gated Na⁺ channels | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels (partial) |
| **Rapid Depolarization (Upstroke)** | −55 → +30 mV | Regenerative Na⁺ influx (positive feedback) | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels (all open) |
| **Repolarization** | +30 → −70 mV | Na⁺ channels inactivate, K⁺ channels open | Voltage-gated K⁺ channels |
| **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** | −70 → −80 mV | K⁺ channels slow to close, overshoot | Voltage-gated K⁺ channels (lingering) |
| **Return to Resting** | −80 → −70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores ionic gradients | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump |

**Key Supporting Points for Full Marks:**
- The **activation gate (m-gate)** of the Na⁺ channel is voltage-sensitive and opens rapidly at threshold.
- The **inactivation gate (h-gate)** is a slower "ball-and-chain" mechanism that closes within ~1 ms.
- The K⁺ channel is **delayed-rectifier** type — opens slowly and closes slowly.
- **All-or-nothing principle** must be stated.
- **Refractory periods** (absolute during Na⁺ inactivation, relative during K⁺ efflux) must be mentioned for full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (7-mark sub-question):**
> - **Most common mark-loss:** (i) drawing the waveform without labelling the *ion channels* responsible for each phase (lose 2 marks); (ii) failing to mention the **threshold value of −55 mV** (lose 0.5 mark); (iii) not mentioning **refractory periods** (lose 1 mark); (iv) confusing Na⁺/K⁺ pump (electrogenic) with Na⁺/K⁺ leak (passive) (lose 1 mark).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation? How does it differ from the Nernst equation? Using GHK, compute the membrane potential at 37°C given:
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $P_K = 1$
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM, $P_{Na} = 0.04$
- $[Cl^-]_o = 100$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 5$ mM, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the "all-or-nothing" principle of action potential generation. Discuss absolute and relative refractory periods with their ionic basis and physiological significance.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

The **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation** gives the steady-state membrane potential when **multiple ion species** are simultaneously permeable:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

**Differences from the Nernst Equation:**

| Feature | Nernst Equation | GHK Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of ions | Single ion | Multiple ions |
| Permeability | Implicit (only that ion) | Explicit ($P$ values) |
| Output | Reversal potential of one ion | Actual membrane potential |
| Validity | At equilibrium for one ion | Steady-state for real membrane |

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Numerator:**

$$N = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(5) = 4 + 5.8 + 2.25 = 12.05$$

**Denominator:**

$$D = (1)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(100) = 140 + 0.48 + 45 = 185.48$$

**Membrane potential:**

$$V_m = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10} \frac{12.05}{185.48} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.06497)$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1874) \approx -73.0 \text{ mV}$$

This is the realistic **resting membrane potential** of a typical neuron.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently write the GHK equation with **all concentrations in the numerator** and forget to flip the **anion (Cl⁻)** position. The **anion concentration is always on the opposite side** from the cations in the GHK equation.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**All-or-Nothing Principle:**

Once the membrane is depolarized to the **threshold potential (−55 mV)**, an action potential is triggered that proceeds to its **full amplitude** (~+30 mV) **regardless of stimulus strength**. A sub-threshold stimulus produces *no* AP; a supra-threshold stimulus produces a *full* AP (not a larger one). Information is encoded not in AP amplitude but in **firing frequency** (rate coding).

**Refractory Periods:**

1. **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):**
   - **Duration:** ~1–2 ms in neurons; ~250 ms in cardiac ventricular cells.
   - **Ionic basis:** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are in the **inactivated** state (h-gate closed, m-gate open). They cannot be reopened regardless of stimulus strength.
   - **Physiological significance:** Sets the **upper limit on AP firing frequency**, ensures **unidirectional propagation** of the AP along the axon, and prevents the cardiac muscle from undergoing **tetanic contraction**.

2. **Relative Refractory Period (RRP):**
   - **Duration:** A few milliseconds after ARP.
   - **Ionic basis:** Na⁺ channels have **recovered from inactivation** (h-gate open), but the membrane is **hyperpolarized** due to lingering K⁺ efflux. A *supra-threshold* stimulus can therefore trigger a new AP, but with reduced amplitude.
   - **Physiological significance:** Allows **frequency modulation** of neural firing; provides a graded recovery window.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Do not confuse ARP with RRP — they have **different ionic mechanisms** and **different durations**.
> - The **cardiac AP is long because of Ca²⁺ influx**, not Na⁺. State this clearly to earn the cardiac-physiology credit.
> - Forgetting to mention the **direction of ion flow** during each refractory period (Na⁺ still leaking in, K⁺ still flowing out) loses 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-Exam Quick-Fire Bullet List — Memorize These Before Walking Into the KTU Hall:**

- **Cell Membrane:** ~7–10 nm thick, lipid bilayer with embedded proteins (Fluid-Mosaic Model).
- **Resting Membrane Potential:** **−70 mV**; dominated by **K⁺ leak permeability**.
- **Threshold Potential:** **−55 mV**; the "firing point."
- **Peak of Action Potential:** **+30 to +40 mV**; driven by **Na⁺ Nernst potential (+60 mV)**.
- **Hyperpolarization:** dips to **~−80 mV**; caused by slow closure of K⁺ channels.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ mV at 37°C.
- **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation:** $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ — remember **anion (Cl⁻) is flipped** in the fraction.
- **Key Constants:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $F = 96{,}485$ C·mol⁻¹; $T = 310$ K; $\dfrac{RT}{F} \ln(10) \approx 61.5$ mV.
- **Five Phases of AP:** Resting → Depolarization → Repolarization → Hyperpolarization → Return to Rest.
- **Na⁺ channel has TWO gates:** *m-gate* (activation, fast) and *h-gate* (inactivation, slow). At rest: m closed, h open. Depolarized: m open, h open. Inactivated: m open, h closed.
- **K⁺ channel is a *delayed rectifier***: opens slowly, closes slowly — the cause of hyperpolarization.
- **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump:** 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP; **electrogenic** (net +1 charge out per cycle).
- **All-or-Nothing Law:** AP amplitude is independent of stimulus strength; only the **firing frequency** encodes intensity.
- **Absolute Refractory Period:** Na⁺ channels inactivated → no new AP possible, no matter how strong the stimulus.
- **Relative Refractory Period:** Membrane hyperpolarized → only *supra-threshold* stimulus can trigger AP.
- **Cardiac AP difference:** Has a prolonged **plateau phase (Phase 2)** driven by L-type **Ca²⁺ channels** → prevents tetany.
- **KTU Board Conventions:** Always state **which side is negative** when reporting membrane potential. Always include **units (mV)**. Always show the **sign of $z$** in the Nernst equation.
- **Biomedical Engineering Linkages:** Defibrillators, pacemakers, neural stimulators (DBS, cochlear implants), ECG/EMG/EEG amplifier design — all derive their operating principles from these cell-membrane equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ionic Basis of Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ionic Basis of Generation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > The **Ionic Basis of Generation** refers to the biophysical and electrochemical mechanism by which an **excitable cell** (such as a neuron, cardiac myocyte, or skeletal muscle fiber) generates an **Action Potential (AP)** — a rapid, transient, all-or-none change in the transmembrane electrical potential — through the coordinated, voltage-gated, and time-dependent movement of specific ions (primarily **Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, and Cl⁻**) across the lipid bilayer of the cell membrane via specialized **ion channels**, **pumps**, and **exchangers**. The resting cell membrane is a **selectively permeable barrier** maintained at an equilibrium electrochemical gradient. When an external stimulus (electrical, chemical, or mechanical) is applied, the membrane's voltage-gated ion channels open in a specific temporal sequence, causing ionic currents that propagate the signal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Dam with Controlled Floodgates" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy (First-Time Learner Friendly):** > Imagine a **hydroelectric dam** separating two lakes at different elevations: > - The **upper lake** is full of **Na⁺** ions (sodium reservoir, high concentration outside the cell). > - The **lower lake** is full of **K⁺** ions (potassium reservoir, high concentration inside the cell). > - The **dam wall (cell membrane)** has **floodgates (ion channels)** that are normally closed. > - A **watchman (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump)** continuously pumps water back uphill to keep the levels constant. > - When an **earthquake (stimulus)** occurs, the **Na⁺ floodgates burst open** first → water rushes down (Na⁺ rushes in → **depolarization**). > - Shortly after, the **K⁺ floodgates open** → water rushes back up (K⁺ rushes out → **repolarization**). > - The watchman then restores the original lake levels (**resting potential re-established**). This is precisely how every nerve impulse, heartbeat, and muscle contraction begins. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded) | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Faraday's Constant | F | **96,485** | C/mol | | Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | | Body Temperature (Physiological) | T | **310** (≈ 37 °C) | K | | Valence of Monovalent Ion | z | **+1** (Na⁺, K⁺) or **−1** (Cl⁻) | — | | Resting Membrane Potential (Neuron) | Vₘ | **−70** | mV | | Threshold Potential | V_th | **−55** | mV | | Peak Action Potential | V_peak | **+30 to +40** | mV | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Fundamental Ionic States of an Excitable Cell The cell membrane exists in **four distinct electrical states** during the generation and propagation of an action potential: 1. **Resting State (Polarized)** - Membrane potential: **−70 mV** (inside negative relative to outside). - High intracellular K⁺, high extracellular Na⁺. - Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump actively maintains gradients (3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in per ATP). - Voltage-gated channels are **closed**. 2. **Depolarization (Rising Phase of AP)** - Stimulus reaches threshold (**−55 mV**). - Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open rapidly** → Na⁺ influx → membrane potential rises toward **E_Na = +60 mV**. - This is a **positive feedback** mechanism (more depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open). 3. **Repolarization (Falling Phase of AP)** - **Na⁺ channels inactivate** (time-dependent). - **Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open** (delayed rectifier) → K⁺ efflux → membrane potential falls back. - K⁺ efflux continues until approaching **E_K = −94 mV**. 4. **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot / After-Potential)** - K⁺ channels close slowly → brief overshoot toward E_K. - Membrane potential dips to **−80 mV** before stabilizing back to −70 mV. - Corresponds to the **relative refractory period**. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** > Marks are awarded for explaining the **directionality** of ion flow (down the electrochemical gradient) and the **gating kinetics** (m-gate / h-gate for Na⁺; n-gate for K⁺). - **Why does Na⁺ rush in?** Because both the **chemical gradient** (high outside → low inside) AND the **electrical gradient** (inside negative) drive Na⁺ inward. - **Why does K⁺ rush out?** Because although the chemical gradient favors K⁺ efflux, the electrical gradient initially opposes it; however, once the cell is depolarized, K⁺ flows out down its electrochemical gradient. - **Why is the action potential unidirectional?** Due to the **absolute refractory period** caused by Na⁺ channel inactivation. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Nernst Equation (Single Ion Equilibrium Potential)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | E_ion: equilibrium potential (V); [ion]: concentration (mol/L); z: valence | Calculates reversal potential for a single ion species | | **Nernst Equation (Log₁₀ form, 37 °C)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ (mV) | Constant 61 mV at 37 °C | Quick exam calculation | | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | P_ion: membrane permeability | Calculates resting membrane potential considering multiple ions | | **Resting Membrane Potential Approximation** | $V_m \approx -70 \text{ mV}$ | Empirically measured | Standard neuron reference | | **Nernst Potential — K⁺** | $E_K \approx -94 \text{ mV}$ | [K⁺]_out = 4 mM, [K⁺]_in = 140 mM | Drives K⁺ efflux | | **Nernst Potential — Na⁺** | $E_{Na} \approx +60 \text{ mV}$ | [Na⁺]_out = 145 mM, [Na⁺]_in = 12 mM | Drives Na⁺ influx | | **Nernst Potential — Cl⁻** | $E_{Cl} \approx -65 \text{ mV}$ | [Cl⁻]_out = 120 mM, [Cl⁻]_in = 4 mM | Inhibitory ion | | **Nernst Potential — Ca²⁺** | $E_{Ca} \approx +150 \text{ mV}$ | z = +2 | Critical in cardiac & smooth muscle | | **Conductance Ratio at Rest** | $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} \approx 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ | Empirical ratio | Explains why V_m ≈ E_K at rest | | **Threshold Condition** | $V_m \leq -55 \text{ mV}$ | Critical firing level | All-or-none AP initiation | | **Membrane Time Constant** | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | R_m: membrane resistance; C_m: capacitance | Determines AP rise time | | **Cable Equation (1-D)** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = R_i \cdot C_m \cdot \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t}$ | R_i: axial (intracellular) resistance | Describes AP propagation | | **Space Constant (Length Constant)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_m}{R_i}}$ | Measure of passive spread | Determines passive decay | | **Sodium-Potassium Pump Ratio** | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed | Stoichiometry | Maintains ion gradients | | **AP Propagation Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L C_m R_i}}$ | L: internodal length | Saltatory conduction | | **AP Propagation Velocity (Unmyelinated)** | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{4 R_i C_m R_m}}$ | d: axon diameter | Continuous conduction | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industry & Research Relevance:** > - **ECG / EEG / EMG Signal Acquisition:** Understanding ionic basis allows design of **bio-amplifiers** with proper bandwidth (0.05 Hz – 1500 Hz) and **input impedance > 10 MΩ**. > - **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Electrode-tissue interface impedance is modeled using **Randles equivalent circuits** derived from membrane biophysics. > - **Defibrillators & Pacemakers:** Deliver controlled current pulses to **reset cardiac action potentials** (≈ 200–360 J biphasic). > - **Pharmacology & Drug Design:** **Channelopathies** (e.g., long QT syndrome) and **ion channel blockers** (lidocaine, tetrodotoxin) are engineered based on Nernst/GHK principles. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (KTU 14-Mark Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Board Requirement:** > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that students derive the Nernst equation from **electrochemical potential equilibrium**, not merely state it. #### Step 1 — Define the Two Driving Forces The total force acting on an ion across the membrane has **two components**: **(a) Chemical Force (Concentration Gradient)** Driven by the difference in **chemical potential** μ (Gibbs free energy per mole): $$\mu_{chem} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[C]$$ where: - $\mu^0$ = standard chemical potential - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $[C]$ = molar concentration **(b) Electrical Force (Membrane Potential)** The electrical work required to move a mole of ions with charge $zF$ across a potential difference $V_m$: $$\mu_{elec} = zFV_m$$ where: - $z$ = valence of the ion - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - $V_m$ = membrane potential (Volts) #### Step 2 — Write the Total Electrochemical Potential The total electrochemical potential difference between **outside (o)** and **inside (i)** is: $$\Delta \mu = (RT \ln[C]_i + zF V_i) - (RT \ln[C]_o + zF V_o)$$ #### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, **no net ionic flux** occurs → $\Delta \mu = 0$: $$RT \ln[C]_i + zF V_i = RT \ln[C]_o + zF V_o$$ #### Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate the Potential $$zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln[C]_o - RT \ln[C]_i$$ $$zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$ #### Step 5 — Define the Nernst Potential By convention, the **Nernst potential** $E_{ion}$ is defined as $V_i - V_o$ (inside relative to outside): $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)}$$ #### Step 6 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm (Exam-Friendly Form) Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$ #### Step 7 — Evaluate the Constant at 37 °C (T = 310 K) $$\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ Substituting and converting to millivolts: $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right) \text{ mV}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Sample Numerical Application — Nernst Potential for K⁺ > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Exam Problem Pattern:** **Given:** - Intracellular K⁺ concentration: $[K^+]_i = 140 \text{ mM}$ - Extracellular K⁺ concentration: $[K^+]_o = 4 \text{ mM}$ - Temperature: 37 °C (use the **61/z** shortcut) - Valence: $z = +1$ **Solution (Substitute Directly):** $$E_K = \frac{61}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_K = 61 \times \log_{10}(0.02857) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_K = 61 \times (-1.5441) \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{E_K \approx -94.2 \text{ mV}}$$ **Interpretation:** At equilibrium, the K⁺ ion's electrochemical potential is balanced when the membrane is at −94 mV (inside negative). This is the reversal potential for K⁺. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Application — Nernst Potential for Na⁺ **Given:** - $[Na^+]_i = 12 \text{ mM}$, $[Na^+]_o = 145 \text{ mM}$, $z = +1$ **Solution:** $$E_{Na} = \frac{61}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{Na} = 61 \times \log_{10}(12.083) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{Na} = 61 \times (1.0821) \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +66.0 \text{ mV}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** $E_{Na}$ is positive because Na⁺ is more concentrated outside. The cell's AP peak (+30 to +40 mV) approaches but does not reach E_Na because Na⁺ channels inactivate before equilibrium is fully established. --- ### 3.4 Derivation Sketch — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation The GHK equation extends the Nernst equation to account for **multiple ion species** with **differential permeabilities** at the resting state. Starting from constant field theory (Goldman, 1943), the membrane potential is: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$ **Standard Values (Squid Axon):** - $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ **Substitution yields $V_m \approx -70 \text{ mV}$**, matching experimental measurement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the GHK Matters:** > The **permeability ratios** change dramatically during an action potential: > - At **rest**: $P_K \gg P_{Na}$ → $V_m$ close to $E_K$ > - During **depolarization**: $P_{Na}$ increases **600-fold** → $V_m$ shifts toward $E_{Na}$ > - During **repolarization**: $P_{Na}$ falls, $P_K$ rises → $V_m$ returns to $E_K$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hodgkin-Huxley Model Summary (Board Favorite) | Variable | Gating Variable | Represents | Range | Rate Constant at V = 0 mV | |---|---|---|---|---| | $m$ | Na⁺ activation | Opens Na⁺ channel | 0 → 1 | $\alpha_m = 1.0 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ | | $h$ | Na⁺ inactivation | Closes Na⁺ channel (m-gate) | 0 → 1 | $\alpha_h = 0.07 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ | | $n$ | K⁺ activation | Opens K⁺ channel | 0 → 1 | $\alpha_n = 0.01 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ | **Membrane Current Equation (Hodgkin-Huxley, 1952):** $$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Note:** The 1952 Hodgkin-Huxley model won the **Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine (1963)**. You may be asked to draw the equivalent circuit or explain gating variable kinetics. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Phases of the Action Potential — Voltage-Time Trace ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["RESTING STATE
Vm = -70 mV
Na and K channels closed"] nodeB["DEPOLARIZATION
Stimulus reaches -55 mV
Na channels OPEN rapidly
Na ions RUSH IN"] nodeC["PEAK / OVERSHOOT
Vm = +30 to +40 mV
Na channels INACTIVATE
K channels begin to OPEN"] nodeD["REPOLARIZATION
Vm falls
K ions RUSH OUT
Na channels RESET"] nodeE["HYPERPOLARIZATION
Vm = -80 mV
K channels close slowly
Undershoot phase"] nodeF["RETURN TO REST
Na/K ATPase pump
restores ion gradients
Vm = -70 mV"] nodeA -->|"Stimulus > threshold"| nodeB nodeB -->|"Rapid Na influx"| nodeC nodeC -->|"Na inactivation + K efflux"| nodeD nodeD -->|"K channels close slowly"| nodeE nodeE -->|"Pump action"| nodeF nodeF -.->|"Ready for next AP"| nodeA ``` ### 4.2 Ion Channel Gating Kinetics — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR node1["RESTING
m-gate: CLOSED
h-gate: OPEN
n-gate: CLOSED"] -->|"Depolarization
m activates fast"| node2["ACTIVATED Na+ CHANNEL
m: OPEN
h: OPEN
Ion flow: Na+ IN"] node2 -->|"h-gate closes
(inactivation)"| node3["INACTIVATED Na+
m: OPEN
h: CLOSED
No ion flow"] node3 -->|"Repolarization
h resets"| node4["RESTING
m: CLOSED
h: OPEN
Ready again"] node5["K+ CHANNEL
n-gate: 4 independent
activation particles"] -.->|"Delayed opening"| node2 ``` ### 4.3 Equivalent Electrical Circuit — Hodgkin-Huxley Model ```mermaid graph LR nodeCap["Cm
Membrane Capacitance
~1 uF/cm2"] nodeNa["gNa (V,t)
Voltage-gated Na+ conductance
ENa = +60 mV"] nodeK["gK (V,t)
Voltage-gated K+ conductance
EK = -94 mV"] nodeL["gL
Leak conductance
EL = -49 mV"] nodeStim["Im
Applied stimulus current"] nodeStim --- nodeCap nodeCap --- nodeNa nodeCap --- nodeK nodeCap --- nodeL nodeNa --- nodeK nodeK --- nodeL ``` ### 4.4 Ionic Flow Summary Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD subgraph OUTSIDE["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE"] ionNaOut["Na+ HIGH (145 mM)"] ionClOut["Cl- HIGH (120 mM)"] end subgraph MEMBRANE["LIPID BILAYER MEMBRANE"] chanNa["VOLTAGE-GATED Na+ CHANNEL
(m-gate + h-gate)"] chanK["VOLTAGE-GATED K+ CHANNEL
(4 x n-gates)"] pump["Na+/K+ ATPase PUMP
3 Na+ OUT : 2 K+ IN"] chanLeak["LEAK CHANNELS (K+, Cl-)"] end subgraph INSIDE["INTRACELLULAR SPACE"] ionKIn["K+ HIGH (140 mM)"] ionPrIn["Anions (A-) HIGH
Proteins, Phosphates"] end ionNaOut -->|"Rush IN during depolarization"| chanNa chanNa --> ionPrIn ionKIn -->|"Rush OUT during repolarization"| chanK chanK --> ionNaOut ionNaOut -->|"Active transport (ATP)"| pump pump --> ionKIn ionKIn -->|"Slow leak"| chanLeak chanLeak --> ionNaOut ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. Define resting membrane potential. Mention its typical value in a mammalian neuron and explain the ionic basis for its existence.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** The resting membrane potential (RMP) is the **steady-state electrical potential difference** (~ **−70 mV**, inside negative) existing across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is **not firing an action potential**. It is established and maintained by: 1. The **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in, ATP-driven) which creates ionic gradients. 2. The **higher permeability of the membrane to K⁺** than to Na⁺ at rest (P_K : P_Na ≈ 1 : 0.04), causing K⁺ to leak out down its concentration gradient, leaving the inside negative. 3. The presence of **impermeant intracellular anions** (proteins, phosphates) that cannot leave the cell. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. Distinguish between depolarization and repolarization phases of an action potential.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Depolarization | Repolarization | |---|---|---| | **Ion involved** | Na⁺ | K⁺ | | **Direction of flow** | Into the cell | Out of the cell | | **Channel** | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channel (m-gate opens) | Voltage-gated K⁺ channel (n-gate opens, delayed) | | **Membrane potential change** | Rises from −70 mV to +30 mV | Falls from +30 mV back to −70 mV | | **Mechanism** | Positive feedback (more depolarization → more Na⁺ influx) | K⁺ efflux down electrochemical gradient | | **Driving force** | High [Na⁺] outside + negative interior | High [K⁺] inside + now-positive interior | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern (Per KTU 2024 Scheme)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q(a). Derive the Nernst equation from fundamental thermodynamic principles. Using the derived equation, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given [K⁺]ᵢ = 140 mM and [K⁺]₀ = 4 mM.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the principles:** The Nernst equation is derived from the equality of **electrochemical potential** for an ion at equilibrium across a permeable membrane. **Step 2 — Write chemical potential:** $$\mu_{chem} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[C]$$ **Step 3 — Add electrical work term:** $$\mu_{total} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[C] + zFV$$ **Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition** (Δμ = 0 between inside and outside): $$RT \ln[C]_i + zFV_i = RT \ln[C]_o + zFV_o$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange:** $$zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for potential difference**: $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Convert to base-10 logarithm**: $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \times RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$ **Step 8 — Calculate for K⁺** (z = +1, T = 310 K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol): $$E_K = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$ $$E_K = 0.0615 \times \log_{10}(0.02857)$$ $$E_K = 0.0615 \times (-1.544)$$ $$\boxed{E_K = -0.0949 \text{ V} = -94.9 \text{ mV}}$$ --- **Q(b). With a neat block diagram, explain the Hodgkin-Huxley model of action potential generation. List the four gating variables and state their physical meaning.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Hodgkin-Huxley Model (1952)** — describes the ionic basis of action potential generation using four voltage-clamp-derived differential equations. **Block Diagram Representation:** ``` Stimulus (Im) → [Cm dV/dt] → Membrane potential Vm(t) ↓ ┌────────────┬─────────────┬──────────────┐ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ g_Na·m³h g_K·n⁴ g_L (Vm - E_ion) (E_Na) (E_K) (E_L) ↓ ↓ ↓ Na+ current K+ current Leak current ``` **The Four Gating Variables:** | Variable | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Activation Range | |---|---|---|---| | Sodium activation | $m$ | m-gate that **opens** Na⁺ channel during depolarization | 0 (closed) → 1 (open) | | Sodium inactivation | $h$ | h-gate that **closes/inactivates** Na⁺ channel after opening | 0 (inactive) → 1 (available) | | Potassium activation | $n$ | n-gate that **opens** K⁺ channel (delayed) | 0 (closed) → 1 (open) | **The Combined Current Equation:** $$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$$ **Significance:** - Hodgkin and Huxley won the **1963 Nobel Prize** for this work. - The model predicts the **shape, threshold, and propagation** of the action potential with remarkable accuracy. - Forms the foundation of **modern neural simulators** (NEURON, GENESIS). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] **Q(a). Explain the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation. How is it superior to the simple Nernst equation in describing the resting membrane potential? Calculate the resting potential assuming $P_K = 1$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Limitations of Nernst Equation:** The Nernst equation considers **only one ion species** and assumes the membrane is **impermeable to all other ions**. This is unrealistic because the cell membrane is simultaneously permeable to K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻ (in varying degrees). **The GHK Equation (Constant Field Theory):** $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$ **Substituting Standard Values:** - $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM - $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM - $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM - $P_K = 1$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$ **Numerator:** $$1(4) + 0.04(145) + 0.45(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$ **Denominator:** $$1(140) + 0.04(12) + 0.45(120) = 140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48$$ **Calculation:** $$V_m = 61 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{11.6}{194.48}\right) \text{ mV}$$ $$V_m = 61 \times \log_{10}(0.0596) \text{ mV}$$ $$V_m = 61 \times (-1.2246) \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{V_m \approx -74.7 \text{ mV}}$$ **This closely matches the experimentally observed value of −70 mV**, confirming the validity of the GHK equation. **Advantages of GHK over Nernst:** 1. Considers **multiple ion species** simultaneously. 2. Accounts for **differential permeabilities** (P_ion). 3. Explains why V_m is **not equal to E_K** (which would be −94 mV). 4. Predicts **permeability changes during the action potential** (e.g., P_Na increases 600-fold). --- **Q(b). Describe the four phases of the action potential in a neuronal axon. For each phase, identify the dominant ion current, the state of the voltage-gated channels, and the corresponding membrane potential range.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** | Phase | V_m Range | Dominant Current | Na⁺ Channel State | K⁺ Channel State | Key Event | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **1. Resting (Polarized)** | −70 mV | Small K⁺ leak | Closed (m, h available) | Closed (n available) | Na⁺/K⁺ pump maintains gradients | | **2. Depolarization (Rising)** | −70 → +30 mV | **Na⁺ influx** (inward) | **m-gate OPENS** rapidly | Still closed | Positive feedback; V approaches E_Na | | **3. Repolarization (Falling)** | +30 → −70 mV | **K⁺ efflux** (outward) | m open, **h-gate INACTIVATES** | **n-gate OPENS** (delayed) | V approaches E_K | | **4. Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** | −70 → −80 → −70 mV | Continued K⁺ efflux | h-gate resets | **n-gate slow to close** | Refractory period; pump restores gradients | **Additional Critical Concepts:** - **Threshold:** A stimulus must depolarize the membrane to **−55 mV** to open enough Na⁺ channels to trigger the regenerative all-or-none response. - **Absolute Refractory Period:** During depolarization and most of repolarization, Na⁺ channels are inactivated — no new AP can be generated, ensuring **unidirectional propagation**. - **Relative Refractory Period:** During hyperpolarization, a **stronger-than-normal stimulus** is required to reach threshold. - **All-or-None Law:** Sub-threshold stimuli produce graded local potentials; supra-threshold stimuli produce full-amplitude APs of constant magnitude. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Errors (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert 2.303·RT/F to **61.5 mV** or leaving answer in Volts instead of mV. Always state the **constant used** explicitly. > 2. **Sign Convention (–1 mark):** Confusing $[C]_o/[C]_i$ with $[C]_i/[C]_o$ in the Nernst equation. The **logarithm argument is ALWAYS (outside)/(inside)**. > 3. **Missing "Inside-relative-to-Outside" Convention (–1 mark):** The membrane potential is conventionally expressed as V_inside − V_outside. State this explicitly. > 4. **Skipping the "Why" (–1 mark):** The KTU 2024 scheme follows **Bloom's Taxonomy L4 (Analyze)**. Merely writing the equation is insufficient — explain **why** each ion flows in its specific direction (chemical + electrical gradient). > 5. **Forgetting Permeability in GHK (–1 mark):** Students often substitute concentrations but forget the **P_ion weights**. The GHK equation is **NOT a simple average** of Nernst potentials. > 6. **Hodgkin-Huxley Notation (–1 mark):** Use **m, h, n** — NOT **a, b, c** or generic Greek letters. Examiners are strict on textbook notation. > 7. **No Block Diagram (–2 marks):** A question asking "with neat block diagram" will **deduct 2 marks** if only text is provided. Always include a labeled circuit/block diagram. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 📋 **KTU 2024 — High-Density Last-Minute Revision List** - [ ] **Resting Membrane Potential** = **−70 mV** (neuron); maintained by **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase** (3:2 stoichiometry). - [ ] **Threshold** = **−55 mV**; sub-threshold = graded potential; supra-threshold = **all-or-none AP**. - [ ] **Nernst Equation:** $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$ mV at 37 °C. - [ ] **E_K ≈ −94 mV**, **E_Na ≈ +60 mV**, **E_Cl ≈ −65 mV**, **E_Ca ≈ +150 mV**. - [ ] **GHK Equation:** Includes **permeability ratios** (P_K : P_Na : P_Cl = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45 at rest). - [ ] **Phase 1 (Rest):** V_m = −70 mV, channels closed, pump active. - [ ] **Phase 2 (Depolarization):** Na⁺ channels open (m-gate), Na⁺ rushes in, V_m → +30 mV. - [ ] **Phase 3 (Repolarization):** Na⁺ channels inactivate (h-gate), K⁺ channels open (n-gate), K⁺ rushes out. - [ ] **Phase 4 (Hyperpolarization):** K⁺ channels close slowly → V_m dips to −80 mV. - [ ] **Refractory Periods:** Absolute (Na⁺ inactivated) → no AP; Relative (hyperpolarized) → requires stronger stimulus. - [ ] **Hodgkin-Huxley Model (1952):** Uses **m³h** (Na⁺ gating) and **n⁴** (K⁺ gating); 1963 Nobel Prize. - [ ] **Cable Equation:** Describes AP propagation along axon; **space constant λ** governs passive spread. - [ ] **Saltatory Conduction:** In myelinated axons, AP "jumps" between **Nodes of Ranvier** → much faster velocity. - [ ] **Engineering Applications:** ECG/EEG amplifier design, defibrillators, neural prosthetics, ion-channel drug design. - [ ] **Constants to Memorize:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, T = 310 K (body), 2.303·RT/F = 61.5 mV. - [ ] **Sign Convention:** V_m = V_inside − V_outside (negative at rest). - [ ] **Direction of Flow:** Na⁺ in (during AP upstroke), K⁺ out (during AP downstroke and undershoot), Ca²⁺ in (at presynaptic terminal for neurotransmitter release).

topics.title: Nernst Potential, Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation and Auto Rhythmic Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nernst Potential, Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation and Auto Rhythmic Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nernst Potential — Formal Definition

The **Nernst Potential** (also called the *reversal potential* or *equilibrium potential*) of a specific ion is the precise value of the transmembrane voltage at which there is **no net flow** of that ion across the cell membrane, even though a concentration gradient exists. At this voltage, the electrical force pulling the ion in one direction is exactly counterbalanced by the chemical (concentration) force pushing it in the opposite direction.

Mathematically, for a monovalent ion at body temperature (**T = 310 K**, i.e., **37°C**), the Nernst equation reduces to:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}\right) \text{ mV}$$

where **z** is the ionic valence and **[ion]** denotes molar concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Nernst potential is the *fundamental* equation upon which the entire bioelectric circuit theory rests. Every action potential, ECG waveform, and nerve impulse can be traced back to this single equilibrium concept.

## 1.2 Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation — Formal Definition

The **GHK Equation** is a generalization of the Nernst equation for a biological membrane that is simultaneously permeable to **multiple ion species** (typically Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻). Unlike the Nernst equation which considers only one ion, the GHK equation weights each ion's contribution by its *membrane permeability*, producing the **resting membrane potential** of a real cell.

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

At **37°C**, the prefactor becomes approximately **61 mV**.

## 1.3 Autorhythmic Cells — Formal Definition

**Autorhythmic (auto-rhythmic) cells** are specialized excitable cells that **spontaneously generate rhythmic action potentials** without any external neural or hormonal input. They act as the body's internal biological pacemakers. The most clinically significant examples are found in the **SA node** and **AV node** of the heart, controlling the cardiac cycle.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

### Analogy 1 — Nernst Potential: The "Water Dam" Analogy

Imagine a U-shaped tank divided by a semi-permeable membrane. Salt water is on one side, fresh water on the other. Water naturally wants to flow from the fresh side to the salt side (chemical gradient). If you now apply a pump creating a pressure difference across the membrane, water can be pushed back. The **Nernst potential is the exact pressure at which the pump's push exactly cancels the chemical pull** — the net flow becomes zero. The membrane is "happily balanced."

### Analogy 2 — GHK Equation: The "Three-Lane Highway Toll Booth"

Picture a toll plaza with three lanes. Lane 1 (K⁺) is open 80% of the time, Lane 2 (Na⁺) 15%, and Lane 3 (Cl⁻) 5%. The toll collected (voltage across the membrane) depends not just on the price of each car, but on **how often each lane is open** (permeability). The GHK equation is the *weighted average toll* of all three lanes combined.

### Analogy 3 — Autorhythmic Cells: The "Self-Winding Watch"

Whereas a regular neuron is like a clock that needs to be wound by an external stimulus, an autorhythmic cell is like a **self-winding mechanical watch** — it has an internal mechanism (a slow inward "funny" current) that automatically re-triggers itself, producing an action potential at a fixed interval. This is why your heart keeps beating even when removed from the body (given proper perfusion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **R (Universal Gas Constant) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **F (Faraday's Constant) = 96,485 C/mol**
> - **T (Body Temperature) = 310 K**
> - **RT/F at 37°C ≈ 26.73 mV**
> - **2.303·RT/F at 37°C ≈ 61.5 mV**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst potential arises from the **electrochemical equilibrium** of a single ionic species across a selectively permeable membrane. The driving forces are:

- **Chemical Force (Concentration Gradient):** Ions diffuse from high to low concentration. This contributes an energy proportional to $\ln([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$.
- **Electrical Force (Membrane Potential):** Ions are pushed by the electric field according to their charge $z$.

At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** on both sides is equal. This equality, when solved for voltage, yields the Nernst equation.

### Boundary & Limiting Conditions:
- If $[ion]_{out} = [ion]_{in}$ → $E_{ion} = 0$ mV (no gradient, no equilibrium voltage needed).
- For a doubly-charged ion ($z = 2$), the Nernst voltage is **halved** (e.g., Ca²⁺ at 61/2 = 30.5 mV per decade).
- A **10-fold** concentration ratio produces **61/z mV** at body temperature.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz Equation

A real biological membrane is never permeable to just one ion. The GHK equation models this realistically:

- **Permeability (P)** acts as a "weight" for each ion. The ion with the highest permeability dominates the resting potential.
- At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na} > P_{Cl}$, so the resting potential is close to $E_K$ (≈ −90 mV) but slightly depolarized by Na⁺ leak.
- During an action potential, Na⁺ permeability surges transiently, so $V_m$ swings toward $E_{Na}$ (≈ +60 mV).

### Why GHK Fails for Action Potentials:
The GHK equation assumes **constant permeabilities**. During an action potential, permeabilities change in milliseconds. Therefore, GHK accurately predicts **resting potential** but not the dynamic waveform.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of Autorhythmic Cells

Unlike contractile cardiac myocytes, pacemaker cells lack a stable resting potential. Their membrane voltage **slowly drifts upward** (Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization) due to:

- **"Funny" Current ($I_f$):** An inward Na⁺ current activated by hyperpolarization, carried primarily by Na⁺ through HCN channels.
- **T-type Ca²⁺ Channels:** Open near the end of Phase 4, contributing additional depolarization.
- **Decreasing K⁺ Efflux:** K⁺ channels gradually close during Phase 4.

When the threshold (~ −40 mV) is reached, **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** open (Phase 0), producing the upstroke of the pacemaker action potential — *unlike neurons, where Na⁺ drives the upstroke*. Repolarization (Phase 3) occurs via K⁺ efflux as Ca²⁺ channels inactivate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Constants | Use Case |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (general) | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Volts; R=8.314, F=96485 | Equilibrium of single ion |
| 2 | Nernst (at 37°C, monovalent) | $E = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV | KTU board problems |
| 3 | Nernst for Ca²⁺ (z=2) | $E_{Ca} = 30.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Ca]_{out}}{[Ca]_{in}}\right)$ | mV | Cardiac/synaptic potentials |
| 4 | GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | mV | Resting membrane potential |
| 5 | Simplified GHK (no Cl) | $V_m = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in}}\right)$ | mV | Neuronal/nerve cells |
| 6 | Slope factor | $2.303 \cdot \frac{RT}{F}$ | **61.5 mV** at 37°C | Convert ln to log₁₀ |
| 7 | Body Temperature | $T = 273 + 37 = 310$ K | Kelvin | Standard for bioelectricity |
| 8 | Pacemaker Phase 4 slope | $\frac{dV}{dt} \approx 0.1$ V/s | mV/ms | SA node firing rate |
| 9 | SA node intrinsic rate | ~ **100 bpm** (depressed to 70 bpm by vagal tone) | beats/min | Cardiac physiology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE:** Always use the **natural logarithm (ln)** with the form $RT/zF$, and the **base-10 logarithm (log₁₀)** with the constant **61 mV** at 37°C. Confusing these is the #1 error in KTU board papers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG / EEG Machine Design:** The leads and amplifiers of an ECG machine measure the *field potential* generated by thousands of cells whose individual potentials are governed by Nernst/GHK equations. ECG waveform morphology is a direct macro-scale expression of these equations.
- **Implantable Pacemakers:** Modern pacemakers replicate the autorhythmic function of the SA node using electronic pulse generators. Engineers designing these must understand the *natural* pacemaker waveforms (Ca²⁺-driven upstroke) to mimic them faithfully.
- **Biosensors & Ion-Selective Electrodes:** pH meters and ion-selective electrodes operate on the exact same Nernst principle (a 59 mV/pH change at 25°C).
- **Drug Action Mechanism:** Many neuropharmacological drugs (e.g., local anesthetics, antiarrhythmics) work by altering $P_{Na}$ or $P_K$, shifting the GHK voltage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation from First Principles

### Step 1: Define the Two Driving Forces

Consider a membrane separating two aqueous compartments. An ion $X^{z}$ (charge $z$) has concentration $[X]_{in}$ inside the cell and $[X]_{out}$ outside.

**Chemical (Diffusion) Force:** The free energy change for moving one mole of ion from inside to outside is:

$$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Electrical Force:** The work done to move one mole of charge $zF$ across a potential difference $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$ is:

$$\Delta G_{elec} = zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = zF V_m$$

### Step 2: Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, the **net electrochemical free energy change** for the ion transfer is zero:

$$\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Solve

$$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) + zF V_m = 0$$

$$zF V_m = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

$$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Invert the Argument Convention (KTU Standard Form)

Most physiology textbooks express the Nernst potential as $E_{ion}$ with outside on top of the log. This requires flipping the sign. Multiplying numerator and denominator by $1/[X]_{out}$:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{1/[X]_{out}}{1/[X]_{in}}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}\right)$$

Some sources write it with the outside on top, which is equivalent to a sign convention. The KTU-accepted form is:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

or equivalently

$$E_{ion} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

### Step 5: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

### Step 6: Plug in Body Temperature (T = 310 K)

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot (8.314) \cdot 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

Therefore, for a **monovalent ion (z = 1)** at **37°C**:

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) \text{ mV}}$$

### Step 7: Numerical Example (K⁺ in a Typical Neuron)

- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM

$$E_K = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{140}{5}\right) = 61 \log_{10}(28)$$

$$= 61 \times 1.4472 = -88.28 \text{ mV}$$

(Negative because we inverted the ratio, consistent with inside-negative convention.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation

### Step 1: Constant Field Assumption

GHK assumes the electric field **E** within the membrane is **constant** (linear voltage drop across the membrane thickness). Starting from the Nernst–Planck flux equation for an ion under a constant field:

$$J_i = -D_i \left(\frac{dC_i}{dx} + \frac{z_i F C_i E}{RT}\right)$$

where $J_i$ is the flux of ion $i$, $D_i$ is the diffusion coefficient, and $C_i(x)$ is the local concentration.

### Step 2: Solve the Differential Equation

The solution, with boundary conditions $C_i(0) = [i]_{in}$ and $C_i(\delta) = [i]_{out}$ (membrane thickness $\delta$), is:

$$J_i = P_i \cdot z_i F \cdot \frac{[i]_{in} - [i]_{out} \cdot e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}$$

where $P_i = D_i / \delta$ is the **permeability**.

### Step 3: Apply the Electroneutrality Condition

At steady state, the **net current across the membrane is zero** (no charge accumulation). Therefore:

$$I_{net} = \sum_i I_i = \sum_i z_i F J_i = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_m$

Setting the sum of currents to zero and solving algebraically for $V_m$ yields the GHK voltage equation:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

The chloride ion appears inverted (inside on top, outside on bottom) because it is a **negative ion** — its flux equation has the opposite sign convention.

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Typical squid axon at rest:
- $[K^+]_{in} = 400$, $[K^+]_{out} = 20$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 50$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 440$ mM
- $[Cl^-]_{in} = 40$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 560$ mM
- Permeability ratios: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

Plugging in (using log₁₀ form):

$$V_m = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1(20) + 0.04(440) + 0.45(40)}{1(400) + 0.04(50) + 0.45(560)}\right)$$

$$= 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20 + 17.6 + 18}{400 + 2 + 252}\right) = 61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{55.6}{654}\right)$$

$$= 61 \log_{10}(0.0850) = 61 \times (-1.0706) \approx -65.3 \text{ mV}$$

This matches the experimentally measured squid axon resting potential (~ −70 mV). ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Concepts

| Feature | Nernst Equation | GHK Equation | Autorhythmic Cells |
|---------|-----------------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Ions considered** | Single ion | Multiple ions (typically 3) | All ions via dynamic currents |
| **Permeability** | Implicit (100% for that ion) | Weighted explicitly by $P_i$ | Time-varying (channels gate) |
| **Output** | Reversal potential (fixed) | Steady-state membrane potential | Oscillating action potentials |
| **Use case** | Single channel analysis | Resting potential of real cell | Pacemaker function (SA/AV node) |
| **Limitation** | Ignores all other ions | Assumes constant $P_i$ | Cannot be expressed in closed form |
| **Body temperature form** | 61/z × log₁₀ ratio | 61 × log₁₀ weighted ratio | Voltage vs. time curve |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Components Influencing the Membrane Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intracellular Space] --> B[Cell Membrane]
    C[Extracellular Space] --> B
    B --> D[Selective Permeability]
    D --> E[Na+ Channel]
    D --> F[K+ Channel]
    D --> G[Cl- Channel]
    D --> H[Ca2+ Channel]
    E --> I[Nernst Equilibrium for Na+]
    F --> J[Nernst Equilibrium for K+]
    G --> K[Nernst Equilibrium for Cl-]
    H --> L[Nernst Equilibrium for Ca2+]
    I --> M[GHK Summation]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Resting Membrane Potential Vm]
    M --> O[Permeability Weights PK, PNa, PCl, PCa]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Workflow of Pacemaker (SA Node) Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Maximum Diastolic Potential -60 mV] --> B[Phase 4 Spontaneous Depolarization]
    B --> B1[Funny Current If Na influx]
    B --> B2[T-type Ca2+ channels open]
    B1 --> C[Threshold Reached -40 mV]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Phase 0 Depolarization]
    D --> D1[L-type Ca2+ channels open]
    D1 --> E[Peak Voltage around 0 mV]
    E --> F[Phase 3 Repolarization]
    F --> F1[K+ efflux increases]
    F1 --> G[Return to Max Diastolic Potential]
    G --> B
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standard Nernst Potentials in Cardiac Cell] --> B[EK = -94 mV]
    A --> C[ENa = +60 mV]
    A --> D[ECa = +132 mV]
    A --> E[ECl = -70 mV]
    B --> F[GHK Weighted Average]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

```mermaid
    A1 --> A2[Needs Stimulus to Reach Threshold]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 0: Fast Na+ Driven]

    B3 --> C
```
