# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RTD Resistance-Temperature Relationship

The metallic resistance of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) varies **linearly** with temperature in a first-order approximation. The full derivation from thermodynamics is not required, but the Taylor expansion logic is:

Starting with the definition of fractional change in resistance with temperature:

$$
R_T = R_0 \, f(T)
$$

Expand $f(T)$ as a Taylor series about reference temperature $T_0$:

$$
f(T) = f(T_0) + f'(T_0)(T - T_0) + \frac{1}{2!} f''(T_0)(T - T_0)^2 + \cdots
$$

Setting $f(T_0) = 1$ and defining $\alpha = f'(T_0)$ (temperature coefficient), and neglecting higher-order terms for first-order approximation:

$$
R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \, (T - T_0) \right]
$$

**Step-by-step meaning of each term:**

- $R_0$ : The known reference resistance at the calibration temperature $T_0$ (usually $0\,°\text{C}$ or $25\,°\text{C}$).
- $\alpha$ : A material property; for platinum, $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,\, /°\text{C}$.
- $(T - T_0)$ : The temperature deviation from the reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Practice (KTU Style):** A platinum RTD has $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at $0\,°\text{C}$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/°\text{C}$. Find $R$ at $T = 100\,°\text{C}$.

$$
R_{100} = 100 \, [1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(100 - 0)]
$$

$$
R_{100} = 100 \, [1 + 0.385] = 100 \times 1.385 = 138.5\,\Omega
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain Gauge Gauge Factor

A strain gauge works on the principle that **resistance depends on both length and cross-sectional area**, and these change when the gauge is stretched/compressed.

Resistance of a conductor:
$$
R = \rho \frac{L}{A}
$$

Take natural logarithms of both sides:
$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

Differentiate (small changes):
$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

For a uniform wire under axial strain, the cross-sectional area change is related to longitudinal strain via Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \, \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon = dL/L$ is the longitudinal strain.

Substitute:
$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \epsilon + 2\nu \epsilon
$$

Define Gauge Factor $G$:
$$
G = \frac{dR / R}{\epsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho / \rho}{\epsilon}
$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

- $1$ : Pure geometric contribution from length change.
- $2\nu$ : Geometric contribution from area change (Poisson effect).
- $\frac{d\rho/\rho}{\epsilon}$ : **Piezoresistive** contribution (change in resistivity due to strain — large in semiconductors, near zero in metals).

For a metallic gauge (e.g., constantan), $\frac{d\rho}{\rho}/\epsilon \approx 0$, so:
$$
G \approx 1 + 2\nu
$$

With $\nu \approx 0.3$ for constantan, $G \approx 1.6$ to $2.0$.

For a **semiconductor gauge**, the piezoresistive term dominates, giving $G \approx 100$ to $200$ — much more sensitive.

> **Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**
> A strain gauge with $G = 2.1$ and $R = 120\,\Omega$ is bonded to a steel beam. Find the change in resistance for a strain of $\epsilon = 1000\,\mu\epsilon$ (i.e., $10^{-3}$).

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 120 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.252\,\Omega
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitive Transducer Displacement Sensitivity

For a parallel-plate capacitor with variable plate separation $d$:
$$
C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $d$ to find sensitivity:
$$
\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C}{d}
$$

So a small displacement $\Delta d$ produces a capacitance change:
$$
\Delta C \approx -\frac{C}{d} \cdot \Delta d
$$

**Normalized sensitivity:**
$$
\frac{\Delta C / C}{\Delta d / d} = -1
$$

This shows the capacitive transducer has a **linear** fractional response when displacement is normalized by the gap. Used in micrometer-precision displacement sensors.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Photoplethysmography (PPG) by Beer-Lambert Law

Optical absorption in tissue is governed by the Beer-Lambert law:
$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\epsilon(\lambda) \, c \, l}
$$

- $I$ : Transmitted light intensity.
- $I_0$ : Incident intensity.
- $\epsilon(\lambda)$ : Wavelength-dependent absorption coefficient.
- $c$ : Concentration of absorber (Hb / HbO₂).
- $l$ : Path length through tissue (changes with blood volume pulse).

For a pulse oximeter, two wavelengths ($\lambda_1 = 660$ nm red, $\lambda_2 = 940$ nm infrared) are used. The ratio of AC to DC components of the PPG signal gives SpO₂:
$$
R_{os} = \frac{AC_{660} / DC_{660}}{AC_{940} / DC_{940}}
$$

This ratio is empirically calibrated to oxygen saturation percentage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Differential Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize This Table for "Differentiate" Questions in ESE (3 marks minimum):**

| **Feature** | **Active Transducer** | **Passive Transducer** |
|---|---|---|
| External power | **Not needed** | **Required** |
| Operating principle | Energy conversion | Energy modulation |
| Output form | EMF / Current | Resistance / Inductance / Capacitance change |
| Examples | Piezoelectric, Thermocouple, Photovoltaic | Thermistor, LVDT, Strain gauge |
| Sensitivity | Generally lower (material dependent) | Generally higher |
| Signal conditioning | Simpler | Requires bridge / oscillator circuit |

| **Bioelectric Signal** | **Source** | **Amplitude (Typical)** | **Frequency Range (KTU)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | Heart muscle | 1 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz |
| EEG | Brain cortex | 50 µV | 0.5 – 100 Hz |
| EMG | Skeletal muscle | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 2000 Hz |
| EOG | Eye retina-cornea dipole | 0.5 mV | DC – 10 Hz |
| ERG | Retina (light response) | 0.5 mV | 0.2 – 200 Hz |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**Classify biomedical signals with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Biomedical signals are classified based on the physical origin of energy:

1. **Bioelectric Signals** — Generated by excitable cells due to ionic current flow. *Examples:* ECG (heart), EEG (brain), EMG (muscle).
2. **Biochemical Signals** — Variations in concentration of biochemicals in body fluids. *Examples:* pO₂, pCO₂, blood glucose, pH.
3. **Biomechanical Signals** — Pressure, force, displacement, flow. *Examples:* Blood pressure, respiration rate.
4. **Bioacoustic Signals** — Vibrations from physiological events. *Examples:* Heart sounds, breath sounds, Korotkoff sounds.
5. **Biothermal Signals** — Heat distribution in tissue. *Example:* Skin/core temperature.
6. **Bio-optical Signals** — Light absorption/reflection. *Example:* Pulse oximeter (PPG), retinal imaging.
7. **Biomagnetic Signals** — Weak magnetic fields from ionic currents. *Example:* MCG, MEG.

*(3 marks — 1 for naming the categories, 1 for examples, 1 for clarity & structure.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 
**Differentiate between active and passive transducers with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Active Transducer** | **Passive Transducer** |
|---|---|
| Generates electrical output on its own; no external supply needed | Requires external power supply to operate |
| Works on energy conversion principle | Works on energy modulation principle |
| Output is EMF, voltage, or current | Output is change in R, L, or C |
| Examples: Piezoelectric crystal, Thermocouple, Photovoltaic cell | Examples: Thermistor, LVDT, Strain gauge, Capacitive sensor |
| Sensitivity depends on material's intrinsic property | Sensitivity can be enhanced by circuit design |

*(3 marks — 2 for the differences in tabular form, 1 for correct examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q. (a)** Classify transducers in detail. Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric transducer with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Classification of Transducers:**

1. **Based on Energy Principle:**
   - **Active:** Self-generating — no external supply. Examples: piezoelectric, thermocouple.
   - **Passive:** Externally powered — modulates R/L/C. Examples: thermistor, LVDT, strain gauge.

2. **Based on Output Form:**
   - **Analog:** Continuous output (LVDT, thermistor).
   - **Digital:** Discrete output (rotary encoder).

3. **Based on Measurand:** Temperature, pressure, displacement, force, light, flow transducers.

4. **Based on Contact:** Invasive (catheter-tip) and non-invasive (cuff BP, ECG electrodes).

5. **Based on Principle of Operation:** Resistive, inductive, capacitive, piezoelectric, photoelectric, electromagnetic.

6. **Primary vs Secondary:** First-stage sensing vs signal-conditioning stage.

7. **Direct vs Inverse:** Non-electrical → electrical, or vice versa.

**Piezoelectric Transducer — Working Principle:**

- Certain crystals (e.g., quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT — lead zirconate titanate) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress.
- This is the **direct piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880).
- Governing equation: $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient (C/N).
- When force $F$ is applied along a sensitive axis, charge $Q$ appears on the electrodes plated on the crystal faces.
- The resulting open-circuit voltage is: $V = Q / C_p$, where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the crystal.
- The inverse piezoelectric effect (applying voltage → mechanical strain) is used in **actuators** and **ultrasonic transmitters**.

**Applications in Biomedical Engineering:**
- **Ultrasonic imaging probes** (transmit & receive 1–20 MHz sound waves).
- **Heart sound microphones** (phonocardiography).
- **Blood pressure sensors** (invasive catheter-tip).
- **Accelerometers** for body movement and tremor analysis.

*(Marks: 4 for classification, 3 for piezoelectric explanation & diagram. Total 7 marks.)*

**Q. (b)** A strain gauge with a gauge factor of 2.1 and resistance 120 Ω is bonded to a steel beam. Find the change in resistance when the beam is subjected to a strain of 800 micro-strain. If the gauge is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms of equal resistance 120 Ω and the bridge is excited by 5 V, calculate the output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Gauge factor $G = 2.1$
- Nominal resistance $R = 120\,\Omega$
- Strain $\epsilon = 800\,\mu\epsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$
- Bridge supply $V_{in} = 5\,\text{V}$
- Other arms $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120\,\Omega$ (assumed equal initially)

**Step 1: Change in resistance**

Using the gauge factor equation:
$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon
$$

$$
\Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \epsilon = 120 \times 2.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 0.2016\,\Omega
$$

**Step 2: Output voltage of Wheatstone bridge**

For a single active arm with all other arms equal $R$, the differential output voltage is:
$$
V_o = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta R}{R} \right)
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{5}{4} \times 8 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.1
$$

$$
V_o = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_o = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.1\,\text{mV}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta R = 0.2016\,\Omega$ and $V_o = 2.1\,\text{mV}$

*(Marks: 2 for ΔR, 3 for output voltage derivation, 2 for correct final value & unit. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various components of a biomedical instrumentation system. Classify the different types of biomedical signals with examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Block Diagram of Biomedical Instrumentation System:**

$$
\text{Patient} \rightarrow \text{Transducer/Electrode} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP/Microcontroller} \rightarrow \text{Display/Recorder}
$$

**Function of Each Block:**

1. **Patient / Physiological Source:** The living system from which the measurand originates.
2. **Transducer / Electrode:** Converts physiological energy (mechanical, chemical, thermal) into an electrical signal.
3. **Amplifier:** Boosts the weak bio-signal (e.g., ECG ~1 mV, EEG ~50 µV) to a usable level. Must have high CMRR, low noise.
4. **Filter:** Removes noise (50 Hz power-line interference, baseline wander, high-frequency noise). Bandpass filtering is most common.
5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Samples and quantizes the analog signal for digital processing. Sampling rate must obey Nyquist: $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$.
6. **DSP / Microcontroller:** Performs feature extraction, compression, analysis (FFT, wavelet, etc.).
7. **Display / Recorder:** Shows the waveform on a monitor (LCD/LED) or prints on paper / stores digitally.

**Classification of Biomedical Signals:**

(Refer to Section 1.4 for detailed table — bioelectric, biochemical, biomechanical, bioacoustic, biothermal, bio-optical, biomagnetic.)

*(Marks: 3 for block diagram, 2 for function explanation, 2 for signal classification. Total 7 marks.)*

**Q. (b)** An LVDT is used to measure displacement. The sensitivity of the LVDT is 0.5 V/mm. The input displacement is a sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 2 \sin(100\pi t)$ mm. Find the expression for the output voltage. Also calculate the RMS value of the output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Sensitivity $K = 0.5\,\text{V/mm}$
- Input displacement $x(t) = 2 \sin(100\pi t)\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1: Output voltage expression**

The output voltage of an LVDT is linearly related to the displacement:
$$
V_o(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$

$$
V_o(t) = 0.5 \times 2 \sin(100\pi t)
$$

$$
V_o(t) = 1.0 \sin(100\pi t)\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2: RMS value of the output voltage**

For a sinusoidal signal $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:
$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

With $V_m = 1.0\,\text{V}$:
$$
V_{rms} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,\text{V}
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_o(t) = 1.0 \sin(100\pi t)\,\text{V}$; $V_{rms} = 0.707\,\text{V} = 707\,\text{mV}$

*(Marks: 2 for displacement-to-voltage conversion, 2 for substituting values, 2 for RMS calculation, 1 for unit. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong gauge factor formula:** Students write $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G$ alone. **Correct:** $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon$. Losing 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing units of strain:** Micro-strain (µε) means $10^{-6}$, not $10^{-3}$. Convert carefully. **Half-mark deduction** in most valuations.
> 3. **Mixing up "active" and "self-generating":** Both mean the same — students often think they are different. **No deduction** if consistent, but **lose 1 mark** if examples contradict your definition.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in Wheatstone bridge:** Use $V_o = (V_{in}/4)(\Delta R / R)$ for single active arm, NOT $V_o = V_{in}(\Delta R / R)$. **Common 1-mark loss.**
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer:** KTU examiners expect "V" or "mV" — never leave it unitless.
> 6. **Piezoelectric diagram:** Skipping the diagram costs **0.5–1 mark** in part (a) of 7-mark questions. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 7. **RMS vs Peak confusion:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$, NOT $V_m \times \sqrt{2}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!)**

- ✅ **Biomedical signals** are time-varying quantities carrying physiological information.
- ✅ **Seven major types:** Bioelectric, Biochemical, Biomechanical, Bioacoustic, Biothermal, Bio-optical, Biomagnetic.
- ✅ **ECG frequency range:** 0.05 – 150 Hz (memorize for filter design questions).
- ✅ **EEG amplitude:** ~50 µV (smallest of the major bioelectric signals).
- ✅ **EMG frequency range:** 20 – 2000 Hz (widest — needs higher sampling rate).
- ✅ **Active transducers** = self-generating; no external power (piezoelectric, thermocouple, photovoltaic).
- ✅ **Passive transducers** = externally powered; modulate R/L/C (thermistor, LVDT, strain gauge).
- ✅ **Primary transducer** = first-stage sensing element.
- ✅ **Secondary transducer** = signal-conditioning stage.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric equation:** $Q = d \cdot F$ (charge proportional to force).
- ✅ **RTD equation:** $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$; for platinum, $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/°\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Strain gauge equation:** $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon$, where $G \approx 2$ for metals, $G \approx 100+$ for semiconductors.
- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge output (single active arm):** $V_o = (V_{in}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- ✅ **LVDT output:** $V_o = K \cdot x$; output is linear over the active range.
- ✅ **Capacitive transducer:** $C = \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A / d$; fractional sensitivity is $-1$ for displacement variation.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert Law for PPG:** $I = I_0 e^{-\epsilon c l}$ — foundation of pulse oximetry.
- ✅ **Biomedical instrumentation chain:** Patient → Transducer → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP → Display.
- ✅ **Nyquist sampling rule:** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- ✅ **Invasive vs non-invasive transducers:** Catheter-tip (invasive) vs cuff BP (non-invasive).
- ✅ **Direct vs inverse transducer:** Energy conversion direction matters for sensors vs actuators.
- ✅ **Common KTU 3-mark questions:** "Differentiate active/passive", "Classify signals", "Explain piezoelectric effect."
- ✅ **Common KTU 7-mark questions:** "Explain block diagram of biomedical instrumentation", "Derive strain gauge equation", "Working of LVDT."
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite diagram:** Always include a **labeled block diagram** wherever possible; missing diagrams = -1 to -2 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECG: Generation of Cardiac Action Potentials, Characteristics of ECG Signal, Lead Systems and Clinical Applications of ECG; EEG: Brain Action Potentials, Characteristics of Signal and Electrode System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS &TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG: Generation of Cardiac Action Potentials, Characteristics of ECG Signal, Lead Systems and Clinical Applications of ECG; EEG: Brain Action Potentials, Characteristics of Signal and Electrode System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cardiac Action Potential — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardiac Action Potential (CAP):** The time-varying transmembrane potential difference generated by the sequential opening and closing of voltage-gated ion channels (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) in cardiac muscle cells (myocytes), responsible for the mechanical contraction of the heart.

The cardiac action potential is fundamentally different from a neural action potential due to its **extended plateau phase (Phase 2)**, which prevents tetanic contraction and allows the ventricles to fill properly before the next beat.

**Standard Phases of the Ventricular Myocyte Action Potential:**

| Phase | Event | Primary Ion Current | Voltage Change |
|-------|-------|---------------------|----------------|
| **0** | Rapid Depolarization | Fast Na⁺ influx | −90 mV → +30 mV |
| **1** | Initial Repolarization | Transient K⁺ efflux, Na⁺ channel inactivation | +30 mV → 0 mV |
| **2** | Plateau Phase | Slow Ca²⁺ influx (L-type) balancing K⁺ efflux | ~0 mV (sustained) |
| **3** | Rapid Repolarization | Delayed rectifier K⁺ efflux | 0 mV → −90 mV |
| **4** | Resting Potential | K⁺ leak channels, Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump | −90 mV (stable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pacemaker cells (SA node, AV node) **lack Phase 1 and Phase 2** and exhibit a *Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization* due to the "funny current" ($I_f$) — a mixed Na⁺/K⁺ inward current. This is what makes the SA node the natural cardiac pacemaker (intrinsic rate ~**100 bpm**, reduced to ~**70 bpm** at rest by vagal tone).

---

### 1.2 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG):** A non-invasive graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart over time, captured at the body surface using skin electrodes. It represents the **summed vector** of all cardiac action potentials occurring during each cardiac cycle.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Orchestra Recording" Analogy:**
Imagine the heart as a large orchestra. Each myocardial cell is an individual musician playing (depolarizing) at a specific moment. The ECG is the recording made by microphones placed around the concert hall. The conductor (SA node) starts each piece (cardiac cycle). The microphone picks up the *net* sound — quiet when only a few cells fire, and loud when the entire ventricular section plays in unison (the QRS complex). You cannot hear individual violinists; you only hear the **summed vectorial activity** of the entire ensemble.

---

### 1.3 Electroencephalogram (EEG) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electroencephalogram (EEG):** A non-invasive recording of the summed postsynaptic electrical activity of cortical pyramidal neurons of the brain, obtained via electrodes placed on the scalp.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Brainwave Symphony" Analogy:**
The brain is like a vast stadium of whispering fans. Each individual whisper is too faint to hear. But when thousands of fans whisper in synchrony (e.g., during focused attention or deep sleep), the collective hum becomes audible at the stadium walls (the scalp). The EEG microphone picks up only these **synchronous, summed cortical signals** — never the activity of a single neuron.

---

### 1.4 Key Signal Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | ECG Signal | EEG Signal |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Amplitude** | **0.5 – 5 mV** (typically ~1 mV) | **10 – 100 µV** (typically ~50 µV) |
| **Frequency Range** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** (clinical: 0.5 – 40 Hz) | **0.5 – 100 Hz** (clinical: 0.5 – 30 Hz) |
| **Source** | Cardiac myocytes | Cortical pyramidal neurons |
| **Reference Point** | Isoelectric line (TP segment) | Reference electrode (ear, mastoid) |
| **Standard Speed** | 25 mm/s paper speed | 30 mm/s paper speed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ionic Basis of the Cardiac Action Potential (Phase-wise)

#### Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization
- Triggered when the membrane potential reaches **threshold (~ −70 mV)**.
- Voltage-gated **fast Na⁺ channels** open → massive Na⁺ influx.
- The Nernst equilibrium potential for Na⁺ is approximately **+60 mV**, so the membrane potential rapidly rises toward this value.
- Governed by the **Hodgkin–Huxley activation variable** $m$ (fast):

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m (1-m) - \beta_m m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Phase 0 in SA/AV node cells is **Ca²⁺-mediated (slow response)**, NOT Na⁺-mediated. This is why nodal cells have a slower conduction velocity (~0.05 m/s) compared to ventricular myocytes (~0.3–1 m/s) and Purkinje fibers (~2–4 m/s).

#### Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization
- Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (h-gate closes).
- **Transient outward K⁺ current ($I_{to}$)** produces a brief notch.

#### Phase 2 — Plateau Phase
- **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** open → sustained Ca²⁺ influx (inward depolarizing current).
- This is balanced by **delayed rectifier K⁺ currents** ($I_{Kr}$, $I_{Ks}$) → outward repolarizing current.
- Net result: a near-constant plateau potential near 0 mV lasting **~200–300 ms**.
- This prolonged plateau is the **hallmark** of cardiac AP and is **absent in skeletal muscle and neurons**.

#### Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization
- Ca²⁺ channels **inactivate**.
- K⁺ efflux dominates → membrane potential returns toward **E_K ≈ −94 mV**.

#### Phase 4 — Resting Potential
- Maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) and K⁺ leak channels.
- Resting membrane potential: **−80 to −90 mV** in ventricular myocytes.

---

### 2.2 ECG Waveform Anatomy — The Standard Cardiac Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **A single cardiac cycle on ECG contains 5 primary waves/intervals**, each corresponding to a specific electrophysiological event:

```
   ┌─ P wave ─┐  ┌────── QRS complex ──────┐  ┌─ T wave ─┐
___╱‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾╲___╱‾‾╲___╱‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾╲___╱‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾╲___
  (atrial    ) (Q) (R) (S)                (ventricular repol.)
   depol.
```

| Feature | Physiological Origin | Normal Duration | Clinical Significance |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | **< 120 ms** | Absent → atrial fibrillation; Inverted → junctional rhythm |
| **PR Interval** | AV nodal conduction delay | **120 – 200 ms** | Prolonged → AV block (1st degree) |
| **QRS Complex** | Ventricular depolarization | **< 120 ms** | Wide → bundle branch block; Tall R → hypertrophy |
| **ST Segment** | Ventricular plateau (Phase 2) | **80 – 120 ms** | Elevation → myocardial infarction; Depression → ischemia |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | **160 ms** | Inverted → ischemia; Tall peaked → hyperkalemia |
| **QT Interval** | Total ventricular activity | **350 – 440 ms** (HR-dependent) | Prolonged → Torsades de Pointes risk |
| **U wave** | Late repolarization of Purkinje fibers | Often absent | Prominent → hypokalemia |

---

### 2.3 The 12-Lead ECG System — Einthoven's Triangle & Beyond

#### 2.3.1 Einthoven's Bipolar Limb Leads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law (KTU High-Yield):** The sum of the potentials in leads I and III equals the potential in lead II:
> $$E_{II} = E_{I} + E_{III}$$

The three standard bipolar leads form **Einthoven's equilateral triangle**, with the heart at its electrical center:

| Lead | Positive Electrode | Negative Electrode | Views |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|-------|
| **Lead I** | Left Arm (LA) | Right Arm (RA) | Lateral wall of LV |
| **Lead II** | Left Leg (LL) | Right Arm (RA) | Inferior wall (best for P waves) |
| **Lead III** | Left Leg (LL) | Left Arm (LA) | Inferior wall |

The voltage measured is the **potential difference** between the two electrodes:

$$E_{I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$
$$E_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$
$$E_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

#### 2.3.2 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger)

These are **unipolar** leads with a reference terminal at the **central terminal (Wilson CT)**:

$$V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

| Lead | Active Electrode | Cardiac Region Viewed |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| **aVR** | Right Arm | Right side of heart / cavity |
| **aVL** | Left Arm | High lateral wall of LV |
| **aVF** | Left Leg | Inferior wall of LV |

Augmentation factor: $aV_X = V_X - \frac{1}{2}(V_Y + V_Z)$ where X, Y, Z are the other two limb electrodes.

#### 2.3.3 Precordial (Chest) Leads

Six unipolar leads placed on the anterior chest wall:

| Lead | Position (Anatomical Landmark) |
|------|-------------------------------|
| **V1** | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border |
| **V2** | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border |
| **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| **V4** | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line |
| **V5** | Anterior axillary line, same level as V4 |
| **V6** | Mid-axillary line, same level as V4 |

---

### 2.4 EEG — Brain Rhythms & Clinical Significance

The EEG signal is classified into **frequency bands**, each associated with specific mental/physiological states:

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Amplitude | State / Clinical Correlation |
|--------|---------------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | High (75–200 µV) | Deep sleep (NREM Stage 3), infants, pathological lesions |
| **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Moderate (50–100 µV) | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, childhood |
| **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Moderate (30–50 µV) | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (posterior dominant) |
| **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Low (5–20 µV) | Active thinking, focused attention, anxiety |
| **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Very low | Sensory binding, cognition, perception |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mu rhythm (8–13 Hz):** A special alpha-range rhythm observed over the **motor cortex** (C3, C4), attenuated by movement or intention to move. Important in BCI applications.

---

### 2.5 EEG Electrode Placement — International 10-20 System

The **10-20 system** (Jasper, 1958) is the international standard for EEG electrode placement:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention:** Each electrode is labeled with a **letter** (anatomical region) followed by a **number/letter** (position):
> - **F** = Frontal, **C** = Central, **P** = Parietal, **T** = Temporal, **O** = Occipital
> - **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8)** = Right hemisphere
> - **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7)** = Left hemisphere
> - **"z" suffix** = Midline (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz)

**Key reference points:**
- **Nasion (Nz):** Intersection of frontal and nasal bones (front)
- **Inion (Iz):** Prominence at back of skull (rear)
- **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** In front of ears (earlobe reference)

**21 standard electrodes** in the 10-20 system: Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz, A1, A2.

> [!TIP]
> The inter-electrode distances are **10% or 20%** of the total skull dimensions (nasion-to-inion or preauricular-to-preauricular) — hence the name "10-20 system."

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Exam Alert:** The following table is a high-density revision resource. Memorize the equations and the typical values for maximum marks in derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|----------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Nernst Potential (single ion) | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | V; at 37°C: $61.5 \log_{10} ([out]/[in])$ |
| 2 | Resting Membrane Potential (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz) | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Einthoven's Law | $E_{II} = E_{I} + E_{III}$ | mV |
| 4 | Central Terminal (Wilson) | $V_{CT} = (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})/3$ | mV |
| 5 | Augmented Lead | $aV_X = V_X - (V_Y + V_Z)/2$ | mV |
| 6 | Heart Rate (from ECG) | $HR = 1500 / \text{RR intervals in mm}$ (at 25 mm/s) | bpm |
| 7 | QTc (Bazett's correction) | $QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | seconds (normalized to HR=60) |
| 8 | Cardiac Output | $CO = HR \times SV$ | L/min |
| 9 | Cardiac Vector Projection | $\vec{V}_{lead} = \vert \vec{M}_{heart} \vert \cos\theta$ | mV (θ = angle between vector and lead axis) |
| 10 | EEG Band Power (Parseval) | $P_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert X(f) \vert^2 \, df$ | µV² |
| 11 | Electrode-Skin Impedance | $Z = \sqrt{R_s^2 + (1/2\pi f C_s)^2}$ | Ω (should be < 5 kΩ) |
| 12 | CMRR (Instrumentation Amp) | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ | dB (typical > 100 dB for ECG) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Starting Premise:** The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) are points on the body surface. The voltage at each point (relative to a common reference) is $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, $V_{LL}$.

**Step 1:** Define the three bipolar lead voltages (potential differences):

$$E_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \quad \text{(Lead I)}$$

$$E_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \quad \text{(Lead II)}$$

$$E_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} \quad \text{(Lead III)}$$

**Step 2:** Add the expressions for Lead I and Lead III:

$$E_I + E_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

**Step 3:** Expand the right-hand side. Notice that $+V_{LA}$ and $-V_{LA}$ **cancel** algebraically:

$$E_I + E_{III} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

$$E_I + E_{III} = -V_{RA} + V_{LL}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder the right-hand side to match the form of Lead II:

$$E_I + E_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that the right-hand side is exactly the definition of $E_{II}$:

$$E_I + E_{III} = E_{II}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **∴ Q.E.D.:** $E_{II} = E_{I} + E_{III}$ — Einthoven's Law is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed loop formed by the three limb electrodes.

**Engineering Utility:** This law serves as a **built-in consistency check** for any 12-lead ECG machine. If $E_{II} \neq E_I + E_{III}$ (within measurement tolerance), the system has a fault (electrode disconnection, amplifier saturation, etc.).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Goldberger's Augmented Lead Equations

**Starting Premise:** Wilson's Central Terminal is a reference electrode with a "neutral" potential:

$$V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

For an unipolar lead $V_X$ measured against this central terminal, the "exploring" electrode signal is:

$$V_X = V_{electrode} - V_{CT}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the expression for $V_{CT}$:

$$V_X = V_{electrode} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Goldberger's modification — **disconnect** the exploring electrode from the central terminal (so the CT uses only the *other two* electrodes). The new "modified" central terminal becomes:

$$V_{CT}^{*} = \frac{V_Y + V_Z}{2}$$

where Y and Z are the two limb electrodes **other than** the exploring one X.

**Step 3:** The augmented lead voltage is then:

$$aV_X = V_X - V_{CT}^{*} = V_X - \frac{V_Y + V_Z}{2}$$

**Step 4:** The factor by which the original unipolar voltage is "augmented":

$$\frac{aV_X}{V_X} = \frac{V_X - (V_Y + V_Z)/2}{V_X - (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})/3}$$

Since $V_X \gg (V_Y + V_Z)/2$ in practice, this ratio approximates **1.5** (a 50% augmentation in signal amplitude).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **∴ KTU Result:** $aV_X = V_X - \frac{V_Y + V_Z}{2}$ and the augmentation factor is **3/2 = 1.5** (an amplitude increase of ~50%).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Nernst Equation for K⁺ at Resting Potential

**Starting Premise:** At equilibrium, the chemical force (concentration gradient) and electrical force (membrane potential) on an ion balance exactly.

**Step 1:** The electrical work to move a monovalent ion across a potential difference $E_K$ is:

$$W_{elec} = zFE_K$$

**Step 2:** The chemical work to move the ion from outside (concentration $[K^+]_o$) to inside ($[K^+]_i$) is:

$$W_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o}\right)$$

**Step 3:** At equilibrium, $W_{elec} = -W_{chem}$ (negative sign because the ion moves against the electrical gradient to reach equilibrium):

$$zFE_K = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o}\right)$$

$$zFE_K = RT \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)$$

**Step 4:** For K⁺, $z = +1$:

$$E_K = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)$$

**Step 5:** At body temperature $T = 310\,K$ (37°C), the prefactor evaluates to:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{96485} \approx 0.0267\,V = 26.7\,mV$$

**Step 6:** Using physiological concentrations $[K^+]_o = 4\,mM$ and $[K^+]_i = 140\,mM$:

$$E_K = 26.7 \times \ln(4/140) = 26.7 \times \ln(0.0286)$$

$$E_K = 26.7 \times (-3.555) = -94.9\,mV$$

> [!NOTE]
> **∴ KTU Result:** $E_K \approx -94\,mV$ at 37°C — this is the equilibrium potential for K⁺, which explains why the resting membrane potential of ventricular myocytes is close to **−90 mV** (K⁺ dominance).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: ECG vs EEG

| Analytical Feature | ECG | EEG |
|--------------------|-----|-----|
| **Origin of Signal** | Vector sum of cardiac dipole moments | Synchronous post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons |
| **Primary Source Current** | Transmembrane ionic fluxes (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺) | Excitatory/inhibitory post-synaptic currents (EPSCs/IPSCs) |
| **Volume Conductor** | Thoracic cavity (high conductivity) | Skull + CSF + brain (low conductivity, attenuating) |
| **Signal Bandwidth** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 0.5 – 100 Hz |
| **Standard Sampling Rate** | 500 Hz – 1 kHz | 256 Hz – 512 Hz |
| **No. of Electrodes (Standard)** | **10 electrodes → 12 leads** | **21 electrodes (10-20 system)** |
| **Reference** | Wilson Central Terminal / Right Leg Drive | Linked-ear / Cz / Average reference |
| **Primary Noise Source** | 50/60 Hz powerline, EMG, motion artifact | 50/60 Hz powerline, EOG (eye blinks), EMG, sweat |
| **Diagnostic Strength** | Arrhythmia, MI, conduction blocks | Epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies |
| **Invasive?** | Non-invasive (surface) | Non-invasive (surface) — also has invasive (ECoG, depth electrodes) |
| **Amplitude Range** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **10 – 100 µV** (i.e., 50× smaller than ECG) |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Limited (far-field from torso) | Poor (1–2 cm scalp; ECoG → mm; depth → sub-mm) |
| **Temporal Resolution** | Excellent (~ms) | Excellent (~ms) — far superior to fMRI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cardiac Action Potential Phases — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Resting State Phase 4: -90 mV"] --> A1{"Threshold Reached: -70 mV?"}
    A1 -- No --> A0
    A1 -- Yes --> A2["Phase 0: Fast Na+ Influx"]
    A2 --> A3["Phase 1: Na+ Inactivates, I_to K+ Efflux"]
    A3 --> A4["Phase 2: Plateau, L-type Ca2+ Influx balances K+ Efflux"]
    A4 --> A5["Phase 3: Ca2+ Inactivates, K+ Efflux Dominates"]
    A5 --> A0

    A2 -. Note: SA/AV node use Ca2+ not Na+ .- ANN1["Pacemaker cells have Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization via I_f current"]
    ANN1 -.-> A0
```

### 4.2 Cardiac Conduction Pathway — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA["SA Node: Natural Pacemaker, 60-100 bpm"] --> AV["AV Node: Delay 100 ms"]
    AV --> BH["Bundle of His"]
    BH --> RBB["Right Bundle Branch"]
    BH --> LBB["Left Bundle Branch"]
    RBB --> PF1["Purkinje Fibers Right"]
    LBB --> PF2["Purkinje Fibers Left"]
    PF1 --> RV["Right Ventricle Depolarization"]
    PF2 --> LV["Left Ventricle Depolarization"]
    RV --> ECG_OUT["Summed Vector: QRS Complex on ECG"]
    LV --> ECG_OUT
```

### 4.3 12-Lead ECG System — Electrode & Lead Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIMB["Limb Electrodes (Bipolar + Augmented)"]
        RA["RA: Right Arm"]
        LA["LA: Left Arm"]
        LL["LL: Left Leg"]
        RL["RL: Right Leg - Ground Reference"]
    end

    subgraph BIPOLAR["Einthoven Bipolar Leads"]
        L1["Lead I: LA minus RA"]
        L2["Lead II: LL minus RA"]
        L3["Lead III: LL minus LA"]
    end

    subgraph AUG["Goldberger Augmented Leads"]
        AVR["aVR: RA vs Modified CT"]
        AVL["aVL: LA vs Modified CT"]
        AVF["aVF: LL vs Modified CT"]
    end

    subgraph PRECORD["Wilson Precordial Leads"]
        V1["V1: 4th ICS Right Sternal Border"]
        V2["V2: 4th ICS Left Sternal Border"]
        V3["V3: Midway V2 to V4"]
        V4["V4: 5th ICS Mid-Clavicular"]
        V5["V5: Anterior Axillary Line"]
        V6["V6: Mid-Axillary Line"]
    end

    RA --> L1
    LA --> L1
    RA --> L2
    LL --> L2
    LA --> L3
    LL --> L3

    RA --> AVR
    LA --> AVL
    LL --> AVF

    V1 --> ECG_SIGNAL["ECG Machine: 12 Simultaneous Channels"]
    V2 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    V3 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    V4 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    V5 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    V6 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    L1 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    L2 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    L3 --> ECG_SIGNAL
    AVR --> ECG_SIGNAL
    AVL --> ECG_SIGNAL
    AVF --> ECG_SIGNAL
```

### 4.4 ECG Waveform — Anatomy Block Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["P Wave: Atrial Depolarization, 80 ms"] --> PRI["PR Segment: AV Node Delay, 50-120 ms"]
    PRI --> PRINTER["PR Interval: 120-200 ms, Start of P to Start of QRS"]
    PRINTER --> Q["Q Wave: Septal Depolarization, Initial Negative"]
    Q --> R["R Wave: Main Ventricular Depolarization, Large Positive"]
    R --> S["S Wave: Late Ventricular Depolarization, Negative"]
    S --> STSEG["ST Segment: Ventricular Plateau Phase 2, Isoelectric"]
    STSEG --> T["T Wave: Ventricular Repolarization, 160 ms"]
    T --> QT["QT Interval: Total Ventricular Electrical Activity, 350-440 ms"]
    QT --> NEXT["Next Cardiac Cycle"]
    NEXT --> P
```

### 4.5 EEG Electrode Placement — 10-20 System Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT["Frontal Region"]
        FP1["Fp1: Frontopolar Left"]
        FP2["Fp2: Frontopolar Right"]
        F3["F3: Frontal Left"]
        F4["F4: Frontal Right"]
        F7["F7: Inferior Frontal Left"]
        F8["F8: Inferior Frontal Right"]
        FZ["Fz: Midline Frontal"]
    end

    subgraph CENTRAL["Central Region"]
        C3["C3: Central Left - Motor Cortex Hand Area"]
        C4["C4: Central Right - Motor Cortex Hand Area"]
        CZ["Cz: Midline Central - Vertex"]
    end

    subgraph PARIETAL["Parietal Region"]
        P3["P3: Parietal Left"]
        P4["P4: Parietal Right"]
        PZ["Pz: Midline Parietal"]
    end

    subgraph TEMPORAL["Temporal Region"]
        T3["T3: Temporal Left"]
        T4["T4: Temporal Right"]
        T5["T5: Posterior Temporal Left"]
        T6["T6: Posterior Temporal Right"]
    end

    subgraph OCCIPITAL["Occipital Region"]
        O1["O1: Occipital Left - Visual Cortex"]
        O2["O2: Occipital Right - Visual Cortex"]
    end

    subgraph REFERENCE["Reference Electrodes"]
        A1["A1: Left Ear Reference"]
        A2["A2: Right Ear Reference"]
    end

    FP1 --> AMP["Differential Amplifier: Gain 10,000-100,000"]
    FP2 --> AMP
    F3 --> AMP
    F4 --> AMP
    C3 --> AMP
    C4 --> AMP
    P3 --> AMP
    P4 --> AMP
    O1 --> AMP
    O2 --> AMP
    T3 --> AMP
    T4 --> AMP
    T5 --> AMP
    T6 --> AMP
    F7 --> AMP
    F8 --> AMP
    FZ --> AMP
    CZ --> AMP
    PZ --> AMP
    A1 --> AMP
    A2 --> AMP
    AMP --> OUTPUT["EEG Signal: 10-100 microV, 0.5-100 Hz"]
```

### 4.6 Brain-to-EEG Signal Generation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["Individual Cortical Neuron: Postsynaptic Potential, ~1 mV"] --> N2["Sum of 10,000 to 1,000,000 Synchronous Neurons"]
    N2 --> N3["Volume Conduction Through CSF and Skull"]
    N3 --> N4["Signal Attenuation: 10x to 100x"]
    N4 --> N5["Scalp Surface Potential: 10 to 100 microV"]
    N5 --> N6["Electrode Picks Up Differential Signal"]
    N6 --> N7["Amplification, Filtering 0.5-100 Hz"]
    N7 --> N8["Digitization: 256-512 Hz Sampling"]
    N8 --> N9["EEG Display and Analysis"]
```
