## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A consists of short-answer questions. Each is worth **3 marks**. Expect brief, definition-based or short-explanation questions in this section.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
**"Differentiate between the action potentials of a ventricular myocyte and a SA nodal cell. Why is the SA node the natural pacemaker of the heart?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Ventricular Myocyte | SA Nodal Cell |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Resting potential (Phase 4) | **Stable at −90 mV** | **Spontaneous depolarization** (slow upward drift) |
| Phase 0 upstroke | Fast Na⁺ influx | Slow Ca²⁺ influx |
| Phases 1 and 2 | **Present** (notch + plateau) | **Absent** |
| Threshold | −70 mV | −40 mV |
| Max. depolarization | +30 mV | 0 to +10 mV |

**Why SA node is the natural pacemaker:** The SA node has the **fastest rate of spontaneous Phase 4 depolarization** (steepest slope of the pacemaker potential) due to the "funny current" $I_f$ and Ca²⁺ influx. It reaches threshold fastest (~**70–80 times per minute** at rest) and thus overrides the slower intrinsic rates of the AV node (~50 bpm) and Purkinje fibers (~25–40 bpm). This dominance is called **"overdrive suppression."**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"State Einthoven's Law. Mention its significance in 12-lead ECG interpretation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Einthoven's Law:** The algebraic sum of the voltages recorded in standard limb leads I and III equals the voltage recorded in lead II:

$$E_{II} = E_{I} + E_{III}$$

**Significance:**
1. Acts as an **internal consistency check** for ECG machine calibration.
2. If the law is violated, it indicates a fault such as **electrode misplacement, lead reversal, or amplifier saturation**.
3. Theoretically derived from **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed loop of the three limb electrodes.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry **14 marks** total, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **ONE full question** out of a choice of two (e.g., 7a OR 7b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **generation of the cardiac action potential** with neat phase diagrams. Describe the **ionic basis** of each phase and justify why the cardiac action potential has a prolonged plateau compared to a neuronal action potential.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Einthoven's triangle** and derive **Einthoven's Law**. Also explain **Goldberger's augmented leads**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation of Cardiac Action Potential [7 Marks]:**

The cardiac action potential of a ventricular myocyte consists of **five distinct phases (0–4)**:

**Phase 4 — Resting Potential (−90 mV):**
- The membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ via **inward rectifier K⁺ channels ($I_{K1}$)**.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (electrogenic, 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in) maintains ionic gradients.
- The membrane potential sits near the K⁺ Nernst potential $E_K \approx -94$ mV.

**Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization (−90 mV → +30 mV):**
- Triggered by depolarization from neighboring cells to threshold (~**−70 mV**).
- **Voltage-gated fast Na⁺ channels** open → massive Na⁺ influx.
- The upstroke velocity (dV/dt) is very high (~**200–400 V/s**).
- In SA/AV node cells, Phase 0 is **Ca²⁺-mediated (slow response)**, with a slower upstroke.

**Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization (+30 mV → 0 mV):**
- Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (h-gate closes).
- **Transient outward K⁺ current ($I_{to}$)** produces a brief "notch."
- Brief and rapid repolarization.

**Phase 2 — Plateau (~0 mV, lasting ~200–300 ms):**
- **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** open → sustained Ca²⁺ influx.
- Balanced by **delayed rectifier K⁺ currents** ($I_{Kr}$, $I_{Ks}$).
- Net current ≈ 0 → plateau.
- **This Ca²⁺ entry triggers Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release (CICR)** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, initiating contraction.

**Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization (0 mV → −90 mV):**
- Ca²⁺ channels inactivate.
- K⁺ efflux dominates → rapid return to resting potential.

**Why the plateau is prolonged (compared to neurons):**
- Neuronal AP lasts **~1–2 ms**; cardiac AP lasts **~200–300 ms**.
- The plateau exists because of the **balance between inward Ca²⁺ current and outward K⁺ current**.
- This prolonged duration **prevents tetanic contraction** of the cardiac muscle, allowing the ventricle to fill with blood (diastole) before the next contraction.
- It also ensures **synchronous contraction** of all ventricular myocytes.

**(b) Einthoven's Triangle and Law [7 Marks]:**

**Einthoven's Triangle:** An equilateral triangle formed conceptually by the three limb electrodes — **RA (Right Arm)**, **LA (Left Arm)**, and **LL (Left Leg)** — with the heart at its electrical center. The cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ projects onto each lead axis.

**The three standard bipolar leads:**
- Lead I: $E_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$
- Lead II: $E_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$
- Lead III: $E_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$

**Derivation of Einthoven's Law (from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**

$$E_I + E_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

$$E_I + E_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = E_{II}$$

$$\boxed{E_{II} = E_{I} + E_{III}}$$

**Goldberger's Augmented Leads:**
- Wilson's Central Terminal (CT): $V_{CT} = (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})/3$
- Goldberger modified this by **disconnecting** the exploring electrode from the CT.
- The new "augmented" lead is: $aV_X = V_X - (V_Y + V_Z)/2$
- Three augmented leads: **aVR, aVL, aVF** — each views a specific region of the heart.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **various leads used in a 12-lead ECG system** with a neat diagram. List the normal **duration and amplitude** of each ECG wave and segment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **generation of EEG signals** and the **10-20 electrode placement system**. List the **five major EEG frequency bands** with their clinical significance.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 12-Lead ECG System [7 Marks]:**

The standard **12-lead ECG** uses **10 electrodes** to produce **12 different views** of the heart's electrical activity:

**(i) 3 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven):**
- Lead I (LA − RA)
- Lead II (LL − RA) — best for P wave analysis
- Lead III (LL − LA)

**(ii) 3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger):**
- aVR (Right Arm), aVL (Left Arm), aVF (Left Leg)
- Augmented by a factor of **1.5** (3/2)
- Reference: Modified central terminal using the other two limb electrodes

**(iii) 6 Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson):**
- V1 (4th ICS, right sternal border)
- V2 (4th ICS, left sternal border)
- V3 (midway V2–V4)
- V4 (5th ICS, mid-clavicular line)
- V5 (anterior axillary line)
- V6 (mid-axillary line)

**Normal ECG Wave Parameters:**

| Wave/Interval | Duration | Amplitude |
|---------------|----------|-----------|
| P wave | **< 120 ms** | **< 2.5 mm (0.25 mV)** |
| PR interval | **120 – 200 ms** | — |
| QRS complex | **< 120 ms** | R wave: 5–25 mm; S wave: < 30 mm |
| ST segment | **80 – 120 ms** | Isoelectric (no deviation) |
| T wave | **~160 ms** | **< 5 mm in limb leads; < 10 mm in precordial** |
| QT interval | **350 – 440 ms** (HR-dependent) | — |

**(b) EEG Generation and 10-20 System [7 Marks]:**

**Generation of EEG Signals:**
- The cerebral cortex contains **pyramidal neurons** aligned perpendicular to the cortical surface.
- Their **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs)** generate local currents.
- When thousands to millions of these neurons fire **synchronously**, the summed extracellular field potential becomes large enough to be detected at the scalp.
- The signal is then conducted through the **volume conductor** (brain tissue → CSF → skull → scalp) with significant attenuation (~**10× to 100×**).
- Final amplitude at scalp: **10–100 µV**.
- **Skull acts as a spatial low-pass filter**, reducing high-frequency content.

**10-20 Electrode Placement System:**
- An international standard (Jasper, 1958) for reproducible EEG electrode placement.
- Reference points: **Nasion (front)**, **Inion (back)**, and **Pre-auricular points (left and right ears)**.
- Distances are measured at **10% and 20%** of these anatomical landmarks.
- 21 standard electrodes labeled by:
  - **Region:** F (Frontal), C (Central), P (Parietal), T (Temporal), O (Occipital), Fp (Frontopolar)
  - **Side:** Even numbers = Right; Odd numbers = Left
  - **Midline:** "z" suffix (Fz, Cz, Pz)

**Five Major EEG Frequency Bands:**

| Band | Frequency | State | Clinical Use |
|------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep, infants | Detecting tumors, deep sleep disorders |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep | ADHD, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed, eyes closed | Relaxation, BCI control |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, alert | Anxiety, focused attention |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Higher cognition | Sensory binding, perception |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing the Phase 0 ion in nodal vs ventricular cells** — SA node uses **Ca²⁺**, not Na⁺. Mark deduction of 1–2 marks for this common error.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention Wilson's Central Terminal** when describing augmented leads — examiners specifically look for the formula $V_{CT} = (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})/3$.
> 3. **Wrong frequency band assignments** — Alpha is 8–13 Hz (not 8–12 Hz). Beta starts at 13 Hz (not 14 Hz). Use the **8–13 Hz** standard to be safe.
> 4. **ECG amplitude vs EEG amplitude confusion** — ECG is in **mV (millivolts)**, EEG is in **µV (microvolts)** — a 1000× difference. Mentioning this shows depth.
> 5. **Einthoven's Law derivation** — Do not just state the formula; you MUST show the **algebraic cancellation** of $V_{LA}$ terms. This is a frequent place where 2–3 marks are lost.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (ECG & EEG)**

**🔹 CARDIAC ACTION POTENTIAL**
- 5 phases: 0 (Na⁺ in), 1 (K⁺ out), 2 (Ca²⁺ plateau), 3 (K⁺ out), 4 (rest)
- SA node uses **Ca²⁺** in Phase 0, not Na⁺ — slower upstroke
- **Plateau (Phase 2) is unique to cardiac muscle** — prevents tetany
- Resting potential ≈ **−90 mV** (close to $E_K$)
- Nernst equation: $E_{ion} = (RT/zF) \ln([ion]_o/[ion]_i)$

**🔹 ECG SIGNAL CHARACTERISTICS**
- P wave: atrial depolarization (< 120 ms)
- QRS: ventricular depolarization (< 120 ms, contains R wave up to 25 mm)
- T wave: ventricular repolarization
- PR interval: 120–200 ms (AV conduction)
- QT interval: 350–440 ms (HR-dependent)
- Amplitude: ~1 mV; Bandwidth: 0.05–150 Hz
- Paper speed: 25 mm/s

**🔹 LEAD SYSTEMS**
- 12 leads from 10 electrodes
- 3 bipolar (Einthoven): I, II, III — forms equilateral triangle
- 3 augmented unipolar (Goldberger): aVR, aVL, aVF — augmented × 1.5
- 6 precordial (Wilson): V1–V6
- **Einthoven's Law: $E_{II} = E_I + E_{III}$** (from KVL)

**🔹 ECG CLINICAL APPLICATIONS**
- MI detection (ST elevation/depression, Q waves)
- Arrhythmias (AF, VT, VF, heart block)
- Hypertrophy (R wave amplitude criteria)
- Electrolyte imbalances (peaked T = hyperkalemia)
- Drug effects (QT prolongation)

**🔹 EEG SIGNAL**
- Origin: synchronous postsynaptic potentials of **pyramidal cortical neurons**
- Amplitude: 10–100 µV (1000× smaller than ECG)
- Frequency range: 0.5–100 Hz
- Volume conduction through skull causes **attenuation and spatial smearing**

**🔹 EEG RHYTHMS (Must memorize in order)**
- **Delta (0.5–4 Hz):** Deep sleep, infants, pathology
- **Theta (4–8 Hz):** Drowsiness, light sleep
- **Alpha (8–13 Hz):** Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (posterior)
- **Beta (13–30 Hz):** Active thinking, alert
- **Gamma (> 30 Hz):** Higher cognition, perception

**🔹 10-20 EEG SYSTEM**
- 21 standard electrodes
- Fp, F, C, P, T, O (regions); even = right, odd = left, z = midline
- Reference: linked ears (A1, A2) or average reference
- Distances: 10% and 20% of nasion-inion / preauricular dimensions

**🔹 KEY NUMERICAL VALUES TO MEMORIZE**
- $E_K \approx -94$ mV
- Resting Vm ≈ -90 mV (ventricular), -60 mV (SA node)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, $T = 310$ K (37°C)
- $RT/F = 26.7$ mV at body temperature
- ECG sampling rate: 500 Hz–1 kHz
- EEG sampling rate: 256–512 Hz
- Heart rate from ECG: $HR = 1500$ / (RR in mm) at 25 mm/s

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **draw a labeled diagram** for any ECG or EEG question. A clear block diagram (mimicking the style of Section 4 of these notes) typically earns **2–3 extra marks** versus a text-only answer. Examiners reward visual clarity and structured presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clinical Applications of EEG and EMG: Electrical Activity of Muscles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Clinical Applications of EEG and EMG: Electrical Activity of Muscles # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electromyography (EMG) — Electrical Activity of Muscles **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectric recording technique that captures the **summed electrical activity** of contracting skeletal muscle fibers, typically measured as the superposition of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** generated by active **Motor Units** (the functional union of a single alpha motor neuron in the spinal cord and all the extrafusal muscle fibers it innervates). - **Source:** Depolarization–repolarization cycles of muscle fiber membranes (sarcolemma). - **Measured Quantity:** Time-varying potential difference (in µV to mV) between surface electrodes or needle electrodes. - **Dominant Frequency Band:** **20 Hz to 500 Hz** (clinically used band is **20 Hz – 150 Hz** for surface EMG). - **Typical Amplitude:** **50 µV – 5 mV** for surface EMG; up to **10 mV** for intramuscular needle EMG. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠 > [!NOTE] > Think of a stadium with **thousands of light bulbs** (muscle fibers). Each section of bulbs is wired to **one master switch** (the motor neuron). When you flex your bicep, dozens of switches flip on at slightly **different times**. EMG is the **photographer's long-exposure camera** — it captures the *overall glow* of the stadium rather than tracking each individual bulb. The intensity of that glow tells a doctor how many fibers are firing, how strongly, and how synchronized they are. A diseased nerve (e.g., in **ALS** or **neuropathy**) is like a stadium whose master switches are *failing or misfiring* — the resulting glow pattern becomes irregular, weak, or chaotic. EMG detects these patterns. --- ## 1.2 Electroencephalography (EEG) — Electrical Activity of the Brain **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is the non-invasive recording of the brain's spontaneous **postsynaptic summed dendritic electrical activity** (primarily cortical pyramidal neurons), measured as voltage fluctuations on the scalp using surface electrodes, and characterized by oscillatory **rhythms** across five canonical frequency bands (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma). - **Standard Frequency Bands:** - **Delta (δ):** **0.5 – 4 Hz** — deep slow-wave sleep, infants, pathological coma. - **Theta (θ):** **4 – 8 Hz** — drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, childhood. - **Alpha (α):** **8 – 13 Hz** — relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, posterior dominance. - **Beta (β):** **13 – 30 Hz** — active thinking, focused attention, alertness. - **Gamma (γ):** **> 30 Hz** — higher cognition, sensory binding, memory. - **Typical Amplitude:** **1 µV – 100 µV** (extremely small — ~100× smaller than EMG). - **Standard Electrode Placement:** International **10–20 system** (21 scalp electrodes). --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎧 > [!NOTE] > Imagine the brain as a **vast orchestra** with billions of neurons playing together. EEG does not record every instrument (that would require intracranial electrodes, like **ECoG**); instead, it is the **microphone placed in the audience** — it captures the *overall harmony*, the tempo (frequency), and the loudness (amplitude). A relaxed, daydreaming brain produces slow, rhythmic **alpha** music. A startled, alert brain produces fast, irregular **beta** notes. A seizing brain creates a sudden, loud, sustained **spike-and-wave** crescendo. --- ## 1.3 Clinical Cross-Cutting Importance | Modality | Primary Diagnostic Target | Clinical Gold Standard Application | |----------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------| | **EMG** | Neuromuscular Junction & Muscle Integrity | ALS, myasthenia gravis, peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, myopathies | | **EEG** | Cortical & Cerebral Function | Epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, brain death confirmation, anesthesia depth monitoring | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Both EMG and EEG are **quasi-periodic / stochastic biomedical signals** that share the **same measurement pipeline** (electrode → differential amplifier → band-pass filter → ADC → display) but differ fundamentally in **amplitude range, frequency content, and clinical interpretation.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of the EMG Signal — The Motor Unit Anatomy The **Motor Unit (MU)** is the smallest physiological unit that can be voluntarily activated. It consists of: 1. **Alpha Motor Neuron** (cell body in the anterior horn of the spinal cord). 2. **Axon** (motor nerve fiber). 3. **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)** — acetylcholine release site. 4. **Muscle Fibers** (extrafusal, innervated by that single neuron). When the motor neuron fires an **action potential (AP)**, all muscle fibers in its MU depolarize nearly simultaneously, producing a **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**. ### Generation Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic): - **Step 1:** *Cortical voluntary command* travels via the corticospinal tract. - **Step 2:** *Spinal alpha motor neuron* integrates input and fires. - **Step 3:** *Action potential* propagates along the axon (saltatory conduction at Nodes of Ranvier). - **Step 4:** *Acetylcholine (ACh)* is released at the NMJ into the synaptic cleft (~50–100 nm wide). - **Step 5:** *ACh binds to nicotinic receptors* on the motor end-plate → end-plate potential (EPP). - **Step 6:** *Muscle fiber AP* propagates along the sarcolemma and into T-tubules. - **Step 7:** *Excitation–Contraction Coupling* triggers Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. - **Step 8:** *Ca²⁺ binds Troponin-C* → Tropomyosin shifts → Actin–Myosin cross-bridges form → contraction. - **Step 9:** *MUAPs from many MUs* sum algebraically at the recording electrode → **interference pattern EMG**. --- ## 2.2 EMG Signal Acquisition Modes | Mode | Electrode Type | Invasiveness | Pickup Volume | Typical Use | |------|----------------|--------------|---------------|-------------| | **Surface EMG (sEMG)** | Ag/AgCl skin electrodes | Non-invasive | Large (many MUs) | Kinesiology, ergonomics, prosthetics | | **Needle EMG (nEMG)** | Concentric / Monopolar needle | Invasive | Single MU | Clinical neurology diagnostics | | **Single-fiber EMG (SFEMG)** | Fine-needle with small recording surface | Invasive | Single muscle fiber | NMJ disorders (e.g., myasthenia gravis) | | **Fine-wire EMG** | Insulated wire inserted via needle | Minimally invasive | Deep muscles | Dynamic muscle studies | --- ## 2.3 Origin of the EEG Signal — The Cortical Generator Unlike EMG (which is AP-driven), EEG originates from **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** in the **dendrites and soma of cortical pyramidal neurons** (layers III–V of the neocortex). - **Why pyramidal cells?** They are aligned *perpendicular to the cortical surface* (dipole orientation), allowing their summed postsynaptic potentials to summate in extracellular space and propagate to the scalp. - **Volume Conductor Effect:** The skull, cerebrospinal fluid, and scalp act as a low-pass spatial filter, heavily attenuating high-frequency components and smoothing local activity. - **Synchronization:** Rhythmic EEG patterns require large populations of neurons (~10⁴–10⁶) firing in synchrony, governed by thalamocortical loops. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Units | |---|-------------------|---------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Motor Unit Action Potential Duration** | $T_{MUAP} = \dfrac{L_{fiber}}{v_{cond}}$ | $L$ = fiber length; $v$ = conduction velocity (m/s); $T$ in seconds | | 2 | **Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | Typically **2 – 6 m/s** for healthy skeletal muscle | | 3 | **EMG Amplitude (RMS)** | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | Indicates muscle activation level | | 4 | **Mean Frequency** | $f_{mean} = \dfrac{\sum f_{i}\,P_{i}(f)}{\sum P_{i}(f)}$ | Used in fatigue analysis | | 5 | **Median Frequency** | $f_{median}$ where $\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\,df = \int_{f_{med}}^{\infty} P(f)\,df$ | Half the total power lies below this frequency | | 6 | **EEG Waveform RMS Amplitude** | $A_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^{2}(n)}$ | Typical: 10–50 µV (alpha) | | 7 | **EEG Band Power Ratio** | $R_{\alpha/\beta} = \dfrac{\int_{8}^{13} P(f)\,df}{\int_{13}^{30} P(f)\,df}$ | Used in cognitive-state classification | | 8 | **Nernst Equilibrium Potential (muscle)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | $R$ = gas const., $F$ = Faraday const., $T$ = temp; $z$ = valence | | 9 | **Spatial Resolution Limit (EEG)** | $\delta_{spatial} \approx 2 \cdot r_{scalp}$ | ~ 2–3 cm at scalp; cannot resolve single neurons | | 10 | **Skin–Electrode Impedance Requirement** | $Z_{skin-electrode} < 5\text{ k}\Omega$ | Critical for low-noise EMG/EEG acquisition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Constants (Memorize for KTU Exam):** > - sEMG Bandwidth: **20 Hz – 500 Hz** > - EEG Bandwidth: **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** (DC rejection filter at 0.5 Hz) > - Alpha rhythm: **8–13 Hz, 20–60 µV, posterior dominance, eyes-closed** > - EMG Input Impedance of amplifier: **> 100 MΩ** > - CMRR requirement: **> 90 dB** for both modalities --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | EMG Application | EEG Application | |--------|-----------------|-----------------| | **Prosthetics** | Pattern-recognition sEMG for controlling bionic arms | BCI-driven wheelchair control | | **Rehabilitation** | Biofeedback for stroke recovery | Motor imagery BCI for neuro-rehabilitation | | **Sports Science** | Muscle fatigue quantification via median frequency | Cognitive load monitoring in athletes | | **Occupational Health** | Work-related musculoskeletal disorder (WRMSD) screening | Vigilance monitoring in drivers/pilots | | **Clinical Diagnosis** | EMG-guided Botulinum toxin injection | Pre-surgical epilepsy focus localization | --- ## 2.6 The 'Why' and 'How' — Intuitive Logic Behind Key Steps - **Why band-pass filter EMG at 20–500 Hz?** Below 20 Hz, **motion artifacts** and baseline wander dominate; above 500 Hz, the **MUAP energy is negligible**, so we reject noise. - **Why use differential amplification in EEG?** The EEG amplitude is **~50 µV**, while ambient 50/60 Hz interference can be **several volts**. A differential amplifier with high CMRR **rejects common-mode noise** while preserving the differential brain signal. - **Why does EMG amplitude increase with force?** **Motor Unit Recruitment** (Henneman's size principle: smaller MUs recruited first) plus **Rate Coding** (increased firing frequency) together raise the algebraic sum of MUAPs. - **Why is alpha rhythm blocked by eye-opening?** Visual input **desynchronizes** occipital cortical neurons (alpha block / Berger effect), shifting the spectrum toward beta. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: EMG RMS Amplitude and Force Relationship A widely used engineering model relates the RMS amplitude of the surface EMG to the level of voluntary muscular force. **Starting Assumption:** During a sustained isometric contraction, the EMG signal $v(t)$ can be treated as a **zero-mean Gaussian stochastic process** with variance $\sigma^{2}$ (justified by the Central Limit Theorem applied to the large number of independent MUAPs). The RMS amplitude is defined as: $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$ For a zero-mean Gaussian process over a long observation window $T \to \infty$, the time average equals the ensemble average, so: $$V_{RMS} \approx \sigma_{v}$$ Now, the variance $\sigma^{2}_{v}$ is proportional to the **number of active motor units** $N$ and to the **square of the average MUAP amplitude** $\bar{A}$ (assuming independent MUAP contributions): $$\sigma^{2}_{v} \propto N \cdot \bar{A}^{2}$$ Taking the square root: $$V_{RMS} \propto \sqrt{N} \cdot \bar{A}$$ From **Henneman's Size Principle**, as voluntary force $F$ increases: - **Below ~30% MVC:** motor unit *recruitment* dominates (small MUs added). - **Above ~30% MVC:** *rate coding* dominates (existing MUs fire faster). - **Above ~80% MVC:** both recruitment and rate coding contribute. An empirical linearized relationship (valid in the mid-range) is: $$V_{RMS} = k \cdot F + b$$ where $k$ is the gain (typically **0.001–0.01 mV/%MVC**) and $b$ is the resting offset. Therefore: $$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{RMS}}{V_{RMS,max}} \approx \dfrac{F}{F_{max}} \quad \text{(within 20–80% MVC)}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Median Frequency (MF) Shift in Muscle Fatigue The **power spectral density** of the EMG, $P(f)$, is estimated via the **Welch periodogram method**: $$P(f) = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} \left\vert X_{i}(f) \right\vert^{2}$$ where $X_{i}(f)$ is the FFT of the $i$-th windowed segment and $K$ is the number of averaged segments. The **median frequency** $f_{med}$ satisfies: $$\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\,df \;=\; \int_{f_{med}}^{f_{max}} P(f)\,df$$ During sustained isometric contraction, muscle fatigue causes a **progressive compression of the power spectrum toward lower frequencies** because: - **Lactic acid accumulation** lowers sarcolemmal pH → slower $\text{Na}^{+}/\text{K}^{+}$ pump kinetics. - **Conduction velocity** $v$ along muscle fibers decreases. - Since $T_{MUAP} \propto 1/v$, MUAPs **broaden in time**, shifting their Fourier energy toward lower frequencies. A standard empirical fatigue model is: $$f_{med}(t) = f_{med,0} \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$$ where $\alpha$ is the fatigue rate constant (typically **0.005 – 0.02 Hz/s** for sustained MVC). Solving for the **time to reach 50% spectral compression**: $$0.5 \, f_{med,0} = f_{med,0} \cdot e^{-\alpha t_{1/2}}$$ $$e^{-\alpha t_{1/2}} = 0.5$$ $$-\alpha t_{1/2} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\alpha}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: EMG Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) with Differential Amplifier The input signal $v_{sig}$ is a differential voltage from two electrodes, $E_1$ and $E_2$: $$v_{sig} = v_{E1} - v_{E2}$$ The common-mode interference (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup) $v_{CM}$ appears on both electrodes. The differential amplifier output is: $$v_{out} = A_{d}\,(v_{E1} - v_{E2}) + A_{cm}\,v_{CM}$$ where $A_{d}$ is the differential gain and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain. The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is defined as: $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$ Therefore, the effective interference amplitude in the output is: $$v_{CM,out} = \dfrac{A_{d}}{\text{CMRR (linear)}}\cdot v_{CM}$$ The Signal-to-Noise Ratio at the output becomes: $$\text{SNR}_{out} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{d}\cdot v_{sig}}{v_{CM,out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{v_{sig}}{v_{CM}}\right) + \text{CMRR}$$ For EMG with $v_{sig} \approx 100\;\mu\text{V}$ and $v_{CM} \approx 1\;\text{V}$, with CMRR = **100 dB** ($10^{5}$ linear): $$\text{SNR}_{out} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{10^{-4}}{1}\right) + 100 = -80 + 100 = 20\;\text{dB}$$ This demonstrates why **CMRR > 90 dB** is the KTU-mandated minimum for biomedical amplifiers. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EEG vs EMG | Parameter | EEG | EMG | |-----------|-----|-----| | **Source** | Cortical pyramidal neuron postsynaptic potentials | Skeletal muscle fiber action potentials (MUAPs) | | **Amplitude Range** | **1 – 100 µV** (scalp) | **50 µV – 10 mV** | | **Frequency Band** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | **20 – 500 Hz** | | **Primary Rhythms** | δ, θ, α, β, γ | Recruitment patterns, interference patterns | | **Electrode System** | Ag/AgCl scalp cups (10–20 system) | Surface Ag/AgCl or concentric needle | | **CMRR Required** | ≥ 100 dB (high mains interference rejection) | ≥ 90 dB | | **Input Impedance** | > 200 MΩ | > 100 MΩ | | **Skin Preparation** | Abrasion + conductive gel | Light abrasion + gel (surface) | | **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Non-invasive (surface) / Invasive (needle) | | **Clinical Flags** | Spikes, spike-wave complexes, slowing | Fibrillation, fasciculation, myotonic discharges | | **Quantitative Metric** | Band power, coherence, ERP | RMS amplitude, MF, conduction velocity | | **Subject to** | Eye movement, EMG artifact (above 30 Hz) | Crosstalk from adjacent muscles, ECG artifact | --- ## 3.5 KTU-Mandated Numerical: MUAP Duration from Conduction Velocity **Problem:** A motor unit has muscle fibers of effective length $L_{fiber} = 10\;\text{cm} = 0.10\;\text{m}$ and an average conduction velocity $v = 4\;\text{m/s}$. Compute the MUAP duration. **Solution:** The MUAP duration is the time taken by the action potential to traverse the length of the muscle fiber under the recording electrode: $$T_{MUAP} = \dfrac{L_{fiber}}{v_{cond}}$$ $$T_{MUAP} = \dfrac{0.10\;\text{m}}{4\;\text{m/s}}$$ $$T_{MUAP} = 0.025\;\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{T_{MUAP} = 25\;\text{ms}}$$ **Clinical Interpretation:** Normal MUAP durations range from **5 to 15 ms** for small hand muscles up to **20–30 ms** for large proximal muscles like the quadriceps. A duration of 25 ms in a large muscle is within the normal range. --- ## 3.6 KTU-Mandated Numerical: Median Frequency Fatigue Half-Time **Problem:** A sustained isometric contraction causes the median frequency of the EMG to decay exponentially with $\alpha = 0.012\;\text{Hz/s}$. Find the time required for $f_{med}$ to drop to 50% and 25% of its initial value. **Solution:** We have $f_{med}(t) = f_{med,0} \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$. **For 50% drop:** $$0.5 = e^{-\alpha t_{0.5}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\alpha} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.012} \approx 57.8\;\text{s}$$ **For 25% drop:** $$0.25 = e^{-\alpha t_{0.25}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{0.25} = \dfrac{\ln(4)}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1.386}{0.012} \approx 115.5\;\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{t_{0.5} \approx 57.8\;\text{s}, \quad t_{0.25} \approx 115.5\;\text{s}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EMG Acquisition System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Muscle Fiber
MUAP Generation] --> B[Surface or Needle
Electrodes] B --> C[Patient Protection
and Isolation Stage] C --> D[Instrumentation
Differential Amplifier] D --> E[Band-Pass Filter
20 Hz to 500 Hz] E --> F[Notch Filter
50 or 60 Hz Rejection] F --> G[Gain Stage
1000x Amplification] G --> H[Analog to Digital
Converter ADC] H --> I[Digital Signal
Processing Unit] I --> J[Display Monitor
and Storage] I --> K[Feature Extractor
RMS MF Frequency] K --> L[Clinical Diagnosis
Decision Support] J --> M[Clinician
Interpretation] L --> M ``` **Signal Flow Explanation:** - **MUAP Generation** → depolarization of sarcolemma. - **Electrodes** → biopotential pickup (bipolar configuration recommended). - **Protection Stage** → optocoupler / isolation barrier (IEC 60601 patient safety). - **Differential Amplifier** → high CMRR (>90 dB), high input impedance (>100 MΩ). - **Band-Pass Filter** → restricts to the clinically relevant 20–500 Hz EMG band. - **Notch Filter** → rejects 50 Hz (India/Europe) or 60 Hz (USA) mains interference. - **ADC** → sampling rate ≥ **1 kHz** (Nyquist: $2 f_{max}$, with $f_{max} = 500$ Hz). - **DSP Unit** → computes RMS, MF, conduction velocity, interference pattern. - **Clinical Decision Support** → automated detection of fibrillation, fasciculation, myotonic discharges. --- ## 4.2 EEG Acquisition System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Cortical Pyramidal
Postsynaptic Activity] --> P2[Volume Conductor
Skull CSF Scalp] P2 --> P3[Scalp Ag/AgCl
Electrodes 10-20 System] P3 --> P4[Electrode Gel
Impedance < 5 kOhm] P4 --> P5[Differential
Amplifier CMRR > 100 dB] P5 --> P6[High Pass Filter
0.5 Hz DC Block] P6 --> P7[Low Pass Filter
100 Hz Anti Alias] P7 --> P8[Notch Filter
50 Hz Mains Rejection] P8 --> P9[Programmable Gain
10000x Typical] P9 --> P10[16 to 24 bit
ADC Sampled at 250 Hz] P10 --> P11[Digital Signal
Processor] P11 --> P12[Rhythm Classifier
Delta Theta Alpha Beta Gamma] P11 --> P13[Spectral Power Map
Topographic Display] P12 --> P14[Neurologist
Report Generation] P13 --> P14 ``` **Key Subsystems Explained:** - **Volume Conductor Block:** Represents the spatial low-pass filtering imposed by skull (low conductivity ~0.006 S/m) and CSF (higher conductivity ~1.8 S/m). - **10–20 System:** Standardized electrode placement (Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz, A1, A2 — total 21 electrodes). - **Filter Chain Order:** High-pass (0.5 Hz) before LPF (100 Hz) before notch (50 Hz) is mandatory to prevent saturation from DC offset. - **Rhythm Classifier:** Typically uses FFT or wavelet transforms to compute band power. --- ## 4.3 Motor Unit Anatomy — Hierarchical Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Alpha Motor Neuron
Cell Body in Spinal Cord] --> B[Motor Axon
Myelinated Nerve Fiber] B --> C[Neuromuscular
Junction NMJ] C --> D[Muscle Fiber 1] C --> E[Muscle Fiber 2] C --> F[Muscle Fiber N] D --> G[Motor Unit Action
Potential MUAP] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Surface EMG
Electrode Recording] H --> I[Interference Pattern
EMG Signal] ``` **Physiological Insight:** - A single motor neuron can innervate **3–2000+ muscle fibers** depending on muscle precision needs (e.g., **3 fibers** in extraocular muscles for fine eye movement vs. **2000+ fibers** in gastrocnemius for gross force). --- ## 4.4 EEG Rhythm Decision Tree — Clinical Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw EEG Signal] --> B[Band Pass
Filter 0.5 to 100 Hz] B --> C[FFT or Wavelet
Transform] C --> D{Dominant
Frequency} D -->|0.5 to 4 Hz| E[Delta Rhythm
Deep Sleep or Coma] D -->|4 to 8 Hz| F[Theta Rhythm
Drowsiness or Pathology] D -->|8 to 13 Hz| G[Alpha Rhythm
Relaxed Wakefulness] D -->|13 to 30 Hz| H[Beta Rhythm
Active Thinking] D -->|>30 Hz| I[Gamma Rhythm
Cognitive Binding] D -->|Spike Wave| J[Epileptiform
Activity] E --> K[Clinical
Interpretation] F --> K G --> K H --> K I --> K J --> L[Neurologist
Epilepsy Evaluation] K --> L ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical Visual Cue for KTU Exams:** Always remember that **Alpha is posterior-dominant** (occipital lobe, **O1, O2** electrodes) and **blocked by eye opening**, while **Beta is frontal-dominant** (frontal lobe, **F3, F4** electrodes) and accentuated by mental effort. This distinction is a **favourite 7-mark question**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP). What is its typical duration range in a healthy adult skeletal muscle?** **Model Answer:** A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the algebraic summation of the individual action potentials of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded extracellularly as a triphasic or biphasic waveform. **Key Components of MUAP:** | Component | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **Initial positivity** | Wave approaching electrode from depolarization front | | **Main negative deflection** | Depolarization directly under electrode | | **Terminal positivity** | Repolarization phase and wave moving away | **Typical Duration Range:** - **Small distal muscles** (e.g., hand intrinsic muscles): **5 – 10 ms** - **Large proximal muscles** (e.g., quadriceps, deltoid): **10 – 30 ms** **Amplitude Range:** **200 µV – 2 mV** (surface); up to **10 mV** (needle). **[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for waveform components, 1 for duration range with muscle-type distinction]** --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: List the five standard EEG frequency bands and state the dominant behavioral state of each.** **Model Answer:** | Band | Frequency Range | Dominant Behavioral State | |------|-----------------|---------------------------| | **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Deep slow-wave sleep, infancy, pathological coma | | **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Drowsiness, light NREM sleep, emotional stress | | **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, posterior dominance | | **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Active thinking, focused attention, alertness | | **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Higher-order cognition, sensory binding, perception | **[3 Marks: 1 for the frequency table format, 1 for correct ranges, 1 for correct behavioral states]** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **Q: Explain in detail the anatomy of a Motor Unit. With a neat block diagram, describe the complete EMG signal acquisition system. Discuss the role of differential amplification, band-pass filtering, and notch filtering in EMG recording.** **Model Answer Outline:** **(a) Motor Unit Anatomy [7 Marks]:** A **Motor Unit (MU)** is the smallest functional contractile unit of the neuromuscular system. It comprises: 1. **Alpha Motor Neuron:** Cell body located in the anterior horn of the spinal cord gray matter; integrates descending corticospinal and segmental inputs. 2. **Motor Axon:** Myelinated efferent nerve fiber; conducts action potentials at velocities of **50 – 120 m/s** via saltatory conduction at Nodes of Ranvier. 3. **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ):** Synaptic cleft ~50 nm wide; acetylcholine released, binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end-plate → End-Plate Potential (EPP) → Muscle fiber AP. 4. **Extrafusal Muscle Fibers:** Innervated fibers contract simultaneously when their motor neuron fires. **Innervation Ratio:** - **Low ratio** (e.g., 1:3 to 1:10) → **fine control muscles** (extraocular, intrinsic hand). - **High ratio** (e.g., 1:1000 to 1:2000) → **gross force muscles** (gastrocnemius, gluteus). **Henneman's Size Principle:** - Small alpha motor neurons (innervating Type I slow-twitch fibers) are recruited first for low-force tasks. - Larger motor neurons (Type II fast-twitch) are recruited progressively as force demand increases. **(b) EMG Acquisition System [7 Marks]:** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram. Step-by-step explanation: 1. **Electrodes:** Bipolar Ag/AgCl surface electrodes placed parallel to muscle fiber orientation, inter-electrode distance **20 mm**. 2. **Patient Protection:** Isolation amplifier (e.g., ADuM3151) provides **> 5000 V isolation** between patient and mains. 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD620):** Gain 10–100×, CMRR **> 100 dB** at 50 Hz, input impedance **> 1 GΩ**. 4. **Band-Pass Filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz):** - High-pass at 20 Hz eliminates DC offset, baseline wander, and motion artifacts. - Low-pass at 500 Hz removes high-frequency thermal noise and radio-frequency interference. - Implemented using **2nd-order Butterworth** topology for maximally flat pass-band. 5. **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Twin-T or active notch with **Q-factor > 30**, attenuation **> 40 dB** at 50 Hz, minimal impact at 49 Hz and 51 Hz (preserves EMG signal energy). 6. **Gain Stage:** Total system gain of **1000×** to bring µV-level EMG to ADC input range (~1–5 V). 7. **ADC:** **16-bit resolution minimum**, sampling rate **≥ 1 kHz** (Nyquist criterion). 8. **Display:** Real-time waveform on oscilloscope/LCD; RMS bar for biofeedback. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the EEG acquisition system following the 10–20 international electrode placement system. Discuss the origin of EEG signals, the five canonical rhythms, and the clinical applications of EEG.** **Model Answer Outline:** **(a) Origin of EEG Signals and 10–20 System [7 Marks]:** **Origin:** - EEG is generated by **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs / IPSPs)** in the apical dendrites of **cortical pyramidal neurons** (cortical layers III–V). - These neurons are aligned **perpendicular to the cortical surface**, producing dipoles that summate in extracellular space. - **Volume conductor theory:** The skull (low conductivity), CSF (high conductivity), and scalp distort and attenuate the signal, requiring averaging over **~10⁴ – 10⁶ neurons** to produce a scalp-detectable signal. **10–20 Electrode Placement System:** - Standardized by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)**. - Electrode positions are at **10% and 20%** intervals along the scalp surface between four anatomical landmarks: **Nasion, Inion, and pre-auricular points (left & right)**. - **21 scalp electrodes** in standard clinical setup: **Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, O2, A1, A2** (A1/A2 are reference ear electrodes). **(b) Block Diagram and Clinical Applications [7 Marks]:** Refer to Section 4.2 block diagram. **Key Specifications:** - Amplifier CMRR: **> 100 dB** - High-pass filter: **0.5 Hz** (blocks DC) - Low-pass filter: **70 – 100 Hz** (anti-aliasing) - Notch filter: **50 Hz** (mains) - ADC sampling rate: **≥ 250 Hz** (commonly 256 Hz or 512 Hz) - ADC resolution: **16 – 24 bits** **Five Canonical Rhythms:** | Band | Frequency | Amplitude | Location | State | |------|-----------|-----------|----------|-------| | Delta | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Generalized | Deep sleep, coma | | Theta | 4 – 8 Hz | < 30 µV | Temporal | Drowsiness | | Alpha | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 60 µV | Occipital | Relaxed awake | | Beta | 13 – 30 Hz | < 20 µV | Frontal | Active thinking | | Gamma | > 30 Hz | < 10 µV | Localized | Cognition | **Clinical Applications:** 1. **Epilepsy Diagnosis:** Detection of spikes, sharp waves, spike-and-wave complexes. 2. **Sleep Disorders:** Polysomnography with EEG + EOG + EMG. 3. **Brain Death Confirmation:** Electrocerebral silence (flatline EEG < 2 µV). 4. **Anesthesia Depth Monitoring:** Bispectral Index (BIS) derived from EEG. 5. **BCI Applications:** P300 spellers, motor imagery classification. 6. **Encephalopathies:** Hepatic, uremic, hypoxic-ischemic patterns. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing EEG and EMG amplitudes:** Writing "EEG amplitude is in mV range" — **WRONG**. EEG is **µV**, EMG is **mV**. Marks deducted for unit errors. > 2. **Skipping the 10–20 system:** KTU examiners **expect** the electrode naming convention (Fp1, F3, C3, etc.) when describing EEG setup. Naming only "scalp electrodes" loses **2 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting filter cutoffs:** Always state **specific Hz values** for filters. "Band-pass filter" without numbers is incomplete. > 4. **No mention of CMRR:** Differential amplification is meaningless without specifying **CMRR > 90 dB (EMG) or > 100 dB (EEG)**. > 5. **Confusing muscle contraction with EEG:** Writing that EMG originates from "brain signals" — **fatal error**. EMG = muscle, EEG = brain. > 6. **Ignoring patient safety isolation:** Modern acquisition must include **isolation stage** for IEC 60601 compliance. Missing this loses a mark. > 7. **No clinical correlation:** Always link technical concepts to **clinical examples** (e.g., alpha rhythm blocked by eye opening = Berger effect; fibrillation potential = denervated muscle). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ ### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **EMG** measures **muscle fiber action potentials (MUAPs)**; **EEG** measures **cortical postsynaptic potentials**. - [ ] **EMG amplitude:** **50 µV – 10 mV**; **EEG amplitude:** **1 – 100 µV**. - [ ] **EMG bandwidth:** **20 – 500 Hz**; **EEG bandwidth:** **0.5 – 100 Hz**. - [ ] **Motor Unit =** Alpha motor neuron + axon + NMJ + all extrafusal muscle fibers it innervates. - [ ] **Henneman's Size Principle:** Small motor units recruited first (Type I), large units added progressively (Type II). - [ ] **MUAP duration** = $L_{fiber} \div v_{cond}$ (typical conduction velocity **2 – 6 m/s**). - [ ] **EMG median frequency** *decreases* during fatigue due to **reduced conduction velocity**. - [ ] **Five EEG rhythms:** δ (0.5–4), θ (4–8), α (8–13), β (13–30), γ (>30) Hz. - [ ] **Alpha rhythm:** posterior (occipital) dominant, eyes-closed, blocked by visual input (**Berger effect**). - [ ] **Beta rhythm:** frontal dominant, accentuated by mental activity. - [ ] **Delta rhythm:** pathological in awake adults; normal in deep sleep and infants. - [ ] **10–20 system:** 21 electrodes placed at 10%–20% intervals from nasion–inion and pre-auricular landmarks. - [ ] **Amplifier requirements:** CMRR **> 90 dB** (EMG) / **> 100 dB** (EEG); input impedance **> 100 MΩ**. - [ ] **Skin-electrode impedance** must be **< 5 kΩ** for noise-free acquisition. - [ ] **EMG electrodes:** surface Ag/AgCl (non-invasive) or concentric needle (clinical single-MU). - [ ] **Clinical uses — EMG:** ALS, myasthenia gravis, peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, myopathies. - [ ] **Clinical uses — EEG:** epilepsy, sleep disorders, brain death, anesthesia depth (BIS), BCI. - [ ] **Fibrillation potential** in EMG = denervated muscle fiber (spontaneous, single-fiber discharge). - [ ] **Fasciculation** = visible muscle twitch from spontaneous MU discharge (benign or ALS sign). - [ ] **Spike-and-wave complex** in EEG = hallmark of absence (petit mal) epilepsy. - [ ] **Patient safety:** Isolation barrier mandatory (IEC 60601); leakage current < 10 µA (Type CF). - [ ] **Sampling rate (KTU favourite):** EMG **≥ 1 kHz**; EEG **≥ 250 Hz** (Nyquist). - [ ] **ADC resolution:** **≥ 16 bits** for both modalities. ### 📌 One-Sentence Mnemonics - **"EMG is M-Volts, EEG is µ-Volts"** — never confuse the amplitude scales. - **"Alpha at Occipital, Beta at Frontal"** — location memorization. - **"Smaller First, Bigger Later"** — Henneman's size principle in recruitment. - **"Filter before Amplify"** — standard biomedical signal chain order.

topics.title: Characteristics of EMG Signal and Clinical Applications of EMG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of EMG Signal and Clinical Applications of EMG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Electromyography (EMG)

**Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectrical technique concerned with the detection, recording, and analysis of the electrical activity generated by skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or evoked contraction. The resulting time-varying voltage waveform, called the **Electromyogram (EMG signal)**, is the spatial and temporal summation of the **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** of all active motor units within the pickup volume of the recording electrodes.

A **Motor Unit (MU)** is the smallest functional contractile unit of a muscle, comprising:
- One **alpha motor neuron** (in the spinal cord / brainstem)
- Its **axon** (peripheral nerve)
- The **neuromuscular junctions** (synapses)
- All the **muscle fibers** innervated by that single axon (typically 5–2000 fibers depending on muscle function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The EMG signal is *not* the action potential of a single fiber. It is a quasi-random, stochastic superposition of MUAP trains firing asynchronously across many motor units — a critical concept frequently asked in 2-mark definitions.

**Electromyograph** = the complete instrumentation system (electrodes + amplifier + filter + display/recorder) that produces the EMG.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra Recording"

Imagine a large orchestra playing a symphony in a concert hall:

| Orchestra Element | EMG Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Each musician playing their instrument | Each motor unit firing its MUAP train |
| The microphone on the stage | The recording electrode |
| The recorded audio track on your phone | The raw EMG waveform |
| A single violinist's note isolated | A single MUAP (seen in needle EMG) |
| Full orchestra chord (forte vs. piano) | Strong vs. weak muscle contraction (high vs. low amplitude EMG) |
| The hall's acoustics filtering highs/lows | Skin, fat, and tissue acting as a low-pass filter |

When the conductor (the **central nervous system**) cues a *forte* (loud) passage, more violinists (motor units) play at higher pitches (firing rates) — your microphone picks up a louder, more complex waveform. When the orchestra tires near the end of a long piece, the *timbre* shifts (the high-frequency content drops) — this is exactly what happens in **muscle fatigue**, where the median frequency of the EMG power spectrum shifts downward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The EMG signal is therefore a *stochastic*, *non-stationary*, *zero-mean*, *quasi-random* process whose statistical properties (amplitude, spectral content) carry diagnostic information about the neuromuscular system.

---

## 1.3 Signal Acquisition Modalities — Two Principal Variants

### A. Surface EMG (SEMG)
- **Electrodes:** Skin-surface Ag/AgCl electrodes (typically bipolar configuration).
- **Pickup Volume:** Large — captures activity from many superficial motor units simultaneously.
- **Invasiveness:** Non-invasive, painless, repeatable.
- **Bandwidth:** Limited (typically filtered to **20–500 Hz**).
- **Use case:** Kinesiology, ergonomics, sports science, prosthetics, biofeedback.

### B. Intramuscular / Needle EMG (NEMG)
- **Electrodes:** Concentric needle, monopolar needle, or fine-wire electrodes inserted into the muscle belly.
- **Pickup Volume:** Small (a few mm³) — can resolve individual MUAPs.
- **Invasiveness:** Invasive, mildly uncomfortable, single-use sterilized needles.
- **Bandwidth:** Wider (typically **2 Hz – 10 kHz**).
- **Use case:** Clinical diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Compare surface EMG and needle EMG."* — Memorize the trade-off: **selectivity (spatial resolution) vs. invasiveness and bandwidth**. Surface = global, painless, low frequency content; Needle = focal, uncomfortable, full bandwidth.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Electrical Metrics of the EMG Signal

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Peak-to-peak amplitude (MUAP) | **100 – 2000** | µV |
| Peak-to-peak amplitude (interference EMG) | **0 – 10** | mV |
| Dominant frequency band | **20 – 500** | Hz |
| Energy concentration | **50 – 150** | Hz |
| MUAP duration | **2 – 15** | ms |
| MUAP rise time | **100 – 200** | µs |
| Firing rate of a single motor unit | **6 – 30** | Hz |
| Voluntary activation upper limit | **~60 – 100** | Hz |
| Input impedance of skin-electrode interface | **10 – 50** | kΩ |
| Recommended amplifier CMRR | **> 100** | dB |
| Skin–electrode contact impedance target | **< 5** | kΩ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **interference pattern** is what is observed during strong voluntary contraction: individual MUAPs become indistinguishable because of dense temporal overlap, producing a noise-like, zero-mean waveform whose amplitude envelope tracks the level of muscular effort.

---

## 1.5 Noise and Artifact Sources in EMG

- **Powerline interference** (50/60 Hz pickup) — dominant noise source.
- **Motion artifacts** — electrode-cable movement causing baseline wander (< 20 Hz).
- **ECG crosstalk** — cardiac electrical activity contaminating thoracic/lumbar EMG recordings.
- **Crosstalk from adjacent muscles** — especially problematic in SEMG.
- **Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise** of electrode–tissue interface.
- **Inherent electronic noise** of the preamplifier (FET-input op-amps essential).

> [!WARNING]
> The **50 Hz notch filter** is mandatory in EMG acquisition chains in India (KTU instrumentation context) — examiners frequently ask *"Why is a notch filter used?"* because of the dedicated 50 Hz mains frequency. The answer must include the fact that biological signals have negligible energy at 50 Hz, so attenuation there is lossless.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the EMG Signal — Step-by-Step Generation

1. **Central Command:** The motor cortex issues a descending voluntary command through the corticospinal tract.
2. **Alpha Motor Neuron Firing:** The anterior horn cell of the spinal cord depolarizes past threshold and fires an **action potential**.
3. **Axonal Propagation:** The AP travels down the motor axon at 50–80 m/s.
4. **Neuromuscular Junction:** Acetylcholine is released, depolarizing the muscle fiber end-plate.
5. **Sarcolemmal Depolarization:** A muscle-fiber AP propagates in both directions along the fiber from the motor end-plate.
6. **Calcium Release & Contraction:** The fiber shortens (excitation–contraction coupling).
7. **MUAP Generation:** The sum of the depolarization waves of all fibers in a motor unit produces one **MUAP**.
8. **EMG Recording:** The volume-conducted superposition of *all* active MUAPs is what the electrodes sense.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept (highly tested):** The motor unit obeys the **"size principle" (Henneman, 1965)** — small (low-threshold, Type I, slow oxidative) motor units are recruited first, with progressively larger (high-threshold, Type II, fast glycolytic) units joining as force demand increases. This *orderly recruitment* is why EMG amplitude rises smoothly with force.

---

## 2.2 Amplitude Characteristics of EMG

### 2.2.1 Amplitude vs. Force Relationship
The **RMS amplitude** of the surface EMG is approximately linearly related to muscle force during isometric contractions in the low-to-moderate force range. At high forces, saturation occurs due to cancellation of overlapping positive/negative phases.

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

### 2.2.2 Integrated EMG (IEMG)
The integral of the *rectified* signal over a fixed epoch; correlates with the number of active motor units and their firing rates.

$$IEMG = \int_{0}^{T} \vert v(t) \vert\, dt$$

### 2.2.3 Average Rectified Value (ARV)
The mean of the absolute value of the EMG; a cheaper alternative to RMS for amplitude tracking.

$$ARV = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \vert v(t) \vert\, dt$$

### 2.2.4 Mean Absolute Value (MAV)
Equivalent to ARV; widely used in prosthetic pattern-recognition controllers.

$$MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert v_i \vert$$

### 2.2.5 Zero-Crossing Rate (ZC)
Number of times the signal crosses zero per unit time — a crude frequency-domain indicator.

$$ZC = \frac{1}{2T}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left[ \mathrm{sgn}(v_i \cdot v_{i+1}) - \mathrm{sgn}(v_i \cdot v_{i+1}) \right]$$

### 2.2.6 Waveform Length (WL)
Cumulative absolute change in signal — sensitive to both amplitude and frequency.

$$WL = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert v_{i+1} - v_i \vert$$

---

## 2.3 Frequency-Domain Characteristics

The Power Spectral Density (PSD) of the EMG is the Fourier transform of its autocorrelation. In healthy muscle, the PSD is concentrated between **20 Hz and 500 Hz** with a peak (median frequency) around **70–130 Hz**.

### 2.3.1 Mean Frequency
$$\bar{f} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} f \cdot P(f)\, df}{\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f)\, df}$$

### 2.3.2 Median Frequency (MDF)
The frequency that splits the PSD into two equal-energy halves. **MDF is the gold-standard fatigue indicator** because it is less sensitive to noise than mean frequency.

$$\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f)\, df$$

### 2.3.3 Fatigue-Induced Spectral Compression
During sustained contraction, the **MDF decreases monotonically** (typically 0.5–1.5% per second of contraction) and the spectrum shifts toward lower frequencies. This is attributed to:
- Slowing of muscle fiber conduction velocity (MFCV: 3–6 m/s at rest → drops 10–20% during fatigue).
- Increased synchronization of motor unit firing.
- Accumulation of metabolic by-products (H⁺, Pi, K⁺).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Typical Range / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RMS Amplitude | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | V | 0–5 mV (SEMG) |
| 2 | Average Rectified Value | $ARV = \dfrac{1}{T}\int \vert v(t)\vert\,dt$ | V | Prosthetic control |
| 3 | Integrated EMG | $IEMG = \int \vert v(t)\vert\,dt$ | V·s | Effort quantification |
| 4 | Mean Frequency | $\bar f = \dfrac{\int f P(f)\,df}{\int P(f)\,df}$ | Hz | 70–130 Hz (healthy) |
| 5 | Median Frequency | $\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\,df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int P(f)\,df$ | Hz | Fatigue tracker |
| 6 | MUAP Duration | Empirical | ms | 2 – 15 ms |
| 7 | Firing Rate | $f_{MU} = \dfrac{1}{T_{isi}}$ | Hz | 6 – 30 Hz |
| 8 | Conduction Velocity | $MFCV = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | 3 – 6 m/s |
| 9 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | dB | > 30 dB clinical |
| 10 | Skin Impedance Target | — | kΩ | < 5 kΩ |
| 11 | Amplifier CMRR | — | dB | > 100 dB |
| 12 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | ≥ 1000 Hz EMG |
| 13 | Bandwidth | — | Hz | 20 – 500 Hz (SEMG) |
| 14 | Notch Filter | At $f_{mains}$ | Hz | 50 Hz (India) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-syntax safeguard:** Every absolute-value expression in the table above uses the **vertical bar directly** without column-pipe ambiguity, because the LaTeX-formatted cells are inside markdown `$$` blocks, not raw table cells. When you must use `|x|` *inside* a markdown table row, switch to `\vert x \vert` to avoid parser breakage.

---

## 2.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for EMG

For diagnostic-grade EMG, the **input-referred noise** of the front-end preamplifier must be below **5 µV RMS** to resolve the smallest clinically relevant MUAPs.

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal,RMS}}{V_{noise,RMS}}\right)$$

For a typical SEMG with $V_{signal} = 1$ mV and $V_{noise} = 5$ µV, the SNR = **46 dB**, which is acceptable for kinesiologic work.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | EMG Application |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Neurology** | Diagnose ALS, peripheral neuropathy, myasthenia gravis, radiculopathy |
| **Rehabilitation Engineering** | Biofeedback therapy post-stroke, gait retraining |
| **Prosthetics** | Pattern-recognition controllers (SVM, LDA) classify intended hand gestures |
| **Sports Science** | Muscle activation timing, fatigue monitoring, training load balancing |
| **Ergonomics** | Risk assessment of work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WRMSD) |
| **BCI** | Brain–computer interfaces (combined with EEG) for severely paralyzed patients |
| **Robotics** | Human–robot collaborative control, exoskeleton intent decoding |
| **Forensic Medicine** | Determination of time since death (cessation of electrical activity = cadaveric) |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Answers:** Always end a clinical question with a sentence linking the engineering parameter to a clinical outcome — e.g., *"A reduction of MDF from 100 Hz to 70 Hz within 30 s of sustained contraction is a quantitative indicator of muscular fatigue, used by sports physiologists to prescribe safe training loads."*

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — RMS Amplitude from First Principles

**Goal:** Show that $V_{RMS}$ of EMG correlates with muscle force and that its derivation arises from the equivalence of electrical power dissipation.

**Step 1 — Power delivered to a load resistor $R$ by an instantaneous voltage $v(t)$:**

$$p(t) = \frac{v^{2}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Average power over an observation window $T$:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{v^{2}(t)}{R}\, dt = \frac{V_{RMS}^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions to define the RMS value:**

$$\frac{V_{RMS}^{2}}{R} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{v^{2}(t)}{R}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{RMS}$:**

$$V_{RMS}^{2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Practical discrete-time realization** (for a sampled EMG with $N$ samples $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_N$ acquired at $f_s$):

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{i}^{2}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the discrete equivalent of the continuous integral; the window length $T = N/f_s$ cancels out in the algebra. **Why it matters clinically:** Because RMS aggregates the entire signal power, it is **insensitive to DC offset** (which EMG, being AC-coupled, has none of anyway) and to the *sign* of fluctuations — both crucial when the signal is zero-mean and stochastic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Median Frequency from the Power Spectral Density

**Goal:** Show that $f_{med}$ partitions the spectrum into two equal-energy halves and prove why it is robust to noise.

**Step 1 — Compute the PSD $P(f)$ of the EMG by Welch's method** (windowed periodogram averaging).

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\left\vert \frac{1}{U}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} v_{k}(n)\,w(n)\,e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2}$$

where $K$ is the number of segments, $L$ is the segment length, $w(n)$ is the window, and $U$ is the window-energy normalization factor.

**Step 2 — Total signal power** is the integral of the PSD over all frequencies (Parseval's theorem):

$$P_{total} = \int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} P(f)\, df$$

**Step 3 — Define $f_{med}$** as the frequency at which the *cumulative* power equals half the total:

$$\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2} P_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Re-arrange using the equality from Step 2:**

$$\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} P(f)\, df$$

$$\boxed{\,\int_{0}^{f_{med}} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} P(f)\, df\,}$$

**Step 5 — Robustness proof (sketch):** A spurious narrow-band noise spike at high frequency inflates the *mean* frequency but contributes only a small fraction of total area — the median is therefore stable. This is the mathematical justification for using $f_{med}$ in fatigue studies rather than $\bar f$.

> **Conversion logic:** Cumulative-area-up-to-$f_{med}$ is exactly half the total area — this is the area-under-the-curve bisection property of the median. The derivative of the cumulative integral (with respect to $f$) is the PSD itself, so $P(f_{med})$ does *not* need to be at a peak; it merely sits at the spectrum's *energy bisector*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV) from Surface EMG

**Goal:** Derive the MFCV from the time delay between MUAPs recorded at two electrodes spaced a known distance along the muscle fiber direction.

**Step 1 — Two surface electrodes $E_1$ and $E_2$ are placed at a known inter-electrode distance $d$ along the muscle fiber direction.**

**Step 2 — A single MUAP propagates under both electrodes, producing a similar waveform at each, but delayed by a time $\Delta t$.**

**Step 3 — The conduction velocity is the distance travelled per unit time:**

$$\boxed{\,MFCV = \frac{d}{\Delta t}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Typical values:** $d = 10$ mm, $\Delta t = 2$ ms → $MFCV = 5$ m/s (within the textbook 3–6 m/s range).

**Step 5 — Clinical correlation:** A **drop in MFCV** below 3 m/s indicates demyelination, myopathy, or fatigue; an **elevation** above 6 m/s may suggest certain myotonic disorders or hyperthyroid myopathy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Surface vs. Needle EMG

| Parameter | Surface EMG (SEMG) | Needle EMG (NEMG) |
|---|---|---|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (skin puncture) |
| Electrode | Ag/AgCl disc / array | Concentric / monopolar needle |
| Pickup volume | Large (~cm²) | Small (~mm³) |
| MUAPs resolved | No (interference pattern) | Yes (single MUAPs) |
| Bandwidth | 20 – 500 Hz | 2 Hz – 10 kHz |
| Amplitude range | 0 – 5 mV | 100 µV – 2 mV |
| Patient comfort | High | Low |
| Repeatability | Excellent | Limited (single-use sterile) |
| Cross-talk | High (adjacent muscles) | Low |
| Primary use | Kinesiology, prosthetics | Clinical diagnosis |
| Cost | Low | High (disposables + expertise) |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Requires neurologist |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 1-second epoch of surface EMG is sampled at 1000 Hz. The first 5 sample values (in mV) are: $v_1 = 0.12$, $v_2 = -0.34$, $v_3 = 0.55$, $v_4 = -0.21$, $v_5 = 0.08$. Compute the ARV, MAV, and zero-crossing count (for the entire window if it oscillates with period 0.02 s, i.e., 50 zero-crossings/s).

**Step 1 — Absolute values of the 5 samples:**

$$\vert v_1 \vert = 0.12,\ \vert v_2 \vert = 0.34,\ \vert v_3 \vert = 0.55,\ \vert v_4 \vert = 0.21,\ \vert v_5 \vert = 0.08$$

**Step 2 — Sum of absolute values:**

$$\sum \vert v_i \vert = 0.12 + 0.34 + 0.55 + 0.21 + 0.08 = 1.30\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — MAV (Mean Absolute Value):**

$$MAV = \frac{1.30}{5} = 0.26\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — ARV = MAV (over the same window):** $\,ARV = 0.26$ mV.

**Step 5 — Zero-crossing rate:** Given a 1-second epoch with **50 zero-crossings**:

$$ZC = \frac{50}{1\ \text{s}} = 50\ \text{crossings/s} = 50\ \text{Hz}$$

> **Conversion logic:** A 50 Hz ZC suggests dominant spectral content near 50 Hz, which for EMG is the *powerline interference band*, signalling that a notch filter is urgently required before clinical interpretation.

---

## 3.6 Pattern-Recognition Feature Set for Prosthetic Control (Analytical View)

Modern myoelectric prostheses use a sliding-window feature vector computed every ~150–250 ms:

$$F_{k} = \left[ MAV,\ WL,\ ZC,\ SSC,\ RMS,\ WAMP \right]^{T}$$

| Feature | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| MAV | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert v_i\vert$ | Effort level |
| WL | $\sum \vert v_{i+1} - v_i\vert$ | Frequency + amplitude |
| ZC | $\#\ \text{sign changes}$ | Frequency |
| SSC | $\#\ \text{slope-sign changes}$ | Frequency detail |
| RMS | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum v_i^2}$ | Signal power |
| WAMP | $\#\ \vert v_{i+1} - v_i\vert > \epsilon$ | Threshold crossings |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner insight:** When asked *"How is EMG used in prosthetics?"*, mention **feature extraction → classifier (LDA / SVM / ANN) → control signal to motor**. This single sentence in a 14-mark answer is worth at least 2–3 marks.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EMG Signal Generation Chain — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Nervous System - Motor Cortex] --> B[Descending Corticospinal Tract]
    B --> C[Alpha Motor Neuron in Spinal Cord]
    C --> D[Axonal Action Potential Propagation]
    D --> E[Neuromuscular Junction - ACh Release]
    E --> F[Muscle Fiber Depolarization]
    F --> G[Motor Unit Action Potential - MUAP]
    G --> H[Volume Conducted Superposition through Tissue]
    H --> I[Recording Electrode - Surface or Needle]
    I --> J[Differential Preamplifier - High CMRR]
    J --> K[Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    K --> L[50 Hz Notch Filter - Mains Rejection]
    L --> M[ADC - Sampling at 1 kHz minimum]
    M --> N[Digital Signal Processing - Feature Extraction]
    N --> O[Display, Storage, or Control Output]
```

> **Reading guide:** The chain moves from physiological origin (left) to engineering output (right). Each block has a clearly defined function — examiners love to test whether students can identify *which stage* the artifact/noise is introduced.

---

## 4.2 EMG Signal Morphology — Time-Domain Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resting Muscle - Baseline Noise < 5 uV] --> B[Insertion Activity - Brief Burst at Needle Insertion]
    B --> C[Single MU Recruitment - Isolated MUAPs Visible]
    C --> D[Weak Voluntary Contraction - Few MUAPs, Asynchronous]
    D --> E[Moderate Contraction - Increased MUAP Density]
    E --> F[Strong Contraction - Interference Pattern MUAPs Overlap Fully]
    F --> G[Fatigue - Amplitude maintained, Frequency Drops]
```

> **Reading guide:** The amplitude of the waveform envelope grows from `A` to `F`, while the *sharpness* (high-frequency content) declines from `F` to `G`. This is the visual signature of Henneman's recruitment + fatigue.

---

## 4.3 EMG Electrode Placement & Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Muscle Fiber Direction Longitudinal Axis] --> B[Active Electrode E1 Placed Mid Belly]
    A --> C[Active Electrode E2 Placed 2 to 3 cm Distal]
    A --> D[Reference Electrode Ref Placed Over Bony Landmark or Inactive Tissue]
    B --> E[Differential Amplifier Positive Input]
    C --> F[Differential Amplifier Negative Input]
    D --> G[Differential Amplifier Ground Reference]
    E --> H[High CMRR Instrumentation Amplifier Output]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

> **Reading guide:** The bipolar (E1–E2) configuration with E1 upstream and E2 downstream along the fiber direction is the **SENIAM-recommended** placement that maximises MFCV estimation accuracy.

---

## 4.4 Clinical Decision Flowchart — Diagnostic EMG Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Presents with Weakness or Numbness] --> B{Neurological Examination}
    B --> C[Insertional Activity Assessed]
    C --> D{Increased or Decreased?}
    D -->|Increased| E[Myotonia or Inflammatory Myopathy]
    D -->|Decreased| F[End-stage Muscle or Severe Denervation]
    C --> G[Spontaneous Activity Check]
    G --> H{Fibrillations or Positive Sharp Waves?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Denervation - Neurogenic Lesion]
    H -->|No| J[Voluntary MUAP Analysis]
    J --> K{MUAP Morphology}
    K -->|Short, Small, Polyphasic| L[Myopathic Pattern]
    K -->|Long, Large, Polyphasic| M[Neuropathic Pattern]
    L --> N[Diagnosis: Muscular Dystrophy or Inflammatory Myopathy]
    M --> O[Diagnosis: Motor Neuron Disease or Peripheral Neuropathy]
```

> **Reading guide:** A KTU question on *"how EMG is used in clinical diagnosis"* can be answered in 7–8 lines by walking the examiner through this decision tree.

---

## 4.5 EMG-Based Prosthetic Hand Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Limb Muscle EMG Electrodes x4 or x8] --> B[Multichannel Differential Amplifier Array]
    B --> C[Analog Bandpass 20 to 500 Hz]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Conversion at 1 kHz]
    D --> E[Sliding Window Feature Extraction every 200 ms]
    E --> F[Feature Vector MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC]
    F --> G[Trained Classifier LDA or SVM or ANN]
    G --> H{Recognized Gesture Class}
    H -->|Hand Open| I1[Servomotor - Open Hand Command]
    H -->|Hand Close| I2[Servomotor - Close Hand Command]
    H -->|Wrist Rotate| I3[Servomotor - Rotation Command]
    H -->|Pinch Grip| I4[Servomotor - Pinch Command]
```

> **Reading guide:** The classifier is the *intelligence core* — this is what makes a "smart" myoelectric prosthesis different from a body-powered hook.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Electromyography. List any four characteristic features of the EMG signal.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
Electromyography (EMG) is the technique of recording the electrical activity of skeletal muscles during voluntary or stimulated contraction. The EMG signal is the superposition of Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs) of the active motor units detected within the electrode pickup volume. Four characteristic features: (1) **amplitude** 0–10 mV for surface EMG, (2) **frequency band** 20 Hz – 500 Hz, (3) **stochastic and zero-mean** in nature, (4) **non-stationary** with statistics that depend on contraction level and fatigue state.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between surface EMG and needle EMG.**

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**
| Aspect | Surface EMG | Needle EMG |
|---|---|---|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (skin puncture) |
| Electrode type | Ag/AgCl skin electrodes | Concentric / monopolar needle |
| Pickup volume | Large (many motor units) | Small (single motor units) |
| Bandwidth | 20 – 500 Hz | 2 Hz – 10 kHz |
| Clinical use | Kinesiology, sports, biofeedback | Neurological diagnosis |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**"With neat block diagrams, explain the generation, characteristics, and recording of the EMG signal. Discuss the key parameters used for EMG signal analysis in both time and frequency domains."**

#### (a) Generation and Recording of EMG Signal (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Physiological Generation (3 marks):**
- The central nervous system issues a descending voluntary command via the corticospinal tract.
- Alpha motor neurons in the anterior horn of the spinal cord fire action potentials.
- The action potential travels down the motor axon (50–80 m/s) to the neuromuscular junction.
- Acetylcholine is released, depolarizing the motor end-plate of muscle fibers.
- A Muscle Fiber Action Potential (MFAP) propagates along the sarcolemma in both directions.
- A Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) is the spatial-temporal sum of MFAPs of all fibers in a single motor unit.
- The EMG signal is the volume-conducted superposition of *all* active MUAPs.

**2. Recording Chain (4 marks):**
- **Electrodes:** Surface (Ag/AgCl) or needle (concentric). Bipolar configuration recommended.
- **Preamplifier:** Instrumentation amplifier with CMRR > 100 dB, input impedance > 10¹⁰ Ω, gain ~1000.
- **Filters:** High-pass (20 Hz) to remove DC offset and motion artifact; Low-pass (500 Hz) to remove high-frequency noise; **50 Hz notch** to remove powerline interference.
- **ADC:** Sampling at $\geq 1$ kHz (Nyquist criterion for 500 Hz max frequency).
- **Display/Storage:** Oscilloscope for clinical inspection, PC/DSP for analysis.

#### (b) Time and Frequency Domain Parameters (7 marks)

**1. Time-Domain Parameters (3.5 marks):**
- **RMS amplitude** $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int v^2(t)\,dt}$ — correlates with force.
- **Average Rectified Value (ARV)** — mean of absolute value.
- **Integrated EMG (IEMG)** — area under rectified signal.
- **Zero-Crossing Rate (ZC)** — sign-change count per second.
- **Waveform Length (WL)** — cumulative absolute sample-to-sample change.
- **Willison Amplitude (WAMP)** — count of threshold-crossings of successive differences.

**2. Frequency-Domain Parameters (3.5 marks):**
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** via Welch's method.
- **Mean Frequency** $\bar f = \frac{\int f P(f)\,df}{\int P(f)\,df}$ — sensitive to noise.
- **Median Frequency $f_{med}$** — spectral bisector, gold-standard fatigue indicator.
- **Spectral compression during fatigue** — MDF drops by 0.5–1.5%/s.
- **Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV)** — derived from two-channel delay.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**"Discuss in detail the clinical applications of EMG. Explain how EMG is used in (i) diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders, (ii) rehabilitation and biofeedback, and (iii) prosthetic control."**

#### (a) Diagnosis of Neuromuscular Disorders (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Neuropathic Disorders (Motor Neuron / Peripheral Nerve):**
- **ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis):** EMG shows widespread denervation — fibrillations, positive sharp waves, fasciculations, and chronic neurogenic motor unit changes (large, long, polyphasic MUAPs) in ≥ 3 body regions.
- **Peripheral Neuropathy:** Reduced recruitment, polyphasic MUAPs; nerve conduction studies (NCS) complement EMG.
- **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome:** EMG of abductor pollicis brevis shows chronic neurogenic changes; median nerve conduction velocity is delayed.
- **Radiculopathy:** EMG of paraspinal and limb muscles localizes the level of nerve root compression.

**2. Myopathic Disorders (Muscle Fiber):**
- **Muscular Dystrophy:** Short-duration, small-amplitude, polyphasic MUAPs with early recruitment.
- **Inflammatory Myopathies (Polymyositis):** Fibrillations, positive sharp waves, myopathic MUAPs.
- **Myotonia:** Persistent insertional activity, "dive-bomber" discharge pattern.

**3. Neuromuscular Junction Disorders:**
- **Myasthenia Gravis:** Decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation (> 10% drop in CMAP amplitude).
- Single-fiber EMG (SFEMG) — gold standard — shows increased jitter.

#### (b) Rehabilitation, Biofeedback, and Prosthetic Control (7 marks)

**1. Rehabilitation and Biofeedback (3 marks):**
- Post-stroke: SEMG biofeedback helps patients re-learn to recruit paretic muscles.
- Gait retraining: SEMG of quadriceps, hamstrings, gastrocnemius guides physical therapy.
- Chronic pain management: SEMG of upper trapezius reduces tension-type headaches.
- Spinal cord injury: SEMG-guided functional electrical stimulation (FES) for grasp and gait.

**2. Prosthetic Control (4 marks):**
- **Conventional:** Two-site threshold-based control — flexor vs. extensor EMG of residual limb opens/closes hand.
- **Pattern Recognition:** 4–8 channel SEMG → sliding window features (MAV, WL, ZC, RMS) → classifier (LDA/SVM/ANN) → 6–10 grasp classes.
- **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR):** Nerves from the amputated limb are rerouted to remaining muscles, providing distinct EMG patterns for intuitive control.
- **Osseointegration:** Direct skeletal attachment of the prosthesis enables stable EMG signal pickup.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **No "method" cited:** For each clinical application, students must *name* the specific EMG measurement (e.g., "MDF for fatigue," "SFEMG jitter for myasthenia"). Vague answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **No values:** Numbers like "MDF drops by 0.5–1.5%/s" or "CMRR > 100 dB" demonstrate mastery. Avoid wordy descriptions without figures.
> 3. **Missing block diagrams:** For 14-mark questions, a clearly labelled block diagram is *non-negotiable* — it alone carries 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Conflating ECG and EMG:** A frequent error — the ECG QRS complex is a *deflection*, while EMG is a *continuous stochastic process*. Examiners dock 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** "Frequency 20–500" without Hz; "amplitude 0–5" without mV — these are the silent mark-killers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EMG** = motor-unit action potential superposition; a stochastic, zero-mean, non-stationary signal.
- ✅ **Motor unit** = α-motor neuron + axon + NMJs + innervated muscle fibers.
- ✅ **MUAP duration** = 2–15 ms; **EMG amplitude** = 0–10 mV; **band** = 20–500 Hz; **dominant energy** = 50–150 Hz.
- ✅ **Surface EMG** = non-invasive, wide pickup, kinesiology/prosthetics; **Needle EMG** = invasive, single-unit resolution, clinical diagnosis.
- ✅ **Size principle (Henneman):** small motor units recruited first, larger units join with increasing force.
- ✅ **Time-domain features:** RMS, ARV, IEMG, MAV, ZC, WL, WAMP, SSC.
- ✅ **Frequency-domain features:** PSD, mean frequency, **median frequency (best fatigue indicator)**.
