# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]
**Define a biosensor. With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block.**

**Model Answer:**
A biosensor is a self-contained analytical device that uses a **biological recognition element (bioreceptor)** in direct contact with a **physico-chemical transducer** to detect a target analyte with high selectivity and sensitivity.

The five functional blocks are:

1. **Analyte** — the target molecule to be detected (e.g., glucose).
2. **Bio recognition element** — the biological component (enzyme, antibody, DNA, cell, MIP) that selectively binds or reacts with the analyte.
3. **Transducer** — converts the biochemical signal into a measurable electrical/optical/thermal/mass signal.
4. **Electronics** — amplifies, filters, and digitises the raw signal.
5. **Display** — presents the result in a human-readable form.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]
**Differentiate between catalytic and affinity bioreceptors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Catalytic Bioreceptors | Affinity Bioreceptors |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Catalyse a chemical reaction on the analyte | Bind the analyte reversibly without chemical change |
| Signal | Rate of product formation (kinetic) | Amount of bound analyte (equilibrium) |
| Reusability | Yes (catalyst is regenerated) | Limited by binding capacity |
| Examples | (1) Glucose oxidase, (2) Urease, (3) Horseradish peroxidase, (4) Whole cells | (1) Anti-hCG antibody, (2) ssDNA probe, (3) GPCR, (4) Aptamer, (5) MIP |

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **biological recognition elements** used in biosensors. Classify them with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different **immobilisation techniques** of enzymes on a transducer surface. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Biological recognition elements (bioreceptors) are the molecular components that confer **specificity** to a biosensor. They are classified into three families:

**1. Catalytic Bioreceptors (transform the analyte)**
- **Enzymes** — globular proteins with active sites. Examples: Glucose oxidase (GOx) for blood glucose, urease for urea, cholesterol oxidase for cholesterol, tyrosinase for catecholamines, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) for organophosphate pesticide detection. High turnover (amplification) and substrate-specific.
- **Whole cells** — living or fixed microorganisms. The *lux*-reporter bacteria emit light on exposure to mercury or arsenic, enabling optical detection. *Pseudomonas putida* is used in BOD sensors. Cells contain co-factors and provide natural enzymatic cascades.
- **Tissues** — banana/plantain slices contain polyphenol oxidase and are used in dopamine biosensors. Mammalian liver slices have been used for drug-metabolism studies.

**2. Affinity Bioreceptors (bind the analyte reversibly)**
- **Antibodies** — IgG, IgM, IgE. Monoclonal antibodies (mAb) give a single epitope specificity; polyclonal antibodies (pAb) give a broader response. Used in pregnancy tests (hCG), cardiac troponin assays, PSA for prostate cancer, and HIV detection.
- **Nucleic acids** — single-stranded DNA probes (15–30 mers) hybridise with complementary targets. Used in pathogen detection (e.g., *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* gene), cancer gene detection (BRCA1, BRCA2), and antibiotic-resistance markers (mecA in MRSA). Aptamers are *in vitro* selected ssDNA/RNA molecules that bind proteins and small molecules.
- **Receptors** — G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) bind hormones/neurotransmitters; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) detects acetylcholine; olfactory receptors (ORs) form the basis of electronic-nose (e-nose) biosensors for disease breath-print analysis.
- **Lectins** — bind specific sugar residues (Concanavalin A binds mannose/glucose); used in glycomics biosensors.

**3. Bio-mimetic Receptors**
- **Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)** — synthetic polymers with templated cavities. Cheap, robust, and stable in harsh conditions. Used in pharmaceutical and environmental sensing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Enzyme immobilisation is essential to (i) reuse the expensive biological component, (ii) maintain a stable contact with the transducer, and (iii) enable continuous-flow operation. The five principal techniques are:

1. **Physical Adsorption** — The enzyme is adsorbed on the transducer surface via Van der Waals, ionic, and hydrophobic interactions. *Advantage:* simple, no chemical modification. *Limitation:* weak binding, leaching under flow, pH- and temperature-sensitive.

2. **Covalent Bonding** — Functional groups of the enzyme (–NH$_2$ of lysine, –COOH of aspartate/glutamate, –SH of cysteine) are covalently linked to activated surfaces using **glutaraldehyde**, **carbodiimide (EDC/NHS)**, or **maleimide** chemistry. *Advantage:* very stable, minimal leaching. *Limitation:* harsh conditions may inactivate the active site.

3. **Entrapment** — The enzyme is physically trapped within a 3-D polymer matrix such as **PVA, polyacrylamide, alginate, sol-gel silica, or chitosan**. The analyte diffuses in; the product diffuses out. *Advantage:* mild conditions, retention of activity. *Limitation:* diffusion barrier slows response, possible leaching through matrix pores.

4. **Encapsulation** — The enzyme is enclosed within a **semi-permeable membrane** (Nafion, cellulose acetate, polycarbonate). *Advantage:* protects the enzyme from interferents (e.g., ascorbate, urate in blood). *Limitation:* additional mass-transfer resistance.

5. **Cross-linking (CLEA — Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates)** — The enzyme is precipitated and cross-linked with **glutaraldehyde** into insoluble aggregates. *Advantage:* very stable, no support required, high enzyme loading. *Limitation:* only for robust enzymes, mechanical brittleness.

6. **Affinity Binding (Avidin–Biotin)** — Biotinylated enzyme binds to streptavidin-coated surface ($K_d \approx 10^{-15}$ M, one of the strongest non-covalent interactions known). *Advantage:* oriented immobilisation, gentle, very specific. *Limitation:* cost of reagents.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in 14-mark answers:**
> - Confusing *entrapment* with *encapsulation* (entrapment is in a polymer matrix; encapsulation is in a membrane).
> - Forgetting to mention the effect of immobilisation on **enzyme kinetics** ($K_m$ typically increases and $V_{\max}$ decreases compared to free enzyme).
> - Writing only one or two immobilisation methods — KTU expects *at least* four for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **glucose biosensor** based on **glucose oxidase (GOx)**. Mention the role of each component and the reactions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Michaelis–Menten equation** for an enzyme-catalysed reaction. Using a Lineweaver–Burk plot, explain how $V_{\max}$ and $K_m$ are obtained experimentally. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **amperometric glucose biosensor** is the most commercially successful biosensor in history (>80 % of the global biosensor market). Its operation relies on the enzyme **glucose oxidase (GOx)**, which is immobilised on the surface of an amperometric working electrode (Pt or graphite), typically held at a potential of **+0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl**.

**Components and their roles:**

- **Outer membrane (e.g., polycarbonate, cellulose acetate):** Controls the diffusion of glucose and oxygen into the enzyme layer; rejects interferents like ascorbate, urate, and paracetamol.
- **Enzyme layer (immobilised GOx):** Catalyses the oxidation of glucose to gluconolactone, producing H$_2$O$_2$.
- **Inner membrane (e.g., cellulose acetate):** Permits H$_2$O$_2$ to reach the electrode.
- **Working electrode (Pt or graphite):** Oxidises H$_2$O$_2$ at +0.6 V; the resulting current is proportional to the glucose concentration.
- **Reference electrode (Ag/AgCl):** Maintains a stable reference potential.
- **Counter electrode (Pt):** Completes the electrical circuit.

**Reactions:**

Step 1 (Enzymatic):

$$\text{Glucose} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx (FAD)}} \text{Gluconolactone} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$$

Step 2 (Electrochemical):

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{+0.6\ \text{V vs Ag/AgCl}} \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

The current $I$ produced is given by:

$$I \;=\; n\,F\,A\,k\,[\text{Glucose}]$$

where $n=2$, $F=96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $A$ = electrode area, $k$ = heterogeneous rate constant. Hence, **current is directly proportional to glucose concentration**, enabling quantitative monitoring.

> A second-generation glucose biosensor uses a **mediator (ferrocene, or potassium ferricyanide)** to shuttle electrons from the reduced FAD directly to the electrode, eliminating the dependence on O$_2$ partial pressure. Third-generation sensors use **direct electron transfer** between the enzyme and a nanostructured electrode (e.g., GOx adsorbed on carbon nanotubes or graphene).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The derivation follows the seven steps of Section 3.1 above. The final Michaelis–Menten equation is:

$$v \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Experimental Determination via Lineweaver–Burk Plot:**

The equation is linearised as:

$$\dfrac{1}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{[S]} \,+\, \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the initial reaction velocity $v$ (or current $I$ for a biosensor) at several known substrate concentrations $[S]$.
2. Compute the reciprocals $1/[S]$ and $1/v$.
3. Plot $1/v$ (y-axis) versus $1/[S]$ (x-axis). A straight line is obtained.
4. The **y-intercept** is $1/V_{\max}$, so $V_{\max} = 1/(\text{y-intercept})$.
5. The **x-intercept** is $-1/K_m$, so $K_m = -1/(\text{x-intercept})$.
6. The **slope** is $K_m / V_{\max}$, which serves as a consistency check.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **y-intercept** of the Lineweaver–Burk plot with the **x-intercept**. The y-intercept gives $V_{\max}$; the x-intercept gives $K_m$. Memorise: "**V** is on the **V**ertical (y) axis; the **K** is on the **K**ross (x) axis."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **No diagram:** A 7-mark question on a biosensor MUST include a labelled block diagram or cross-sectional sketch. Drawing alone is worth ~2 marks.
> 2. **No equations:** Any question on enzyme kinetics expects the Michaelis–Menten equation and at least one derivation step.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_m$ and $K_d$:** $K_m$ is for *catalytic* (enzyme–substrate) interactions; $K_d$ is for *affinity* (antibody–antigen, DNA hybridisation) interactions.
> 4. **Ignoring units:** Always quote $K_m$ in mM, $K_d$ in nM or pM, LOD in appropriate mass/volume units.
> 5. **No mention of transducer pairing:** Mentioning the bioreceptor *without* naming the transducer (e.g., GOx + amperometric) is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A biosensor = **bioreceptor + transducer + electronics + display**.
- [ ] The **bioreceptor** provides *selectivity*; the **transducer** provides *sensitivity*.
- [ ] Bioreceptors are of **three families**: catalytic (enzymes, cells, tissues), affinity (antibodies, DNA, aptamers, receptors, lectins), and bio-mimetic (MIPs, DNAzymes).
- [ ] **Glucose oxidase (GOx)** is the *most important* enzyme in commercial biosensors.
- [ ] **Anti-hCG mAb on a lateral-flow strip** is the *most important* antibody-based biosensor (pregnancy test).
- [ ] **Aptamers** are *chemical antibodies* selected by SELEX with $K_d \sim 10^{-9}$–$10^{-12}$ M.
- [ ] **MIPs** are *plastic antibodies* — cheap, robust, but lower affinity than real antibodies.
- [ ] **Michaelis–Menten equation:** $v = V_{\max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$.
- [ ] **Lineweaver–Burk equation:** $1/v = (K_m/V_{\max})(1/[S]) + 1/V_{\max}$.
- [ ] **Scatchard equation:** $B/F = B_{\max}/K_d - (1/K_d)B$.
- [ ] **Hill equation** (cooperativity): $\theta = [L]^n / (K_d + [L]^n)$.
- [ ] **LOD** $= 3\sigma / S$; **selectivity coefficient** $k_{i,j} = S_i / S_j$.
- [ ] **Immobilisation methods** to remember: adsorption, covalent, entrapment, encapsulation, cross-linking, avidin–biotin. *EDC/NHS* and *glutaraldehyde* are favourite KTU answers.
- [ ] **Faraday constant** $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$ — commit to memory.
- [ ] **Sauerbrey equation** relates mass change to frequency shift in QCM biosensors.
- [ ] **GOx kinetics:** $K_m$ (free) $\approx 27$ mM; $K_m$ (immobilised) $\approx 4$–$10$ mM.
- [ ] **Streptavidin–biotin** binding is the strongest known non-covalent interaction ($K_d \sim 10^{-15}$ M).
- [ ] **Second-generation biosensors** use a mediator (e.g., ferrocene) to replace O$_2$.
- [ ] **Third-generation biosensors** achieve direct electron transfer via nanomaterials (CNT, graphene, AuNP).
- [ ] **Sensitivity** has units of *signal per concentration* (e.g., µA/mM, Hz/ng).
- [ ] **Selectivity** is dimensionless (ratio of two sensitivities).
- [ ] Always mention the **disadvantages** of each bioreceptor — KTU awards balanced answers higher marks.
- [ ] Draw a **neat labelled block diagram** in every 7- or 14-mark question on biosensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Topper Tip:** When asked *"Discuss bio recognition elements in biosensors,"* structure your answer as: (1) **definition** of bioreceptor, (2) **classification tree** (catalytic / affinity / bio-mimetic), (3) **examples of each** with the transducer they are paired with, (4) **criteria for choosing** a bioreceptor (selectivity, affinity, stability, cost), and (5) **immobilisation methods**. This 5-part structure is exactly what a KTU board examiner's marking scheme looks for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Immobilization Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Immobilization Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of biomedical transducers and biosensors, **Immobilization** refers to the physical or chemical fixation of a biologically active material (such as an enzyme, antibody, microbial cell, organelle, or nucleic acid) onto a solid support matrix or transducer surface in such a way that it retains its catalytic or binding activity while being prevented from freely migrating into the bulk solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *Immobilization is the technique of confining a bio-recognition element (BRE) within a defined region of space — either on the surface of, or within the bulk of, a support material — to enable its repeated and continuous use while preserving its functional bioactivity.*

The "bio-recognition element" forms the heart of a biosensor. Without immobilization, the bioreceptor would either be washed away by the sample stream (in flow systems) or would lose stability and reusability. Immobilization is, therefore, the **bridge between molecular biology and transducer electronics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **chef in a busy restaurant kitchen**. The cooking pan (the **transducer surface**) needs to hold a precious spice mixture (the **biological element**) so that every dish that passes over it gets a perfectly measured flavor.

- If you just sprinkle the spice loose on the pan, it falls off, gets wasted, and you cannot reuse it.
- If you **glue** the spice onto the pan (covalent bonding), it stays for thousands of dishes.
- If you **trap** the spice inside a mesh basket placed on the pan (entrapment), it cannot escape but the flavor (signal) still passes through.
- If you **magnetize** the spice particles so the pan attracts them (affinity binding), they stick strongly.

**Immobilization = the technique of "anchoring" the bioreceptor to the transducer without killing its activity.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Core Objectives of Immobilization (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> 1. **Reusability** — The expensive bioreceptor can be used hundreds to thousands of times.
> 2. **Stability** — Thermal, pH, and operational stability are dramatically enhanced.
> 3. **Separation** — The bioreceptor is easily separated from the product stream, preventing contamination of the analyte.

### 1.3 Why Immobilization Matters in a Biosensor

A biosensor has three functional layers stacked on the transducer:

1. **Bio-recognition layer** (the immobilized enzyme/antibody/cell)
2. **Transducer interface** (electrode, optical fiber, piezoelectric crystal)
3. **Electronics** (amplifier, signal processor, display)

The sensitivity, selectivity, response time, and shelf-life of the entire sensor hinge upon **how well the bio-recognition layer is immobilized**. Poor immobilization = poor sensor.

### 1.4 Categories of Immobilization

There are **two overarching families** of immobilization methods, and this classification is a KTU-favourite for 2-mark and 3-mark questions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A. Physical Methods (non-covalent interactions dominate)**
> - Adsorption
> - Microencapsulation
> - Entrapment
> - Affinity binding
>
> **B. Chemical Methods (covalent bonds or cross-links form)**
> - Covalent bonding
> - Cross-linking
> - Ionic binding (sometimes classified separately as a physio-chemical method)

The boundary between "physical" and "chemical" is not always rigid — many practical immobilizations use a **hybrid approach** (e.g., adsorption followed by cross-linking with glutaraldehyde).

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics in Immobilization

The following metrics are used universally to grade the quality of an immobilization procedure. These appear in KTU numerical and design problems:

- **Immobilization Yield (η_y)**: The fraction of biological material successfully bound.
- **Activity Recovery (A_r)**: The fraction of original biological activity retained after immobilization.
- **Operational Stability**: Half-life (t₁/₂) of activity under working conditions.
- **Loading Capacity**: Amount of biomaterial per unit mass or area of support.
- **Binding Strength (K_b)**: Equilibrium binding constant.

> [!NOTE]
> A well-engineered immobilization achieves **high activity recovery (>70%)** with **strong binding (low leaching)** and **minimal diffusion barrier** to the analyte.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Immobilization Methods

Below is a structured, exhaustive walk-through of every immobilization method a KTU 2024 student must know. Each method is described in terms of its **mechanism**, **support materials**, **procedure**, **advantages**, and **limitations**.

#### 2.1.1 ADSORPTION (Physical Method)

**Mechanism**: The biological element is held to the support via **weak, non-covalent forces** — van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and ionic (electrostatic) attractions.

**Common Support Materials**:
- Activated charcoal
- Porous glass (controlled pore glass, CPG)
- Cellulose derivatives
- Ion-exchange resins (DEAE-cellulose, CM-cellulose)
- Alumina, silica
- Polystyrene beads

**Procedure**:
1. The support is brought into contact with a buffered solution containing the biomaterial.
2. Equilibrium is established (typically 30 minutes to several hours).
3. The support is washed to remove loosely adsorbed species.
4. The system is ready for use.

**Why it works**: The surface energy of the support is reduced when biomolecules adsorb — this is thermodynamically favorable.

**Advantages**:
- Simple, cheap, mild conditions (no harsh chemicals).
- No chemical modification of the biomolecule.
- High retention of activity.

**Limitations**:
- Weak binding → **leaching** under changes in pH, ionic strength, or temperature.
- Non-specific — multiple biomolecules may compete.
- Sensitive to flow rate in continuous systems.

#### 2.1.2 COVALENT BONDING (Chemical Method)

**Mechanism**: The biomolecule is **chemically attached** to the support via covalent bonds, usually through the **amino groups (–NH₂)** of lysine residues, **carboxyl groups (–COOH)** of aspartate/glutamate, or **sulfhydryl groups (–SH)** of cysteine.

**Common Coupling Reagents**:
- Glutaraldehyde (most common bifunctional cross-linker)
- Carbodiimide (EDC / DCC) — activates –COOH to form amide with –NH₂
- Cyanogen bromide (CNBr) — activates polysaccharides
- Periodate oxidation — for carbohydrate-containing supports
- Tresyl chloride, tosyl chloride

**Procedure (CNBr activation of agarose, e.g., Sepharose)**:
1. Sepharose is activated with CNBr at pH 11.
2. Cyanate esters / imidocarbonates form on the matrix.
3. The biomolecule's –NH₂ groups react with these active sites.
4. Stable isourea linkages form.

**Advantages**:
- Very strong, permanent bonding → minimal leaching.
- Stable under varying pH, ionic strength, temperature.
- Suitable for continuous flow reactors and implantable sensors.

**Limitations**:
- Harsh chemistry may **denature** the biomolecule (loss of activity).
- Active site of enzyme may be blocked by the bond.
- Irreversible — once bound, cannot be regenerated without destruction.

#### 2.1.3 ENTRAPMENT (Physical Method)

**Mechanism**: The biomolecule is **trapped within the three-dimensional lattice of a polymer matrix** while the matrix is being formed. The biomolecule is physically caged but free to interact with substrates that diffuse in.

**Common Matrices**:
- Polyacrylamide gel
- Calcium alginate beads
- Agar / agarose gel
- κ-Carrageenan
- Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)
- Sol-gel silica (SiO₂)
- Chitosan

**Procedure (Calcium alginate, classical example)**:
1. Sodium alginate is mixed with the biomolecule solution.
2. The mixture is dripped into a CaCl₂ solution.
3. Ca²⁺ ions cross-link the alginate chains (egg-box model), forming beads.
4. The biomolecule is physically entrapped inside the gel network.

**Advantages**:
- Mild conditions → high activity recovery.
- Applicable to a very wide range of biomolecules (enzymes, cells, organelles).
- The matrix is permeable to substrates and products.

**Limitations**:
- Diffusion barrier → slower response time.
- Matrix may degrade or swell.
- Possible leakage from the surface.

#### 2.1.4 MICROENCAPSULATION (Physical Method)

**Mechanism**: The biomolecule is **enclosed within a spherical, semi-permeable membrane** (a microcapsule) of diameter typically 1–100 μm. Substrates and products can pass through the membrane, but the enzyme or cell remains inside.

**Common Membrane Materials**:
- Nylon
- Polyamide
- Cellulose acetate
- Polyethyleneimine
- Lipid vesicles (liposomes)

**Procedure (Interfacial polymerization for nylon microcapsules)**:
1. An aqueous solution containing the biomolecule is dispersed in an organic phase.
2. A hydrophilic monomer (e.g., a diamine) is added to the water phase; a hydrophobic monomer (e.g., a diacid chloride) is in the organic phase.
3. At the interface, polycondensation forms a thin nylon membrane.
4. The microcapsules are harvested and washed.

**Advantages**:
- Very high surface area → fast response.
- Can protect biomolecule from external harsh environment (pH, proteases).
- Suitable for very small reaction volumes (microfluidic biosensors).

**Limitations**:
- Membrane preparation can damage sensitive biomolecules.
- Membrane may rupture.
- Difficult to scale up reproducibly.

#### 2.1.5 CROSS-LINKING (Chemical Method)

**Mechanism**: The biomolecules are **joined to each other** using bifunctional or multifunctional reagents, forming a three-dimensional aggregate that is insoluble but still catalytically active. **No support is strictly required** — the cross-linked enzyme crystals (CLEC) or cross-linked enzyme aggregates (CLEA) themselves serve as the immobilized form.

**Common Cross-Linkers**:
- Glutaraldehyde (most common — reacts with –NH₂ groups)
- Hexamethylene diisocyanate
- Dialdehyde starch

**Procedure**:
1. The enzyme is precipitated from solution (e.g., by ammonium sulfate or acetone).
2. Glutaraldehyde is added.
3. Schiff bases and Michael adducts form, cross-linking the enzymes.
4. The CLEA is filtered, washed, and dried.

**Advantages**:
- No support needed → very high specific activity.
- Extremely stable (CLEAs can survive organic solvents, high temperatures).
- Concentrated enzyme activity.

**Limitations**:
- Severe activity loss due to harsh cross-linking conditions.
- The cross-linker can react with the active site.
- Mechanical brittleness of the aggregates.
- Poor reproducibility.

#### 2.1.6 AFFINITY BINDING (Bio-specific Physical Method)

**Mechanism**: Exploits the **highly specific biological interaction** between a ligand on the support and its binding partner on the biomolecule.

**Classic Example**: The **biotin–avidin (or biotin–streptavidin) interaction** with K_a ≈ **10¹⁵ M⁻¹** — one of the strongest non-covalent interactions known in nature.

**Procedure (streptavidin–biotin system)**:
1. The transducer surface is functionalized with streptavidin.
2. The biomolecule is biotinylated (covalently attached to a biotin tag).
3. The biotinylated biomolecule binds tightly to the streptavidin surface.
4. The system is ready — orientation can be controlled by site-specific biotinylation.

**Advantages**:
- Very high specificity and affinity.
- Mild conditions, no harsh chemicals.
- **Orientation can be controlled** — the active site can be directed outward.
- Highly reproducible.

**Limitations**:
- Expensive reagents.
- Multi-step procedure.
- The biotin tag may subtly alter biomolecule behavior.

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you need to solve KTU 2024 numerical and analytical problems on immobilization. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning of each.

**Table 2.1: Master Formula Sheet for Immobilization**

| **#** | **Equation** | **Where it applies** | **Key variables & units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$ | Langmuir adsorption isotherm (equilibrium binding) | $q$ = adsorbed amount (mg/g); $q_{max}$ = max capacity; $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mol); $C$ = free concentration (mol/L) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{q} = \dfrac{1}{q_{max}} + \dfrac{1}{K_L \, q_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C}$ | Linear form of Langmuir — used to fit experimental data | Plot $1/q$ vs $1/C$ → slope = $1/(K_L q_{max})$, intercept = $1/q_{max}$ |
| 3 | $\eta_y = \dfrac{A_{bound}}{A_{offered}} \times 100\%$ | Immobilization yield | $A_{bound}$ = activity of bound biomaterial; $A_{offered}$ = activity initially added |
| 4 | $A_r = \dfrac{A_{immob}}{A_{free}} \times 100\%$ | Activity recovery | $A_{immob}$ = activity after immobilization; $A_{free}$ = activity in free solution |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d}$ | Operational half-life (first-order deactivation) | $k_d$ = deactivation rate constant (s⁻¹ or day⁻¹) |
| 6 | $J = k_L \, (C_b - C_s)$ | External mass transfer (film diffusion) | $J$ = flux (mol/m²s); $k_L$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s); $C_b$, $C_s$ = bulk and surface concentrations |
| 7 | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_p \, D}{\tau}$ | Effective diffusion in porous support | $D$ = molecular diffusivity; $\varepsilon_p$ = porosity; $\tau$ = tortuosity |
| 8 | $\phi = R \, \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{D_{eff}}}$ | Thiele modulus (internal diffusion) | $\phi$ = dimensionless; $R$ = particle radius (m); $k_1$ = first-order rate constant (s⁻¹) |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2} \, (\phi \coth \phi - 1)$ | Effectiveness factor for first-order reaction in a sphere | $\eta$ = ratio of actual rate to rate without diffusion limitation; $\eta \to 1$ → no limitation; $\eta \to 1/\phi$ → strong limitation |
| 10 | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \, S}{K_{m,app} + S}$ | Apparent Michaelis–Menten for immobilized enzyme | $K_{m,app} \geq K_{m,free}$ due to diffusion barrier; $V_{max}$ may decrease due to steric hindrance |
| 11 | $K_d = \dfrac{[E][S]}{[ES]}$ | Equilibrium dissociation constant (binding affinity) | Smaller $K_d$ → tighter binding |
| 12 | $\theta = \dfrac{[ES]}{[E]_{total}}$ | Surface coverage / fractional saturation | $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The *Thiele modulus* and *effectiveness factor* are almost guaranteed to appear in a 14-mark question. Understand that $\phi$ is large when the reaction is fast or the particle is big or the matrix is dense — and when $\phi$ is large, $\eta \to 1/\phi$, meaning only a thin outer shell of the support is effectively used. This is the **diffusion limitation problem** of immobilization.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of immobilization method determines the **commercial viability** of a biosensor. Real-world examples:

- **Glucometer (glucose oxidase electrode)**: Glucose oxidase is immobilized by **entrapment in a polymer layer** (PVA or polypyrrole) on a platinum electrode.
- **Lactate biosensor (sports medicine, ICU)**: Lactate oxidase is **covalently bonded** to a Prussian Blue mediator electrode using EDC/NHS coupling.
- **Implantable glucose sensor (continuous glucose monitor, CGM)**: Uses an **affinity-coupled, hydrogel-entrapped** enzyme layer that lasts 7–14 days in subcutaneous tissue.
- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) sensor**: Whole microbial cells are **entrapped in calcium alginate** on a Clark-type oxygen electrode.
- **DNA microarrays (gene chips)**: Oligonucleotide probes are **covalently bonded** to a glass slide via an amino-linker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Design Rule of Thumb (often tested):**
> If the bioreceptor is **expensive** (e.g., a monoclonal antibody) → prefer **covalent bonding** or **affinity binding** (no leaching, full reusability justifies the cost).
> If the bioreceptor is **cheap and abundant** (e.g., glucose oxidase from Aspergillus niger) → **entrapment or adsorption** is preferred for economic reasons.
> If the bioreceptor is a **whole cell or organelle** → **entrapment in alginate or κ-carrageenan** is the industry standard.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section presents the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the Thiele modulus / effectiveness factor, and the apparent Michaelis-Menten kinetics for immobilized enzymes — exactly the type of derivation a KTU 14-mark question demands. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly; no logical jumps are allowed.

---

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

The Langmuir isotherm is the **cornerstone model** for adsorption-based immobilization. It assumes:
1. The support surface has a fixed number of identical binding sites.
2. Each site can hold **at most one** biomolecule (monolayer coverage).
3. All sites are energetically equivalent.
4. No interaction between adsorbed biomolecules.

**Step 1 — Define the reversible adsorption reaction.**

A free biomolecule $P$ in solution binds reversibly to an empty surface site $S$ to form a bound complex $SP$:

$$P + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{+1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} SP$$

where $k_{+1}$ is the adsorption rate constant (L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹) and $k_{-1}$ is the desorption rate constant (s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Express the rate of adsorption (forward direction).**

The forward rate is proportional to the concentration of free biomolecule $C$ and the fraction of empty sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_{+1} \, C \, (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Express the rate of desorption (reverse direction).**

The reverse rate is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_{-1} \, \theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium condition.**

At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_{+1} \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_{-1} \, \theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$.**

Rearrange the equation to isolate $\theta$:

$$k_{+1} \, C - k_{+1} \, C \, \theta = k_{-1} \, \theta$$

Bring all $\theta$ terms to one side:

$$k_{+1} \, C = \theta \, (k_{-1} + k_{+1} \, C)$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_{+1} \, C}{k_{-1} + k_{+1} \, C}$$

**Step 6 — Define the equilibrium (Langmuir) constant.**

$$K_L = \dfrac{k_{+1}}{k_{-1}}$$

with units of **L mol⁻¹** (volume per mole).

**Step 7 — Substitute into the equation for $\theta$.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$

**Step 8 — Convert fractional coverage to adsorbed amount.**

If $q_{max}$ is the maximum amount of biomaterial that can be adsorbed (i.e., when $\theta = 1$, monolayer), then the actual adsorbed amount is:

$$q = q_{max} \, \theta$$

Substitute the expression for $\theta$:

$$\boxed{\,q = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}\,}$$

This is the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** — used to model the equilibrium of any adsorption-based immobilization.

**Step 9 — Derive the linear (reciprocal) form.**

Take the reciprocal of both sides:

$$\dfrac{1}{q} = \dfrac{1 + K_L \, C}{q_{max} \, K_L \, C} = \dfrac{1}{q_{max} \, K_L \, C} + \dfrac{1}{q_{max}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{q} = \dfrac{1}{q_{max}} + \dfrac{1}{K_L \, q_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C}\,}$$

This linear form is used experimentally: a plot of $1/q$ versus $1/C$ yields a straight line with slope $1/(K_L \, q_{max})$ and intercept $1/q_{max}$, from which both $K_L$ and $q_{max}$ can be determined.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effectiveness Factor $\eta$ for a Spherical Immobilized Particle

This derivation models the situation where a substrate $S$ diffuses into a porous spherical support of radius $R$ and reacts with an immobilized enzyme following **first-order kinetics** (valid when substrate concentration is much lower than $K_m$).

**Step 1 — Write the material balance on a thin spherical shell.**

Consider a hollow sphere of inner radius $r$ and outer radius $r + dr$. The steady-state diffusion-reaction equation in spherical coordinates is:

$$D_{eff} \left( \dfrac{d^2 C}{dr^2} + \dfrac{2}{r} \dfrac{dC}{dr} \right) - k_1 \, C = 0$$

where $D_{eff}$ is the effective diffusion coefficient (m²/s) of the substrate inside the porous support, and $k_1$ is the first-order rate constant (s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Make the equation dimensionless.**

Define a dimensionless radial coordinate $\rho = r/R$ and a dimensionless concentration $c = C/C_s$, where $C_s$ is the substrate concentration at the particle's external surface.

Substituting $r = \rho R$, $dr = R \, d\rho$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 C}{dr^2} = \dfrac{1}{R^2} \dfrac{d^2 C}{d\rho^2}$$

$$\dfrac{2}{r} \dfrac{dC}{dr} = \dfrac{2}{\rho R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} = \dfrac{2}{\rho R^2} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho}$$

The equation becomes:

$$D_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R^2} \left[ \dfrac{d^2 C}{d\rho^2} + \dfrac{2}{\rho} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} \right] - k_1 \, C = 0$$

Multiply through by $R^2 / D_{eff}$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 C}{d\rho^2} + \dfrac{2}{\rho} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} - \dfrac{k_1 \, R^2}{D_{eff}} \, C = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the Thiele modulus.**

The dimensionless group $\phi$ is defined as:

$$\phi = R \, \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{D_{eff}}}$$

The equation now reads:

$$\dfrac{d^2 C}{d\rho^2} + \dfrac{2}{\rho} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} - \phi^2 \, C = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**

At the outer surface of the sphere ($\rho = 1$), the concentration equals the bulk surface concentration $C_s$:

$$C(\rho = 1) = C_s$$

At the center of the sphere ($\rho = 0$), the concentration must be finite (no singularity) and by symmetry:

$$\left. \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} \right|_{\rho = 0} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the differential equation.**

The standard solution of this second-order linear ODE in spherical coordinates is:

$$C(\rho) = \dfrac{C_s \, \sinh(\phi \rho)}{\rho \, \sinh(\phi)}$$

(One can verify: as $\rho \to 0$, $\sinh(\phi \rho) \approx \phi \rho$, so $C \to C_s \phi / \sinh \phi$, which is finite; at $\rho = 1$, $C = C_s \sinh \phi / \sinh \phi = C_s$ ✓.)

**Step 6 — Compute the actual rate of reaction inside the sphere.**

The total actual rate of reaction in the sphere is the volume integral of $k_1 C$:

$$r_{actual} = \int_0^R k_1 \, C(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 \, dr$$

In dimensionless form:

$$r_{actual} = 4\pi R^3 k_1 C_s \int_0^1 \dfrac{\sinh(\phi \rho)}{\rho \, \sinh(\phi)} \, \rho^2 \, d\rho = \dfrac{4\pi R^3 k_1 C_s}{\sinh \phi} \int_0^1 \rho \, \sinh(\phi \rho) \, d\rho$$

**Step 7 — Compute the rate if there were no diffusion limitation.**

If the entire sphere were at concentration $C_s$ (no diffusion resistance), the rate would simply be:

$$r_{ideal} = k_1 \, C_s \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$

**Step 8 — Define the effectiveness factor.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{r_{actual}}{r_{ideal}}$$

Carrying out the integration $\int_0^1 \rho \sinh(\phi \rho) d\rho = \dfrac{\sinh \phi}{\phi^2} - \dfrac{\cosh \phi}{\phi} + \dfrac{1}{\phi^2}$ and simplifying gives the classical result:

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2} \left( \phi \coth \phi - 1 \right)\,}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the limiting cases.**

- **Case 1: $\phi \to 0$ (very small particle, slow reaction, fast diffusion).**
  $\coth \phi \approx 1/\phi + \phi/3$, so $\eta \to 1$. The whole particle is effectively used.
- **Case 2: $\phi \to \infty$ (large particle, fast reaction, slow diffusion).**
  $\coth \phi \to 1$, so $\eta \to 3/\phi$. The reaction is confined to a thin outer shell of thickness $\delta \approx R/\phi$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Immobilization Method

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-density, exam-ready summary**. The KTU examiner expects you to discuss, compare, and justify the choice of method. Internalize the trade-offs.

**Table 3.1: Comparison of Immobilization Methods**

| **Criterion** | **Adsorption** | **Covalent** | **Entrapment** | **Microencapsulation** | **Cross-linking** | **Affinity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binding strength** | Low | Very high | Medium | Medium | High (within aggregate) | Very high |
| **Activity recovery** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| **Leaching risk** | High | Negligible | Medium | Medium | None | Negligible |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Procedural complexity** | Very low | Medium | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| **Reusability** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Diffusion barrier** | Minimal | Minimal | Significant | Significant | Significant | Minimal |
| **Applicability to cells** | Limited | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Limited |
| **Operational stability** | Low | Very high | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Best for** | Initial screening | Industrial bioreactors | Whole-cell systems | Microfluidics | Solvent-stable enzymes | Sensitive immunoassays |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example Problem (Exam-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **fully solved sample problem** typical of a KTU 14-mark Part B question. Practice this style of solution for maximum marks.

**Problem:**
An enzyme (molecular weight 60,000 Da) is immobilized by adsorption onto a support. The support has a maximum binding capacity $q_{max} = 50$ mg/g and Langmuir constant $K_L = 2.5$ L/mmol. Calculate:
(a) The amount of enzyme adsorbed at equilibrium when the free concentration is 0.8 mmol/L.
(b) The fractional surface coverage $\theta$ at this concentration.
(c) The immobilization yield if 100 mg of enzyme was added to 2 g of support and 85% bound.

**Solution:**

**(a) Apply the Langmuir isotherm:**

$$q = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$

Substitute $q_{max} = 50$ mg/g, $K_L = 2.5$ L/mmol, $C = 0.8$ mmol/L:

$$q = \dfrac{50 \times 2.5 \times 0.8}{1 + 2.5 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 2.0} = \dfrac{100}{3} = 33.33 \text{ mg/g}$$

**(b) Fractional surface coverage:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C} = \dfrac{2.0}{3.0} = 0.667 \text{ (or 66.7\%)}$$

**(c) Immobilization yield:**

$$\eta_y = \dfrac{85 \text{ mg bound}}{100 \text{ mg offered}} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the relationship: $q = q_{max} \cdot \theta$. Verify: $50 \times 0.667 = 33.35$ mg/g, consistent with (a).

### 4.4 Diffusion-Reaction Model Inside an Immobilized Particle (Conceptual)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Thiele modulus concept** graphically — the substrate concentration profile inside a spherical support, showing why the effectiveness factor $\eta$ is critical.

```mermaid
graph TD

    R2[External Film Boundary Layer]:::outer
    R3[Particle Surface C_s Lower than C_b due to film resistance]:::surface
    R4[Inside Porous Support Concentration Drops Exponentially]:::inner
    R5[Center of Particle C approaches 0 if phi is large]:::inner
    R6[Thiele Modulus phi dictates the profile shape]:::concept
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5
    R6 -.controls.-> R4
    classDef outer fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000
    classDef surface fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef inner fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
    classDef concept fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As $\phi$ increases, the concentration profile becomes steeper — the enzyme deep inside the particle is "starved" of substrate. This is the **single most important engineering limitation** of immobilization and is responsible for the often-observed phenomenon that **immobilized enzymes have higher apparent $K_m$** than free enzymes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Part A — Q1** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define immobilization. List any four methods of immobilization with one example of a support material for each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Immobilization is the process of physically or chemically confining a biologically active material (such as an enzyme, antibody, microbial cell, or organelle) within a defined region of space, typically on or within a solid support, in such a way that it retains its catalytic or binding activity and can be separated from the reaction mixture and reused.

**Four methods with support examples:**

| **#** | **Method** | **Example Support Material** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adsorption | Activated charcoal / porous glass |
| 2 | Covalent bonding | CNBr-activated Sepharose |
| 3 | Entrapment | Calcium alginate beads |
| 4 | Microencapsulation | Nylon microcapsules |

*(Any four valid combinations are acceptable.)*

---

**Part A — Q2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare adsorption and covalent bonding methods of immobilization with respect to binding strength, activity recovery, and leaching.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Adsorption** | **Covalent Bonding** |
|---|---|---|
| Binding strength | Weak (van der Waals, H-bonds) | Very strong (covalent bonds) |
| Activity recovery | High (mild conditions) | Medium (harsh reagents may denature) |
| Leaching | High (pH/ionic-strength sensitive) | Negligible (permanent attachment) |
| Reusability | Limited | Excellent |

**Conclusion:** Adsorption is preferred for **initial screening and lab-scale** studies where cost and simplicity matter. Covalent bonding is preferred for **industrial and implantable biosensors** where long-term stability is critical.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **covalent bonding method** of immobilization in detail. Explain the activation of Sepharose by CNBr and the coupling of an enzyme to it. Discuss the advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** and state the assumptions. An immobilized enzyme preparation on a porous support has $q_{max} = 80$ mg/g and $K_L = 3.0$ L/mmol. Determine the amount adsorbed (in mg/g) and the fractional surface coverage when the free substrate concentration is 0.5 mmol/L. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Principle of Covalent Bonding:**
In covalent immobilization, the biomolecule is **chemically attached** to a pre-activated support via stable covalent bonds. The most commonly exploited functional groups on the protein surface are:
- **ε-amino group (–NH₂)** of lysine residues
- **Carboxyl group (–COOH)** of aspartate / glutamate
- **Sulfhydryl group (–SH)** of cysteine
- **Phenol –OH** of tyrosine
- **Imidazole** of histidine

**2. CNBr Activation of Sepharose (mechanism step-by-step):**

Step 1: CNBr reacts with the hydroxyl groups of the agarose (Sepharose) polysaccharide at alkaline pH (≈ 11):

$$\text{Agarose–OH} + \text{CNBr} \rightarrow \text{Agarose–O–CN} + \text{HBr}$$

Step 2: The cyanate ester tautomerizes to a more reactive cyclic imidocarbonate:

$$\text{Agarose–O–CN} \rightarrow \text{Cyclic Imidocarbonate (reactive intermediate)}$$

Step 3: The nucleophilic ε-amino group of lysine attacks the imidocarbonate, forming a stable **isourea linkage**:

$$\text{Agarose–O–C(=NH)–NH–R (enzyme)}$$

**3. Advantages of Covalent Bonding:**
- Permanent attachment — no leaching.
- High operational stability under varying pH, temperature, ionic strength.
- Suitable for continuous flow reactors and implantable biosensors.
- High reusability — supports hundreds of reaction cycles.

**4. Limitations of Covalent Bonding:**
- Harsh reagents (CNBr is toxic) can denature the enzyme.
- Active site may be blocked by the covalent bond (low activity recovery, 30–70%).
- Irreversible — if the support fails, the entire batch is lost.
- Requires precise pH and temperature control during coupling.
- High cost of pre-activated supports.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of the Langmuir isotherm (7-step proof):**

Step 1: Write the equilibrium binding reaction:

$$P + S \rightleftharpoons PS$$

Step 2: Forward rate (adsorption): $r_f = k_{+1} \, C \, (1 - \theta)$

Step 3: Reverse rate (desorption): $r_r = k_{-1} \, \theta$

Step 4: At equilibrium, $r_f = r_r$:

$$k_{+1} \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_{-1} \, \theta$$

Step 5: Rearrange to isolate $\theta$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_{+1} \, C}{k_{-1} + k_{+1} \, C}$$

Step 6: Define $K_L = k_{+1} / k_{-1}$ and substitute:

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$

Step 7: Multiply by $q_{max}$ to get $q$:

$$q = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$

**Assumptions of the Langmuir isotherm:**
1. Monolayer coverage.
2. All sites are energetically equivalent.
3. No interaction between adsorbed molecules.
4. The support surface is homogeneous.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $q_{max} = 80$ mg/g, $K_L = 3.0$ L/mmol, $C = 0.5$ mmol/L.

$$q = \dfrac{80 \times 3.0 \times 0.5}{1 + 3.0 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{120}{1 + 1.5} = \dfrac{120}{2.5} = 48 \text{ mg/g}$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{3.0 \times 0.5}{1 + 1.5} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.60 \text{ (or 60\%)}$$

**Verification:** $q = q_{max} \cdot \theta = 80 \times 0.60 = 48$ mg/g ✓

---

#### **Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is the **Thiele modulus**? Derive the **effectiveness factor** for a first-order reaction in a spherical immobilized particle and explain its two limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **entrapment method** of immobilization with a suitable example. Compare it with **microencapsulation** in terms of membrane properties and applications. An enzyme is entrapped in a spherical alginate bead of radius 2 mm. The effective diffusion coefficient of the substrate inside the bead is $5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, and the first-order rate constant is $0.02$ s⁻¹. Calculate the Thiele modulus and the effectiveness factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition of the Thiele modulus:**
The Thiele modulus $\phi$ is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies the ratio of the surface reaction rate to the rate of internal diffusion inside a porous catalyst (or immobilized enzyme) particle. It is defined as:

$$\phi = R \, \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{D_{eff}}}$$

where $R$ is the particle radius, $k_1$ is the first-order rate constant, and $D_{eff}$ is the effective diffusion coefficient.

**Derivation of the effectiveness factor (concise 5-step version for the exam):**

Step 1: Steady-state material balance in a spherical shell:

$$D_{eff} \left( \dfrac{d^2 C}{dr^2} + \dfrac{2}{r} \dfrac{dC}{dr} \right) - k_1 \, C = 0$$

Step 2: Convert to dimensionless form using $\rho = r/R$ and $\phi = R \sqrt{k_1/D_{eff}}$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 C}{d\rho^2} + \dfrac{2}{\rho} \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} - \phi^2 \, C = 0$$

Step 3: Apply boundary conditions: $C(\rho=1) = C_s$ and $\left. \dfrac{dC}{d\rho} \right|_{\rho=0} = 0$.

Step 4: Solution: $C(\rho) = \dfrac{C_s \, \sinh(\phi \rho)}{\rho \, \sinh(\phi)}$.

Step 5: The effectiveness factor is the ratio of the actual volumetric rate to the rate that would be obtained if the entire particle were at $C_s$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{r_{actual}}{r_{ideal}} = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2} \left( \phi \coth \phi - 1 \right)$$

**Limiting cases:**

- **$\phi \to 0$ (diffusion is fast, reaction is slow):** $\eta \to 1$. The whole particle is used effectively. No diffusion limitation.
- **$\phi \to \infty$ (reaction is fast, diffusion is slow):** $\eta \to 3/\phi$. The reaction occurs only in a thin outer shell. Most of the enzyme inside is "wasted."

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Entrapment method:**
In entrapment, the biomolecule is **physically trapped within the three-dimensional lattice of a polymer matrix** that is formed in situ around the biomolecule. The matrix is porous, allowing substrates and products to diffuse in and out, but the enzyme itself is mechanically caged and cannot escape.

**Example:** Glucose oxidase is entrapped in a **calcium alginate bead** by dripping a sodium alginate + enzyme solution into CaCl₂. The Ca²⁺ ions cross-link the guluronic acid blocks of alginate (the "egg-box model"), instantly gelling the mixture into beads.

**Comparison: Entrapment vs Microencapsulation**

| **Criterion** | **Entrapment** | **Microencapsulation** |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Solid gel matrix (bead, film, fiber) | Hollow sphere with thin membrane |
| Membrane property | Continuous porous polymer throughout | Thin semi-permeable shell only |
| Surface area | Moderate | Very high (small capsules) |
| Typical size | 0.5 – 5 mm | 1 – 100 μm |
| Mechanical strength | Higher (bulk gel) | Lower (thin shell) |
| Best for | Whole cells, robust enzymes | Sensitive proteins, microfluidic sensors |
| Example | Ca-alginate bead with yeast | Nylon microcapsule with urease |

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $R = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $D_{eff} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, $k_1 = 0.02$ s⁻¹.

**Step 1: Thiele modulus:**

$$\phi = R \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{D_{eff}}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.02}{5 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

Compute the ratio inside the square root:

$$\dfrac{0.02}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 4 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{4 \times 10^{7}} = 2 \times 10^{3.5} = 6324.6 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Multiply by $R$:

$$\phi = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 6324.6 = 12.65$$

**Step 2: Effectiveness factor:**

For large $\phi$, use the asymptotic form $\eta \approx 3/\phi$ (since $\phi > 3$):

$$\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi} = \dfrac{3}{12.65} = 0.237 \text{ (or 23.7\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** Only about **24% of the enzyme inside the bead is effectively utilized**. The remaining 76% is "wasted" due to severe internal diffusion limitation. **Engineering solution:** reduce the bead size (use 0.5 mm beads), which lowers $\phi$ by a factor of 4 and increases $\eta$ to nearly 60%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit errors in $\phi$:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert $R$ from mm to m. KTU examiners mark this strictly — **always show the unit conversion explicitly**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{}$ sign in $\phi$:** Students sometimes write $\phi = R \cdot k_1 / D_{eff}$ (incorrect — it's a square-root expression). The Thiele modulus is **dimensionless**; verify this.
> 3. **Confusion between $K_m$ and $K_{m,app}$:** Immobilized enzymes have a higher apparent $K_m$ due to diffusion limitation. If a question asks why immobilization increases $K_m$, the answer is the **diffusion barrier**, not denaturation.
> 4. **Confusing activity recovery with immobilization yield:** $\eta_y$ is about *amount bound*; $A_r$ is about *activity retained*. They are different; confining them is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping steps:** KTU 14-mark Part B carries 7 marks per sub-part. Step-by-step derivation is mandatory — never write just the final equation. The valuation key explicitly awards partial marks for intermediate steps.
> 6. **Not mentioning the three objectives of immobilization** (reusability, stability, separation) in a 3-mark definition question — this alone costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Immobilization = confining a bioreceptor on/in a support while retaining activity.
- [x] **Three Objectives:** Reusability, Stability, Separation from product.
- [x] **Two families:** Physical (adsorption, entrapment, microencapsulation, affinity) and Chemical (covalent, cross-linking, ionic).
- [x] **CNBr activation of Sepharose** forms a **cyanate ester** → **imidocarbonate** → **isourea linkage** with –NH₂ of lysine.
- [x] **Glutaraldehyde** is the most common bifunctional cross-linker (reacts with –NH₂).
- [x] **Biotin–Avidin $K_a \approx 10^{15}$ M⁻¹** — strongest non-covalent biological interaction.
- [x] **Calcium alginate** entrapment: Na-alginate + biomolecule dripped into CaCl₂ → Ca²⁺ egg-box cross-links form beads.
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm:** $q = \dfrac{q_{max} K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$. Linear form: $\dfrac{1}{q} = \dfrac{1}{q_{max}} + \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C}$.
- [x] **Thiele modulus:** $\phi = R \sqrt{k_1 / D_{eff}}$ — dimensionless.
- [x] **Effectiveness factor:** $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2} \left( \phi \coth \phi - 1 \right)$.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $\phi \to 0 \Rightarrow \eta \to 1$ (no limitation); $\phi \to \infty \Rightarrow \eta \to 3/\phi$ (severe limitation).
- [x] **Immobilization yield** $\eta_y$ (fraction bound) ≠ **Activity recovery** $A_r$ (fraction of original activity retained).
- [x] **Operational half-life:** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d$.
- [x] **Apparent $K_m$** of immobilized enzyme $\geq$ **free $K_m$** due to diffusion barrier.
- [x] **Design trade-off:** Smaller particles → higher $\eta$ but harder to handle; larger particles → easier to handle but lower $\eta$.
- [x] **Pick the right method for the application:** Cells → entrapment; expensive proteins → covalent or affinity; screening → adsorption; solvent-stable → CLEAs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ISFET (Ion-Sensitive Field Effect Transistor)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ion-Sensitive Field Effect Transistor (ISFET)** is a semiconductor-based bioelectric transducer that operates on the principle of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (MOSFET), in which the metal gate electrode is replaced by a **reference electrode**, an **electrolyte solution**, and an **ion-sensitive membrane**. The device converts the chemical activity (concentration) of a specific ionic species in an electrolyte into an electrical signal by modulating the source–drain current of the underlying field-effect transistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The ISFET is classified as a *chemical-to-electrical transducer* and falls under the broader category of *Bioelectric Transducers* in Module 3. It is one of the most frequently asked short-answer topics in Part A questions (3 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Inventor:**
> The ISFET was first reported by **Piet Bergveld** in **1970** at the University of Twente, Netherlands, originally developed for neurophysiological measurements. The device was later refined by Matsuo, Wise, and others.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an ISFET as a **"chemical faucet"**:

| Familiar Device | Analogous Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Faucet handle (gate) | Reference electrode + ion-sensitive membrane | Controls how much water (current) flows |
| Water flow (Id) | Source-to-drain current | Output that we measure |
| Water pressure (pH) | Ion concentration in electrolyte | Input stimulus |

In a regular **MOSFET**, the *gate voltage* decides how much current flows between the **Source** and the **Drain**. In an **ISFET**, instead of applying a gate voltage directly, the *chemical environment* (ion concentration or pH) decides the effective voltage at the "gate." The smarter the faucet (more sensitive membrane), the better it senses the chemical "pressure."

> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a glass slab whose surface becomes *positively charged* when placed in an acidic solution and *negatively charged* in a basic solution. This invisible surface charge "pushes" or "pulls" the electrons in the underlying silicon, just like a voltage applied to a MOSFET gate would. The ISFET is essentially a *chemically-gated* transistor.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

- **Electrolyte:** A solution containing free ions that conducts electricity (e.g., blood, urine, saliva).
- **Reference Electrode:** A stable, half-cell electrode (commonly Ag/AgCl) that provides a fixed electrical potential.
- **Ion-Sensitive Membrane (ISM):** A thin dielectric layer (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Ta₂O₅, Al₂O₃, polymer, or glass) that selectively interacts with specific ions.
- **Site-Binding Model:** The theoretical framework describing the chemical equilibrium of H⁺/OH⁻ at the oxide-electrolyte interface.
- **Isoelectric Point (pH_pzc):** The pH value at which the surface potential of the membrane is zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Memorization):**
> - **Faraday's constant, F = 96,485 C/mol** (approximately 9.648 × 10⁴ C/mol)
> - **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Thermal voltage at 25 °C (298 K), V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV**
> - **Nernstian pH sensitivity at 25 °C = 59.2 mV/pH**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an ISFET

The ISFET is a four-terminal device with the following structural layers (from top to bottom):

1. **Reference Electrode (RE):** Inserted into the bulk electrolyte. Establishes a stable DC bias and closes the electrical circuit through the electrolyte.
2. **Electrolyte:** The aqueous solution whose ionic content is being measured.
3. **Ion-Sensitive Membrane (ISM):** A thin insulating layer in direct contact with the electrolyte. This replaces the metal gate of a MOSFET.
4. **Insulator Layer (e.g., SiO₂):** Sometimes present beneath the ISM for passivation and improved adhesion.
5. **Semiconductor Substrate (p-type Si):** The body of the transistor.
6. **n+ Source and n+ Drain:** Heavily doped regions forming the conducting channel.

> **Cross-reference:** The structure closely resembles a MOSFET, but the **gate metal is removed** and replaced by the *electrolyte + ISM + reference electrode* combination.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

The ISFET operates in **three simultaneous stages**:

1. **Chemical Recognition (at the ISM–electrolyte interface):**
   - Target ions (e.g., H⁺) adsorb onto the active sites of the ion-sensitive membrane.
   - This creates a **surface potential (ψ₀)** at the membrane–electrolyte boundary.
   - The site-binding model gives: $\psi_0 = 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF}(pH_{pzc} - pH)$

2. **Electrochemical Transduction (across the ISM):**
   - The surface potential is capacitively coupled through the ISM to the silicon–insulator interface.
   - The effective gate voltage becomes: $V_{G,eff} = V_{RE} - \psi_0 - \chi_{sol}$
     where $V_{RE}$ is the reference electrode potential and $\chi_{sol}$ is the surface dipole potential of the solution.

3. **Electronic Modulation (in the MOSFET channel):**
   - The change in $\psi_0$ modulates the **threshold voltage** of the underlying MOSFET structure.
   - This in turn modulates the source–drain current $I_D$.
   - The drain current follows the standard MOSFET equation (linear/triode region):
     $$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{TH(ISFET)} = V_{TH(MOSFET)} - \Delta\psi_0 + \chi_{sol}$ | Modified threshold voltage of ISFET | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Delta\psi_0 = 2.303 \cdot \frac{RT}{zF} \cdot \Delta pH$ | Surface potential change (Nernst) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $\psi_0 = 2.303 \cdot \frac{RT}{zF}(pH_{pzc} - pH)$ | Surface potential at pH (site-binding) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Sensitivity $= \frac{\partial V_{TH}}{\partial pH} = 2.303 \cdot \frac{RT}{zF}$ | pH sensitivity (Nernstian slope) | **59.2 mV/pH at 25 °C** |
| 5 | $I_D = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Drain current (saturation) | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{zF} \ln Q$ | Nernst equation (general electrode) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $E = E^0 + \frac{0.0592}{z} \log_{10}[M^{z+}]$ | Nernst equation at 25 °C | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Drift rate $= \frac{\Delta V_{TH}}{\Delta t}$ | Long-term stability metric | mV/hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL SYMBOL SUBSTITUTION RULE:**
> Inside this KTU formula table, all absolute values and divide-symbols are written using the safe LaTeX equivalents `\vert` or `\mid`. The vertical pipe character `\vert` is **strictly forbidden** in table cells because it breaks the markdown column separator. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in KTU 2024 table notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ISFETs have revolutionized the following biomedical domains:

- **In-vivo Blood pH Monitoring:** Continuous real-time pH tracking during cardiac surgery.
- **DNA Sequencing (Ion Torrent / pH-mediated detection):** Commercially exploited by Thermo Fisher Scientific.
- **Wearable Sweat Sensors:** Real-time electrolyte monitoring (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻) in athletes and patients.
- **Enzyme Field Effect Transistors (EnFET):** Glucose, urea, and lactate biosensors by immobilizing enzymes over the gate.
- **Cell Culture Bioprocess Control:** Monitoring of fermentation broth pH without contamination risk.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Detection of heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺) in water supplies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage of an ISFET

**Starting Premise (KTU Board Favorite):**
The threshold voltage of a conventional MOSFET is given by:
$$V_{TH(MOSFET)} = \phi_{MS} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where:
- $\phi_{MS}$ = metal–semiconductor work function difference
- $\phi_F$ = Fermi potential of the semiconductor
- $\varepsilon_{si}$ = permittivity of silicon
- $N_A$ = acceptor (doping) concentration
- $C_{ox}$ = oxide capacitance per unit area

**Step 1 — Modification for ISFET:**
In the ISFET, the *metal gate* is replaced by the *electrolyte + reference electrode + ISM* combination. Therefore, the metal–semiconductor work function difference $\phi_{MS}$ is replaced by an *electrochemical* potential difference:
$$\phi_{MS}^{ISFET} = \phi_{RE} - \psi_0 - \chi_{sol} - \phi_{Si}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting Back:**
Substituting this modified work-function term into the standard MOSFET threshold equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH(ISFET)} &= \phi_{RE} - \psi_0 - \chi_{sol} - \phi_{Si} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with MOSFET Threshold Voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH(MOSFET)} &= \phi_{MS} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}} \\
V_{TH(ISFET)} &= V_{TH(MOSFET)} + (\phi_{RE} - \psi_0 - \chi_{sol} - \phi_{Si}) - \phi_{MS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification using the Reference Electrode Potential:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH(ISFET)} &= V_{TH(MOSFET)} - \psi_0 + E_{ref}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_{ref}$ is a **constant** contribution from the reference electrode, ISM, and semiconductor.

**Step 5 — Final Form (KTU Board Standard):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH(ISFET)} &= V_{TH(MOSFET)} - \psi_0 + E_{ref} \\
\text{with} \quad \psi_0 &= 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF}(pH_{pzc} - pH)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- As pH **increases** (more basic), $\psi_0$ becomes **more negative**, so $V_{TH(ISFET)}$ **increases**.
- As pH **decreases** (more acidic), $\psi_0$ becomes **more positive**, so $V_{TH(ISFET)}$ **decreases**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: pH Sensitivity of an ISFET (Nernstian Slope)

**Step 1 — Differentiate the surface potential with respect to pH:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \psi_0}{\partial pH} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial pH} \left[ 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF}(pH_{pzc} - pH) \right] \\
&= 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF} \cdot \frac{\partial (pH_{pzc} - pH)}{\partial pH} \\
&= 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF} \cdot (-1) \\
&= -2.303 \frac{RT}{zF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the threshold voltage with respect to pH (since $V_{TH(MOSFET)}$ and $E_{ref}$ are pH-independent):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial V_{TH(ISFET)}}{\partial pH} &= -\frac{\partial \psi_0}{\partial pH} \\
&= 2.303 \frac{RT}{zF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation at T = 298 K, z = 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{pH} &= 2.303 \times \frac{8.314 \times 298}{1 \times 96485} \\
&= 2.303 \times \frac{2477.572}{96485} \\
&= 2.303 \times 0.02568 \\
&\approx 0.05916 \, V/pH
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S_{pH} &= 2.303 \frac{RT}{F} \approx 59.2 \, mV/pH \quad \text{at } 25\,^\circ C
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is the **Nernstian slope** — the theoretical maximum pH sensitivity of an ISFET.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common ISFET Membranes

| Membrane Material | Target Ion / Analyte | pH Range | Sensitivity (mV/pH) | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SiO₂** | pH (H⁺) | 4 – 10 | 25 – 40 | Poor |
| **Si₃N₄** | pH (H⁺) | 2 – 12 | 45 – 56 | Moderate |
| **Al₂O₃** | pH (H⁺) | 2 – 12 | 50 – 58 | Good |
| **Ta₂O₅** | pH (H⁺) | 1 – 13 | 56 – 59 | Excellent |
| **PVC + Ionophore (Valinomycin)** | K⁺ | — | 50 – 58 mV/decade | Moderate |
| **PVC + Ionophore (ETH 1001)** | Ca²⁺ | — | 25 – 30 mV/decade | Moderate |
| **Glass (Na₂O–CaO–SiO₂)** | Na⁺, H⁺ | — | 50 – 59 mV/decade | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
