## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for Ion-Selective Biosensors

The Nernst equation governs the potential response of potentiometric biosensors (e.g., urea, pH-based sensors).

**Starting Point (Chemical Thermodynamics):**

The Gibbs free energy change for an electrochemical reaction is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$

For an electrochemical cell, $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$.

Therefore:

$$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging for $E$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

For a generic reaction $Ox + ne^- \rightarrow Red$, the reaction quotient $Q = [Red]/[Ox]$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$$

**Step-by-Step Evaluation at 25 °C (298 K):**

Substituting $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$, $T = 298 \text{ K}$:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569 \text{ V}$$

Converting from natural log to log base 10 (multiply by 2.303):

$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$$

This is the **working Nernst equation** at 25 °C. For potentiometric biosensors, the slope is typically **±59.2 mV/decade** (n = 1) or **±29.6 mV/decade** (n = 2).

### 3.2 Derivation: Michaelis-Menten Kinetics for Enzyme-Based Biosensors

Enzyme kinetics defines the dynamic range and saturation behavior of enzymatic biosensors.

**Step 1: Reaction Scheme**

$$E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P$$

Where $E$ = enzyme, $S$ = substrate, $ES$ = enzyme-substrate complex, $P$ = product.

**Step 2: Steady-State Assumption**

Rate of ES formation = Rate of ES breakdown:

$$k_1 [E][S] = k_{-1}[ES] + k_{cat}[ES]$$

$$k_1 [E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES]$$

**Step 3: Define Michaelis Constant**

$$K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_1}$$

**Step 4: Conservation of Enzyme**

Total enzyme: $[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$

Free enzyme: $[E] = [E]_0 - [ES]$

**Step 5: Substitute and Solve for [ES]**

$$K_m \cdot ([E]_0 - [ES]) = [ES]$$

$$K_m [E]_0 = [ES] (K_m + [S])$$

$$[ES] = \frac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 6: Rate of Product Formation**

The initial reaction velocity is $v = k_{cat} [ES]$:

$$v = \frac{k_{cat} [E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]} = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

where $V_{max} = k_{cat} [E]_0$ is the maximum velocity at substrate saturation.

**Step 7: Linearized Form (Lineweaver-Burk)**

Taking the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

This is the **Lineweaver-Burk equation**, used experimentally to determine $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ from a straight-line plot of $1/v$ vs. $1/[S]$.

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A glucose biosensor exhibits a calibration curve with slope $S = 1.2 \,\mu A/mM$ and noise standard deviation $\sigma = 0.015 \,\mu A$. Calculate the **Limit of Detection (LOD)**.

**Solution:**

Using the formula $LOD = 3\sigma / S$:

$$LOD = \frac{3 \times 0.015 \,\mu A}{1.2 \,\mu A/mM} = \frac{0.045}{1.2} mM$$

$$LOD = 0.0375 \,mM = 37.5 \,\mu M$$

Since the normal physiological glucose range is **3.9 – 7.1 mM** (fasting) and **up to 11.1 mM** (postprandial), this sensor easily detects clinically relevant concentrations.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Common KTU Question Type)

| Feature | Electrochemical | Optical (SPR) | Piezoelectric (QCM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transduction | Current/Voltage | Refractive index | Frequency shift |
| Sensitivity | High (nM) | Very High (pM) | High (nM) |
| Response time | Seconds | Minutes | Seconds |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Portability | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| Label needed? | No (label-free possible) | No | No |
| Medical example | Glucose (GOx) | DNA hybridization | Pathogen detection |
| Environmental use | Heavy metals | Pesticide residue | BOD in wastewater |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
*List any three major medical applications of biosensors and state the biological recognition element used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Blood glucose monitoring** — Recognition element: **Glucose Oxidase (GOx)** enzyme. Used by diabetic patients for self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG).
2. **Urea detection in renal patients** — Recognition element: **Urease** enzyme. Reaction produces NH₃, measured by pH change via ISFET.
3. **PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen) cancer screening** — Recognition element: **Anti-PSA monoclonal antibody** immobilized on SPR gold chip.

*(1 mark each: name + bioreceptor + clinical relevance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Explain the principle of operation of a BOD biosensor. Mention the microorganism used and the transducer type.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Principle:** The BOD biosensor measures the **rate of oxygen consumption** by immobilized microorganisms (typically *Trichosporon cutaneum* yeast) in the presence of biodegradable organic matter in the wastewater sample.
- **Reaction:** Organic substrate + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + biomass.
- **Transducer:** **Clark-type dissolved oxygen electrode** (amperometric).
- **Signal:** The drop in dissolved O₂ concentration is proportional to BOD. Calibration is done using a standard glucose-glutamic acid (GGA) solution (BOD = **198 mg/L**).
- **Range:** 0 – 500 mg/L BOD; response time: **15 – 30 min**.

*(1 mark each: principle, organism, transducer + signal interpretation)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Students answer **ONE** of the two given choices.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Electrochemical & Clinical Biosensors*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Module 3 Choice 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **amperometric glucose biosensor** based on glucose oxidase. Explain the role of the mediator and the Clark oxygen electrode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Michaelis-Menten equation** for enzyme kinetics and explain its significance in biosensor calibration. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A three-electrode electrochemical cell: **Working electrode (Pt or screen-printed carbon)**, **Reference electrode (Ag/AgCl)**, and **Counter electrode (Pt wire)**.
- **Glucose oxidase (GOx)** is immobilized on the working electrode using a polymer matrix (e.g., Nafion, glutaraldehyde cross-linking, or sol-gel entrapment).
- The electrode is housed in a small flow cell or disposable strip.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The biochemical reaction at the enzyme layer:
  $$\text{Glucose} + O_2 \xrightarrow{GOx} \text{Gluconolactone} + H_2O_2$$
- A constant potential of **+0.6 V to +0.7 V** (vs. Ag/AgCl) is applied to the working electrode.
- The **H₂O₂** generated is electrochemically oxidized at the electrode:
  $$H_2O_2 \rightarrow O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
- The resulting **anodic current** is directly proportional to the glucose concentration (Beer-Lambert / Cottrell-type behavior).
- In the **first-generation** sensor, O₂ is the natural mediator; in the **second-generation** sensor, a synthetic mediator (ferrocene, Prussian Blue) replaces O₂ to avoid oxygen dependence; the **third-generation** uses direct electron transfer without mediator.

**Role of Mediator (1 mark):**
A mediator like **ferrocene** shuttles electrons between the reduced enzyme (FADH₂) and the electrode surface, enabling low-potential operation (~+0.2 V) and reducing interference from other species like ascorbic acid and uric acid.

**(b) Model Solution:**

See detailed derivation in **Section 3.2** above. Key elements to include:

1. State the reaction: $E + S \xrightleftharpoons{k_1, k_{-1}} ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P$ (1 mark)
2. Apply steady-state assumption: $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$ (1 mark)
3. Derive $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})/k_1$ and conservation $[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$ (2 marks)
4. Final Michaelis-Menten equation: $v = V_{max} [S] / (K_m + [S])$ (2 marks)
5. **Significance:** $K_m$ defines the substrate concentration at half-saturation; lower $K_m$ → higher sensor sensitivity. The **linear range** of the biosensor is typically $[S] < 0.1 K_m$. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Optical, Immunosensors & Environmental Applications*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Module 3 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) biosensor** principle with a labeled diagram. Discuss any two **medical and two environmental applications** of SPR biosensors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, describe the application of biosensors in **(i)** food safety and **(ii)** defense/bioterrorism. Mention the recognition elements used in each case. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**SPR Principle (3 marks):**
- SPR occurs at a **metal-dielectric interface** (typically gold film ~50 nm) when **p-polarized light** is incident through a glass prism at a specific angle (SPR angle).
- At the SPR angle, surface plasmons are excited, causing a sharp drop in reflected light intensity.
- When biomolecules bind to the gold surface (pre-functionalized with antibodies/DNA), the **refractive index** of the dielectric layer changes, shifting the SPR angle.
- This angle shift ($\Delta \theta_{SPR}$) is proportional to the bound mass and is detected in real-time, **label-free**.

**Mathematical relation (2 marks):**

$$\Delta \theta_{SPR} = S \cdot \Delta n \cdot d$$

where $S$ = sensitivity factor, $\Delta n$ = refractive index change, $d$ = thickness of the adsorbed layer.

**Medical Applications (1 mark each):**
1. **Detection of cancer biomarkers** (e.g., PSA, CEA) — using anti-PSA antibodies on gold chip.
2. **Drug-target interaction studies** — real-time binding kinetics ($k_{on}$, $k_{off}$) of pharmaceuticals to membrane receptors.

**Environmental Applications (1 mark each):**
1. **Pesticide detection** (atrazine, organophosphates) — competitive immunoassay format.
2. **Heavy metal ion detection** (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) — using DNA-aptamer-functionalized gold surfaces.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**(i) Food Safety Applications (3.5 marks):**

| Target | Recognition Element | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Salmonella | Anti-Salmonella antibody | Poultry, eggs (rapid screening <2h) |
| Listeria monocytogenes | Antibody | Ready-to-eat meat products |
| Aflatoxin B1 | Monoclonal antibody | Peanuts, maize, spices (regulatory limit **20 µg/kg**) |
| E. coli O157:H7 | Antibody | Beef and dairy |
| Biogenic amines (histamine) | Diamine oxidase enzyme | Fish spoilage detection |
| Lactose | β-galactosidase | Dairy industry quality control |

**Example Working Principle:** A typical amperometric immunosensor for *Salmonella* uses alkaline phosphatase enzyme-labeled secondary antibody, with p-nitrophenyl phosphate as substrate; the current generated is proportional to the pathogen concentration.

**(ii) Defense and Bioterrorism Applications (3.5 marks):**

| Biothreat Agent | Recognition Element | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|
| *Bacillus anthracis* (anthrax) | Antibody against protective antigen (PA) | SPR or amperometric |
| Smallpox (Variola) | Antibody | Fluorescence immunosensor |
| Ricin (protein toxin) | Aptamer or antibody | Piezoelectric (QCM) |
| Botulinum toxin A | Antibody | Lateral flow + electrochemical |
| Organophosphate nerve agents | Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme | pH-ISFET / amperometric |
| TNT / RDX explosives | Antibody or molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) | SPR / QCM |

> **AChE Inhibition Mechanism (commonly asked):**
> $$AChE \text{ (active)} \xrightarrow{\text{Organophosphate}} AChE \text{ (inhibited)}$$
> The degree of inhibition is proportional to the nerve agent concentration. Detection limit: **~10 nM**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating recognition element with transducer** (e.g., writing "GOx is a transducer" — it is the *bioreceptor*; the electrode is the *transducer*). *(−1 mark)*
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (LOD, sensitivity, BOD). Always write `mM`, `µA/mM`, `mg/L`. *(−0.5 to −1 mark)*
> 3. **Missing the mediator's role** in 2nd-generation glucose sensors. Examiners specifically check this. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Wrong Nernst slope**: at 25 °C, the slope is **±59.2 mV/decade for monovalent ions** and **±29.6 mV/decade for divalent ions**. Many students use the wrong denominator.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram** when asked "with a neat diagram" — minimum 0.5 to 1 mark deduction.
> 6. **Writing the enzyme name but not its function** (e.g., saying "AChE" without explaining that it hydrolyzes acetylcholine). Always pair the enzyme name with its action.
> 7. **Ignoring the application context** — in a "medical" question, conclude with clinical significance (e.g., "PSA screening helps early detection of prostate cancer").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Reference Checklist (Bookmark This!):**
>
> - ✅ A **biosensor = bioreceptor + transducer + signal processor**.
> - ✅ **Glucose Oxidase (GOx)** is the most commercially important bioreceptor — used in blood glucose strips.
> - ✅ **First-generation glucose sensors** use O₂ as natural mediator; **second-generation** use ferrocene/Prussian Blue; **third-generation** use direct electron transfer.
> - ✅ The **Nernst slope at 25 °C** is **59.2 mV/decade** (n = 1) — memorize this!
> - ✅ The **Michaelis-Menten equation** is $v = V_{max}[S] / (K_m + [S])$ — defines the linear dynamic range of enzymatic biosensors.
> - ✅ **LOD = 3σ/S** — for noise-limited detection.
> - ✅ **BOD biosensor** uses *Trichosporon cutaneum* yeast + Clark oxygen electrode.
> - ✅ **AChE inhibition** → organophosphate pesticide/nerve agent detection.
> - ✅ **SPR biosensors** are **label-free** and use **gold film + prism** geometry.
> - ✅ **PSA, CEA, AFP** are the most commonly screened cancer biomarkers by immunosensors.
> - ✅ **Aflatoxin B1 limit** in food = **20 µg/kg**; **Pb²⁺ in water** = **10 µg/L** (WHO).
> - ✅ **Wearable sweat biosensors** monitor glucose, lactate, and electrolytes in real time.
> - ✅ **Major commercial biosensors**: Blood glucose strips, pregnancy tests (hCG), CGMs, COVID-19 rapid antigen kits.
> - ✅ **Transducer types**: Amperometric, Potentiometric, Optical (SPR/fluorescence), Piezoelectric (QCM), Calorimetric.
> - ✅ **Market size**: Medical biosensors dominate (>60 %); global market projected to exceed **$50 billion by 2030**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Applications of Biosensors (Module 3)**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PEERT413 Biomedical Signals & Transducers*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biosensors for Clinical Diagnostics, Biomedical Transducers and Electrodes: Temperature Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biosensors for Clinical Diagnostics, Biomedical Transducers and Electrodes: Temperature Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: BIOMEDICAL TRANSDUCERS, ELECTRODES & TEMPERATURE SENSING

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Biosensors — The Molecular Detectives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **biosensor** is an analytical device that integrates a **biological recognition element** (enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid, cell, or tissue) with a **physicochemical transducer** (electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric) to detect a specific **analyte** and convert that biochemical interaction into a measurable electrical signal proportional to analyte concentration.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Lock and Key with a Megaphone":**
Imagine a tiny security guard (biological element) stationed at a door. He can recognize only one specific person (analyte) by the shape of their key. When the right person arrives, the guard shouts into a megaphone (transducer) that converts his whisper into a loud, measurable sound (electrical signal). The louder the shout, the more people arrived. That's exactly how a **glucose biosensor** works — the enzyme **glucose oxidase** recognizes glucose, and an electrode amplifies the chemical reaction into microamperes of current that a glucometer displays as mg/dL.

### 1.2 Biomedical Transducers — The Universal Translators

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A **biomedical transducer** is a device that converts one form of biological energy (chemical, mechanical, thermal, optical) into an electrical signal that can be processed, displayed, and recorded for diagnostic or monitoring purposes.

**Analogy — The Multilingual Diplomat:**
Just as a UN translator converts French speech into English text, a biomedical transducer converts the body's silent "language" — be it a heartbeat's vibration, blood's warmth, or muscle's electrical activity — into the universal "language" of voltage and current that computers and oscilloscopes can understand.

### 1.3 Electrodes — The Body's Electrical Antennas

**Definition:** An **electrode** is a conductive interface (usually a metal–electrolyte junction) that either *delivers* electrical current into tissue (**stimulating electrode**) or *extracts* bioelectric potentials from tissue (**recording electrode**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** In clinical practice, **Ag/AgCl (Silver/Silver-Chloride)** electrodes are the gold standard for ECG, EEG, and EMG recording due to their stable half-cell potential of **+0.222 V** vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) at **25 °C**.

### 1.4 Temperature Transducers — The Thermal Watchmen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **temperature transducer** is a device whose measurable electrical property (resistance, voltage, or current) varies in a **reproducible, monotonic, and well-defined** manner with temperature, allowing precise conversion of thermal energy into an electrical output signal.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Thermometer That Texts You:**
A mercury thermometer is a *visual* device — you must look at it. A temperature transducer is a *smart* thermometer — it continuously converts the warmth of a patient's skin into an electrical signal that can be sent wirelessly to a nurse's phone, plotted on a monitor, or stored in a database. It is the fundamental sensing element behind **fever monitoring in ICUs, infant incubators, and cryosurgery probes**.

### 1.5 Classification of Temperature Transducers

| Type | Active / Passive | Output | Typical Range (°C) | Medical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermocouple (TC)** | Active | Voltage (mV) | −270 to +2300 | Hyperthermia probes, skin temp |
| **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** | Passive | Resistance (Ω) | −200 to +850 | Core body temp, blood bath |
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Passive | Resistance (Ω) | −55 to +150 | Digital thermometers, incubators |
| **Semiconductor (IC sensor)** | Active | Voltage/Current | −55 to +150 | Wearables, disposable patches |
| **Infrared (Pyrometer)** | Passive | Voltage | −50 to +3000 | Tympanic (ear) thermometers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Active vs Passive — KTU High-Yield Concept:** *Active transducers* are **self-generating** (they produce voltage from energy they harvest — e.g., thermocouple via Seebeck effect). *Passive transducers* require an **external excitation** (current or voltage source) to operate — e.g., RTD needs current injection to develop a measurable voltage across its resistance.

### 1.6 Physical Constants to Memorize

- **Standard Reference Temperature (IEC 60584):** **0 °C (273.15 K)** for thermocouple cold-junction tables.
- **Ice-Point of Water:** Exactly **0.01 °C = 273.16 K** (triple point).
- **Nominal RTD Resistance (PT100):** Exactly **100.00 Ω at 0 °C**.
- **Thermistor β-constant (typical NTC):** **3000 K to 5000 K**.
- **Seebeck Coefficient for Type K TC:** Approximately **41 μV/°C** at 25 °C.
- **Normal Human Core Body Temperature:** **37.0 °C (98.6 °F)**, range **36.1 – 37.2 °C**.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (for IR sensors):** **σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**.
- **Body Emissivity (skin):** ε ≈ **0.98** (near-black-body).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Biosensor — Architecture & Working Principle

A biosensor comprises three cascaded functional blocks:

1. **Bioreceptor (Recognition Layer):** Selectively binds the target analyte. Examples:
   - *Enzymatic* (e.g., Glucose Oxidase — GOx for glucose)
   - *Immunological* (Antibody–Antigen, e.g., for pregnancy hCG)
   - *Nucleic Acid* (DNA probes for pathogens)
   - *Whole Cell* (microbial biosensors for BOD)
2. **Transducer (Interface Layer):** Converts the biorecognition event into a quantifiable signal.
3. **Signal Processor (Electronic Layer):** Amplifies, filters, digitizes, and displays the result.

### 2.2 Classification of Biosensors by Transducer Type

- **Electrochemical Biosensor:** Amperometric (measures current at fixed potential), Potentiometric (measures potential at zero current), Conductometric (measures conductance change).
- **Optical Biosensor:** Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR), Fluorescence, Chemiluminescence.
- **Piezoelectric Biosensor:** Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) — mass change → frequency shift.
- **Calorimetric / Thermal Biosensor:** Enzyme reaction enthalpy detected by thermistor.
- **Field-Effect Transistor (FET) Based:** ISFET (Ion-Sensitive FET) for pH and ion detection.

### 2.3 Thermocouples — The Seebeck Effect

**Principle:** When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined at a hot junction while the other ends (cold junction) are held at a reference temperature, a continuous EMF is generated proportional to the temperature difference.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$V_{AB} = \int_{T_{C}}^{T_{H}} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] \, dT = \int_{T_{C}}^{T_{H}} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

where:
- $V_{AB}$ = Seebeck voltage (Volts)
- $S_A(T), S_B(T)$ = Seebeck coefficients of metals A and B (V/°C)
- $S_{AB}(T)$ = Relative Seebeck coefficient
- $T_H$ = Hot (measuring) junction temperature
- $T_C$ = Cold (reference) junction temperature

**Approximation (when Seebeck coefficient is roughly constant over the range):**

$$V_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

**Standard Thermocouple Types (IEC 60584):**

| Type | Materials | Range (°C) | Seebeck Coeff. (μV/°C) | Color Code (IEC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **J** | Iron / Constantan | 0 to 750 | ~50 | Black |
| **K** | Chromel / Alumel | −270 to 1370 | ~41 | Green |
| **T** | Copper / Constantan | −270 to 400 | ~40 | Brown |
| **E** | Chromel / Constantan | −270 to 900 | ~68 | Violet |
| **R** | Pt–13%Rh / Pt | −50 to 1768 | ~10 | Orange |
| **S** | Pt–10%Rh / Pt | −50 to 1768 | ~10 | Orange |
| **B** | Pt–30%Rh / Pt–6%Rh | 0 to 1820 | ~9 | Grey |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** The thermocouple measures $T_H - T_C$, NOT absolute $T_H$. In modern instruments, the cold junction is physically placed on a **precision semiconductor temperature sensor** (e.g., LM35) whose output is mathematically added to compensate. This is called *software CJC* or *hardware CJC*.

### 2.4 Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)

**Principle:** The electrical resistance of a pure metal increases *linearly and reproducibly* with temperature.

**Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (for Platinum RTD, IEC 60751):**

For $T \geq 0$ °C:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right]$$

For $T < 0$ °C:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 + C \cdot (T - 100) \cdot T^3 \right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = Resistance at temperature $T$ (Ω)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at 0 °C (= 100 Ω for PT100)
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ °C⁻¹
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ °C⁻²
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ °C⁻⁴ (only for T < 0)

**Simplified Linear Approximation (for small ΔT around 0 °C):**

$$R_T \approx R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot T)$$

where **α (temperature coefficient of resistance)** for platinum = **0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** (IEC 60751 standard).

**3-Wire and 4-Wire RTD Connections (Lead-Wire Compensation):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3-wire RTD?** A 2-wire RTD suffers from lead-resistance error (each copper lead can add 2–3 Ω → temperature error of 5–7 °C). A 3-wire configuration uses one extra lead to measure lead resistance and subtract it via a Wheatstone bridge. The **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** is the most accurate — it separates excitation current leads from voltage-sensing leads, completely eliminating lead and contact resistance errors.

### 2.5 Thermistors (Thermally Sensitive Resistors)

**Two Major Types:**
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **decreases** with rising temperature (semiconductor oxides like Mn, Ni, Co).
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **increases** sharply near a *Curie temperature* (used as resettable fuses).

**Steinhart-Hart Equation (high-accuracy NTC model):**

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^3$$

where T is in **Kelvin**, R in **Ω**, and A, B, C are Steinhart-Hart coefficients specific to the thermistor batch.

**Simplified Exponential β-Model (most common in KTU problems):**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = Resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (K)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298.15 K = 25 °C)
- $\beta$ = Material constant (typically 3000–5000 K)

**Sensitivity Comparison (KTU High-Yield Table):**

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | NTC Thermistor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity (dV/dT or dR/dT) | 10–80 μV/°C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | −100 to −1000 Ω/°C |
| Linearity | Moderate (polynomial) | Excellent (near-linear) | Poor (logarithmic) |
| Accuracy | ±0.5 to ±2 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±0.05 to ±0.5 °C |
| Range | Very wide | Wide | Narrow |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High | Very low |
| Medical Use | Surface probes, RF heating | Blood/critical care | Digital thermometers |

### 2.6 Semiconductor IC Temperature Sensors

**Working Principle:** Two identical transistors operated at different current densities exhibit a $\Delta V_{BE}$ that is **Proportional To Absolute Temperature (PTAT)**.

$$V_{PTAT} = \frac{k_B \cdot T}{q} \cdot \ln(N)$$

where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $q$ is electron charge, and $N$ is the ratio of current densities.

**Common ICs:** LM35 (10 mV/°C), TMP36 (10 mV/°C), DS18B20 (digital, 1-Wire protocol).

### 2.7 Infrared (Non-Contact) Temperature Sensors — Tympanic Thermometers

**Principle — Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**

$$P = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

where:
- $P$ = Radiated power (W)
- $\varepsilon$ = Surface emissivity (0 to 1)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $A$ = Surface area (m²)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

The tympanic thermometer measures IR radiation from the **eardrum (tympanic membrane)**, which shares blood supply with the **hypothalamus** — the body's true temperature control center — making it an excellent proxy for core body temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Tympanic, Not Axillary?** The eardrum is the only superficial site that reflects *core* (hypothalamic) temperature, because both are perfused by the same carotid artery. Axillary (underarm) readings are typically 0.5–1.0 °C *lower* than true core temperature.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Table

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{AB} = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck voltage | Thermocouple basic |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T]$ | Linear RTD approx. | Quick platinum |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0[1+AT+BT^2]$ | Quadratic Callendar | PT100 ≥ 0 °C |
| 4 | $R_T = R_T e^{\beta(1/T-1/T_0)}$ | β-equation | NTC thermistor |
| 5 | $1/T = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart-Hart | High-prec NTC |
| 6 | $V_{PTAT} = (kT/q)\ln N$ | Bandgap reference | IC sensors |
| 7 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann | IR/pyrometers |
| 8 | $\Delta R = 2 R_L$ | Lead wire error | 2-wire vs 4-wire |
| 9 | $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln a$ | Nernst equation | Ag/AgCl electrode |
| 10 | $I = nFAkC$ | Cottrell-like | Amperometric biosensor |

### 2.9 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

- **Closed-Loop Infant Incubators:** NTC thermistor feedback controls the radiant heater to maintain 36.5 °C for neonates.
- **MRI-Compatible Fiber-Optic Thermometry:** Uses a GaAs crystal whose optical absorption edge shifts with temperature — NO electrical wires in the magnetic field!
- **Hypothermia/Hyperthermia Blankets:** RTD-based feedback for cardiac surgery.
- **Pandemic-Grade Infrared Forehead Thermometers:** Stefan-Boltzmann based, mass screening.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Seebeck Effect (Thermocouple EMF)

**Starting Postulate (Thomson's Formalization):**

When a temperature gradient $\nabla T$ exists along a conductor, an EMF is induced. The local Seebeck EMF generated in a small length $dx$ of a single conductor is:

$$dE_A = -S_A(T) \cdot \frac{dT}{dx} \cdot dx = -S_A(T) \, dT$$

**Total EMF in a Closed Thermoelectric Loop (Metals A and B, with junctions at $T_H$ and $T_C$):**

For the full circuit, we integrate around the loop. The two arms contribute oppositely:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_B(T) \, dT - \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_A(T) \, dT$$

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] dT = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

This is the **fundamental thermocouple equation**. For small ΔT, if $S_{AB}$ is constant:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem):**

> *A Type-K thermocouple has a Seebeck coefficient of 41 μV/°C. If the hot junction is at 250 °C and the cold junction is at 25 °C, calculate the output EMF.*

**Step 1 —** Identify the variables.

$S_{AB} = 41 \times 10^{-6}$ V/°C, $\quad T_H = 250$ °C, $\quad T_C = 25$ °C

**Step 2 —** Compute ΔT.

$$\Delta T = T_H - T_C = 250 - 25 = 225 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 —** Apply linear approximation.

$$E_{AB} = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times 225 = 9225 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 —** Final answer.

$$\boxed{E_{AB} = 9.225 \text{ mV}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of RTD Temperature from Resistance (PT100 Inverse)

**Given:** $R_T = R_0 [1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2]$

**Solve for T (quadratic in T):**

$$B \cdot T^2 + A \cdot T + (1 - R_T/R_0) = 0$$

**Applying the quadratic formula:**

$$T = \frac{-A + \sqrt{A^2 - 4B(1 - R_T/R_0)}}{2B}$$

(The positive root is taken because T > 0.)

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> *A PT100 RTD reads 138.50 Ω. Find the temperature.*

**Step 1 —** Identify constants.

$R_0 = 100.00$ Ω, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$

**Step 2 —** Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{138.50}{100.00} = 1.3850$$

$$1 - \frac{R_T}{R_0} = 1 - 1.3850 = -0.3850$$

**Step 3 —** Discriminant inside the square root.

$$A^2 = (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.5274 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$-4B(1 - R_T/R_0) = -4 \cdot (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (-0.3850) = -8.8935 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\Delta = 1.5274 \times 10^{-5} - 8.8935 \times 10^{-7} = 1.4385 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 —** Square root of discriminant.

$$\sqrt{\Delta} = \sqrt{1.4385 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.7928 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 —** Final T.

$$T = \frac{-3.9083 \times 10^{-3} + 3.7928 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$T = \frac{-0.1155 \times 10^{-3}}{-1.1550 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1155 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1550 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{T = 100.0 \text{ °C}}$$

This matches the standard reference: at 100 °C, the PT100 reads exactly **138.50 Ω** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of NTC Thermistor Resistance at a New Temperature

**Given β-model:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$

**Worked Example:**

> *An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at $T_0 = 298.15$ K and $\beta = 3950$ K. Find its resistance at body temperature 37 °C = 310.15 K.*

**Step 1 —** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.

$T_0 = 298.15$ K, $\quad T = 37 + 273.15 = 310.15$ K

**Step 2 —** Compute the inverse temperature difference.

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$\frac{1}{310.15} = 3.2243 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\text{Difference} = 3.2243 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -1.297 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the exponent.

$$\beta \cdot \Delta(1/T) = 3950 \cdot (-1.297 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.51232$$

**Step 4 —** Compute exponential.

$$e^{-0.51232} = 0.5990$$

**Step 5 —** Final resistance.

$$R_T = 10000 \cdot 0.5990 = 5990 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R_{37°C} \approx 5.99 \text{ kΩ}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** A mere 12 °C rise (25 → 37) caused a **40% resistance drop**. This *extreme sensitivity* is why thermistors dominate digital medical thermometers — small temperature changes produce large, easily measured resistance swings.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Decision Tree for Medical Temperature Sensing

| Clinical Scenario | Recommended Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Core body temp in ICU | RTD (PT100) | High accuracy ±0.1 °C, stable |
| Disposable digital oral thermometer | NTC Thermistor | Cheap, small, fast |
| Tympanic (ear) thermometer | IR Pyrometer | Non-contact, fast (1 s), hygienic |
| Skin surface during surgery | Type-T Thermocouple | Small, fast response, Cu–Cn biocompatible |
| Neonatal incubator | NTC + heater feedback | High sensitivity, low cost |
| MRI environment | Fiber-optic (GaAs) | No metallic wires, MRI-safe |
| Cryosurgery probe | Type-K or Type-T TC | Wide range, fast response |
| Continuous wearable patch | LM35 / TMP36 IC | Low power, linear, integrated |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Biosensor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Analyte Sample"] --> A2["Bioreceptor Layer"]
    A2 --> A3["Transducer Interface"]
    A3 --> A4["Signal Amplifier"]
    A4 --> A5["ADC and Processor"]
    A5 --> A6["Display or Monitor"]
    A2 -.->|Recognition Event| A3
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Glucose Amperometric Biosensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Blood Droplet"] --> B2["GOx Enzyme Membrane"]
    B2 --> B3["Mediator Fe CN 6"]
    B3 --> B4["Platinum Working Electrode"]
    B4 --> B5["Current I proportional to Glucose"]
    B5 --> B6["Potentiostat Circuit"]
    B6 --> B7["Microcontroller"]
    B7 --> B8["LCD mg per dL Display"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction at B2:** $\text{Glucose} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{Gluconolactone} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$
> The hydrogen peroxide is oxidized at +0.7 V (vs Ag/AgCl), and the resulting current is proportional to glucose concentration (Clark's principle).

## 4.3 Schematic — Thermocouple Circuit with Cold-Junction Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Hot Junction T hot"] --> C2["Metal A Lead"]
    C2 --> C3["Cold Junction T cold"]
    C3 --> C4["Metal B Lead"]
    C4 --> C5["Amplifier"]
    C5 --> C6["ADC"]
    C6 --> C7["Microcontroller"]
    C8["Semiconductor IC Sensor LM35 at cold junction"] --> C7
    C7 --> C9["Compensated T hot display"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — 4-Wire (Kelvin) RTD Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["Current Source I plus"] --> D2["PT100 Element"]
    D2 --> D3["Current Return I minus"]
    D4["Voltmeter V high"] --> D2
    D5["Voltmeter V low"] --> D2
    D4 --> D6["High Impedance Voltmeter"]
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> D7["Microohm Resolution Resistance to T"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4-Wire is Best:** Because the voltmeter has >10 MΩ input impedance, virtually *zero* current flows through the sense leads, so $V = I \cdot R_{\text{PT100}}$ exactly — no $IR_{\text{lead}}$ error.

## 4.5 Flowchart — NTC Thermistor Signal Conditioning (Wheatstone Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Excitation V plus 5V"] --> E2["Bridge Top Resistor R1"]
    E2 --> E3["Node A"]
    E3 --> E4["RTD NTC R thermistor"]
    E4 --> E5["Node B"]
    E5 --> E6["R2 reference"]
    E6 --> E7["R3 reference"]
    E3 --> E8["Instrumentation Amplifier"]
    E5 --> E8
    E8 --> E9["ADC"]
    E9 --> E10["Microcontroller Display"]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — IR Tympanic Thermometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["Tympanic Membrane Emits IR Radiation"] --> F2["Optical Lens Concentrates Beam"]
    F2 --> F3["Thermopile Detector 6 to 60 Junctions"]
    F3 --> F4["Microvolt Signal nV range"]
    F4 --> F5["Low Noise Amplifier with Chopper"]
    F5 --> F6["ADC 16 bit"]
    F6 --> F7["Reference Black Body Calibration Lookup"]
    F7 --> F8["LCD Temperature deg C in 1 second"]
```

## 4.7 Architecture Flow — Body Temperature Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["Patient Skin / Tympanic"] --> G2["Temperature Sensor"]
    G2 --> G3["Signal Conditioner"]
    G3 --> G4["Microcontroller with PID"]
    G4 --> G5["Heater or Cooler Driver"]
    G5 --> G6["Incubator or Blanket"]
    G6 -.->|Feedback| G1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Implementation:** This is exactly the architecture used in **neonatal incubators** and **hypothermia therapy units** — the sensor is the "eye," the microcontroller is the "brain," and the heater is the "muscle." The PID loop maintains ±0.1 °C stability over hours.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define a biosensor. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an amperometric glucose biosensor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **biosensor** is an analytical device that couples a *biological recognition element* (enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid) with a *physicochemical transducer* to detect a specific analyte and produce a measurable electrical signal proportional to the analyte's concentration. **[1 mark]**

**Block Diagram of Glucose Biosensor:**

The biological element is **Glucose Oxidase (GOx)** immobilized on a membrane. The transducer is a **Platinum working electrode** maintained at +0.7 V (vs Ag/AgCl reference) by a potentiostat. **[1 mark]**

**Working:** When blood contacts the GOx membrane, the following reaction occurs:

$$\text{Glucose} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{Gluconolactone} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$$

The H₂O₂ is oxidized at the Pt electrode:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt, +0.7 V}} \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

The resulting current ($I = nFAkC$) is **directly proportional to glucose concentration**. The signal is amplified and displayed as mg/dL. **[1 mark]**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the Seebeck effect. With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Type-K thermocouple used for skin temperature measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar metals are joined at two junctions held at *different temperatures*, an electromotive force (EMF) is generated in the loop, proportional to the temperature difference between the junctions. The EMF is given by $V = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$. **[1 mark]**

**Construction of Type-K Thermocouple:** It consists of two wires:
- **Chromel** (Ni–Cr alloy, ~90% Ni, 10% Cr) — positive leg
- **Alumel** (Ni–Al alloy, ~95% Ni, 2% Al, 2% Mn, 1% Si) — negative leg
The two wires are welded at one end to form the *hot (measuring) junction*, placed on the skin. The other end is the *cold (reference) junction* held at 0 °C (or compensated by an IC sensor). **[1 mark]**

**Working for Skin Temperature:** The hot junction senses skin temperature (~33 °C). The cold junction is at 25 °C. The Seebeck EMF generated is approximately $41 \,\mu\text{V/°C} \times 8\,\text{°C} \approx 328 \,\mu\text{V}$. This tiny voltage is amplified by a precision instrumentation amplifier, digitized, and displayed. The thermoelectric current, if short-circuited, also follows the **Peltier effect** in reverse. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — Choice 1 [14 Marks Total]**

> **Question A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the classification of biomedical transducers. Describe the principle and working of an **Ag/AgCl electrode** used in ECG recording. How is the half-cell potential related to the Nernst equation? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A biomedical transducer converts biological energy (chemical, mechanical, thermal) into an electrical signal.

**Classification Table (4 marks):**

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| Active (self-generating) | Piezoelectric, Thermocouple, Photovoltaic |
| Passive (externally excited) | RTD, Thermistor, LVDT, Strain gauge |
| Electrochemical | Ag/AgCl, pH electrode |
| Biosensor | Glucose oxidase, SPR |

**Ag/AgCl Electrode — Working (2 marks):**

A silver wire is coated with a thin layer of AgCl. It is immersed in a KCl electrolyte (saturated or 3M) inside a housing with a porous junction contacting the skin.

**Half-cell Reactions:**

$$\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^-$$

The half-cell potential is given by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{AgCl}}}{a_{\text{Ag}} \cdot a_{\text{Cl}^-}}\right) = E^0 - \frac{RT}{F} \ln(a_{\text{Cl}^-})$$

where $E^0 = +0.222$ V at 25 °C, $n=1$, $F = 96485$ C/mol. The Ag/AgCl electrode has a *stable, non-polarizable* junction — current can flow without changing the potential — making it ideal for ECG. **[1 mark]**

---

> **Question A (b) [7 Marks]:** Derive the working principle of a **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)**. A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100$ Ω at 0 °C. Using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation, find its resistance at **150 °C**. Given: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ °C⁻¹, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ °C⁻². **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** The resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature due to increased lattice vibration (phonon) scattering of conduction electrons. For platinum, the relationship is given by the Callendar-Van Dusen equation:

$$R_T = R_0 [1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2]$$

**Step 1 —** Substitute $R_0 = 100$ Ω, $T = 150$ °C, $A, B$ as given. **[1 mark]**

$$R_{150} = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(150) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(150)^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $A \cdot T$.

$$A \cdot T = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 = 0.5862$$

**Step 3 —** Compute $B \cdot T^2$.

$$B \cdot T^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 22500 = -0.0130$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute and simplify.

$$R_{150} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.5862 - 0.0130 \right] = 100 \times 1.5732$$

$$\boxed{R_{150} = 157.32 \text{ Ω}}$$

**Verification with IEC 60751 Table:** The standard reference value is **157.31 Ω at 150 °C** ✓ (matches to 4 significant figures). **[4 marks for calculation steps]**

---

### **QUESTION B — Choice 2 [14 Marks Total]**

> **Question B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** of black-body radiation. Describe the construction and working of a **tympanic (ear) infrared thermometer** used in clinical practice. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law (3 marks):** Every object with temperature above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic (thermal) radiation. The total radiant power per unit area is:

$$P/A = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, $\varepsilon$ is emissivity (0 to 1), and $T$ is absolute temperature. The **$T^4$ dependence** means even a small temperature change produces a *large* change in radiated power — ideal for sensitive non-contact measurement.

**Tympanic Thermometer — Construction (2 marks):**
- A tapered probe (to fit the ear canal).
- An **optical lens** (often silicon) that focuses IR radiation from the tympanic membrane onto a detector.
- A **thermopile detector** — a series of 6 to 60 tiny thermocouples connected in series, producing a microvolt-level signal proportional to incident IR power.
- A reference temperature sensor for ambient compensation.

**Working (2 marks):** The probe is inserted into the ear canal. The lens focuses IR emitted by the eardrum (emissivity ≈ 0.98, same as skin) onto the thermopile. The thermopile generates an EMF:

$$V = N \cdot S_{AB} \cdot (T_{\text{eardrum}} - T_{\text{reference}})$$

This voltage is amplified (typically by a **chopper-stabilized amplifier** to suppress 1/f noise), digitized, and the microcontroller looks up the equivalent temperature from a factory-calibrated lookup table. Total measurement time: **1 second** with accuracy **±0.1 °C**.

---

> **Question B (b) [7 Marks]:** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 10$ kΩ and $\beta = 3950$ K. Calculate its resistance at **37 °C** and at **0 °C**. Comment on its suitability for a **digital clinical thermometer**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The β-equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

with $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $R_0 = 10000$ Ω, $\beta = 3950$ K.

**Step 1 — Resistance at 37 °C = 310.15 K (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = -1.297 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta \cdot \Delta(1/T) = 3950 \cdot (-1.297 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.5123$$

$$R_{37} = 10000 \cdot e^{-0.5123} = 10000 \cdot 0.5990$$

$$\boxed{R_{37} = 5.99 \text{ kΩ}}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance at 0 °C = 273.15 K (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = (3.661 \times 10^{-3}) - (3.354 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta \cdot \Delta(1/T) = 3950 \cdot 3.07 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2127$$

$$R_{0} = 10000 \cdot e^{1.2127} = 10000 \cdot 3.363$$

$$\boxed{R_{0°C} = 33.63 \text{ kΩ}}$$

**Suitability Comment (1 mark):** Highly suitable. The resistance swing from 33.6 kΩ (0 °C) → 10 kΩ (25 °C) → 6.0 kΩ (37 °C) is *large and monotonic*, providing excellent sensitivity (±0.01 °C resolution is easy). The component is small, cheap, and can be placed at the tip of a disposable probe. The major drawback is **non-linearity**, which is corrected in firmware via the Steinhart-Hart equation.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often forget to convert °C to K in the β-equation. The NTC β-equation **REQUIRES absolute Kelvin** — submitting 37 in place of 310.15 K loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong Root in Quadratic:** In the RTD inverse problem, students sometimes take the *negative* root. Always select the root that gives a *physically meaningful* temperature in the expected range.
> 3. **Seebeck Coefficient Units:** Writing $S_{AB} = 41$ V/°C instead of $41 \times 10^{-6}$ V/°C causes the answer to be off by $10^6$. Always state the units explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting CJC:** A thermocouple answer that doesn't mention **Cold Junction Compensation** is considered incomplete by most KTU examiners — you lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **RTD Linear Approximation Validity:** The simple $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ is only valid for $|T| \lesssim 100$ °C. Beyond that, the Callendar-Van Dusen quadratic is required.
> 6. **Emissivity Assumption:** IR thermometer problems often *forget* that the **Stefan-Boltzmann** equation requires the actual *emissivity* of the target. Skin ≈ 0.98, polished metal ≈ 0.05. The difference is enormous.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Final 60-Second Revision:**

- ✅ **Biosensor** = Bioreceptor + Transducer + Processor (3 blocks).
- ✅ **Transducer Classification** = Active vs Passive; Active = self-generating (no external power).
- ✅ **Electrode half-cell potential** is governed by the **Nernst equation**: $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a$.
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl** is the gold-standard biomedical electrode, $E^0 = +0.222$ V, non-polarizable.
- ✅ **Thermocouple** uses **Seebeck effect** — voltage ∝ ΔT; needs **Cold Junction Compensation**.
- ✅ **RTD (PT100)** uses Callendar-Van Dusen: $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$; $\alpha = 0.00385$ /°C.
- ✅ **NTC Thermistor** uses β-equation: $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$; **must use Kelvin**.
- ✅ **IC sensors** (LM35) output **10 mV/°C**, highly linear, low power.
- ✅ **IR / Pyrometer** uses **Stefan-Boltzmann** $P \propto T^4$; tympanic measures eardrum (~core).
- ✅ **Sensitivity ranking:** NTC > RTD > Thermocouple (NTC is most sensitive, but least linear).
- ✅ **Medical use mapping:** Incubator → NTC; ICU core → RTD; Tympanic → IR; Surgery skin → Type-T TC; MRI → Fiber-optic.
- ✅ **Key constants:** σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴; PT100 α = 0.00385; Type-K S = 41 μV/°C.
- ✅ **Cold junction is ALWAYS at the instrument end, NOT at the patient.**
- ✅ **4-wire RTD = highest accuracy**; 2-wire = worst (lead resistance error).
- ✅ **Skin emissivity ≈ 0.98**, ear-drum shares blood with **hypothalamus** (true core).

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — PEERT413 Biomedical Signals & Transducers*

topics.title: Displacement and Pressure Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Displacement and Pressure Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Displacement Transducers — Formal Definition

A **displacement transducer** is a device that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional, measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, capacitance, or frequency). According to the KTU 2024 scheme, displacement measurement forms the foundational basis for deriving many other physiological measurements in biomedical instrumentation — including blood pressure, heart sounds, respiration, joint movement, and muscle contraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEERT413 / Module 3):**
> *Displacement transducers measure the position or movement of an object relative to a reference. They are classified as active (self-generating) or passive (require external excitation). The most common biomedical-grade displacement transducers are the Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT), the resistive potentiometric transducer, the capacitive transducer, and the eddy-current / Hall-effect transducer.*

### 1.2 Classification of Displacement Transducers

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Classification used by KTU Board Examiners:**

| Class | Energy Conversion | External Power Required? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resistive (Potentiometric)** | Mechanical → Resistance change | **Yes** (excitation voltage) | Linear/sliding pot, rotary pot |
| **Inductive (LVDT)** | Mechanical → Mutual inductance | **Yes** (AC excitation) | LVDT, RVDT |
| **Capacitive** | Mechanical → Capacitance change | **Yes** (AC excitation) | Parallel-plate, differential capacitor |
| **Eddy-Current** | Mechanical → Eddy current loss | **Yes** (AC excitation) | Proximity probes |
| **Hall-Effect** | Mechanical → Hall voltage | **Yes** (DC excitation) | Position sensors |
| **Optical** | Mechanical → Light intensity/phase | **Yes** (light source) | LVDT-style optical, encoders |
| **Piezoelectric (active)** | Mechanical → Charge | **No** | Force/acceleration sensors |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Displacement Transducers

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of a **ruler and a sliding marker**. The ruler is the *reference* (fixed frame) and the marker tells you *how far* the object has moved. A displacement transducer is the "electronic ruler" — except instead of you reading a number, the device translates the marker's position into a voltage you can read on a meter. The **LVDT** is like a *transformer whose iron core slides like a sewing-machine needle*; the more the needle moves out of the middle, the louder (higher voltage) one coil "speaks" relative to the other.

## 1.4 Pressure Transducers — Formal Definition

A **pressure transducer** is a device that converts a pressure input (force per unit area applied by a fluid, gas, or biological tissue) into a proportional electrical output. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically studies non-invasive and invasive biomedical pressure measurement (arterial, venous, intracranial, intraocular, intrauterine, and pulmonary pressures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 14.7 psi = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**
> - **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa**
> - **1 torr = 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa**
> - Biomedical pressures are typically quoted in **mmHg** (1 mmHg ≈ 133.3 Pa).

### 1.5 Classification of Pressure Transducers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Standard Classification of Pressure Transducers:**

| Class | Operating Principle | Output Nature | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strain-Gauge (Piezoresistive)** | Resistance change under strain | Active / passive | **Blood-pressure catheter** |
| **Capacitive** | Gap-area change under pressure | Passive | Intrauterine, tympanic |
| **Piezoelectric** | Charge generation by crystal deformation | **Active** (self-generating) | **Heart-sound (PCG) microphones**, ultrasonic |
| **LVDT-based** | Diaphragm coupled to LVDT core | Passive | Sphygmomanometer (aneroid type) |
| **Semiconductor piezoresistive** | Piezoresistive effect in Si | Passive | **Modern disposable BP transducers** |
| **Fiber-optic** | Pressure modulates light | Passive | MRI-safe intracranial sensors |

## 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — Pressure Transducers

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing your thumb against a **squeezable rubber bulb** connected to a digital scale. The harder you press, the more the bulb deforms, and the scale shows a larger number. A pressure transducer is exactly this — a thin **diaphragm** (the rubber bulb) that bends when pressure is applied, and inside it has tiny **strain gauges** (like microscopic rulers glued to the rubber) that change their electrical resistance as the rubber stretches. The reading is the pressure.
> 
> For **piezoelectric** pressure sensors, think of a **piezoelectric gas-lighter click** — press a crystal, get a spark. The harder you press, the bigger the spark (charge). So no battery is needed; the crystal *generates* the electricity itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Potentiometric Displacement Transducer

### Working Principle
A resistance element of total resistance $R_T$ has a sliding wiper that moves in response to input displacement $x$. The wiper divides the resistor into two parts: $R_1$ (proportional to $x$) and $R_2 = R_T - R_1$.

### Step-by-step Logic
- The element is excited by a stable DC voltage $V_{ex}$.
- The output voltage is taken between the wiper and one fixed terminal.
- As the wiper moves, the output voltage is **linearly proportional to displacement**.
- The resolution is limited by the **discrete wire-wound pitch** or the **infinite resolution of conductive-plastic tracks**.

### Advantages & Limitations (KTU-favourite topic)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-favourite advantages:** Simple, inexpensive, gives high-level output, no signal conditioning needed.
> **Examiner-favourite limitations:** Sliding contact causes **wear and noise**; limited frequency response; **finite resolution** (wire-wound type); requires a **large displacement** for measurable output (typically 1 mm to 1 m).

## 2.2 The Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)

### Construction
- One **primary winding** $P$ (centred) and two identical **secondary windings** $S_1$ and $S_2$ (wound on either side of $P$, in series-opposition).
- A movable high-permeability **ferromagnetic core** slides inside the hollow bobbin.
- Primary is excited by a sinusoidal AC voltage $V_p = V_{ex}\sin(\omega t)$.

### Working Logic
- When the core is at **NULL position** (central), EMFs induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$ are **equal and opposite** → differential output $V_o = 0$.
- When the core moves **towards $S_1$**, $S_1$ becomes more tightly coupled → its EMF increases.
- When the core moves **towards $S_2$**, $S_2$ becomes more tightly coupled → its EMF increases.
- The differential output is **linear** over a wide range of core displacements.

### Phase-Sensitive Detection
The phase of the output $V_o$ tells us the **direction** of motion (core moved up or down). A **phase-sensitive demodulator** converts this AC signal into a clean DC voltage whose polarity indicates direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-favourite LVDT highlights:**
> - **Infinite resolution** (no contact friction).
> - **Rugged**, hermetically sealed, ideal for **cardiac catheter-tipped** pressure measurement.
> - Output is **null-balanced** at centre → automatic **temperature compensation**.
> - Frequency range of excitation: typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz**.

## 2.3 Capacitive Displacement Transducer

### Working Logic
For a parallel-plate capacitor, capacitance is

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

- A **change in $d$** (gap) → inverse, **non-linear** displacement transducer.
- A **change in $A$** (overlap area) → **linear** displacement transducer.
- A **change in $\varepsilon_r$** (dielectric) → used in **liquid-level** sensors.

## 2.4 Strain-Gauge Pressure Transducer — Detailed Logic

### Piezoresistive Effect

For a strain gauge of resistance $R$:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

Under strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, the change in resistance is

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

The **Gauge Factor** $G_F$ is the figure of merit:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- For **metal gauges** (e.g., constantan), $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 0$, so $G_F \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx \mathbf{1.7 \text{ to } 2.1}$.
- For **semiconductor gauges** (Si, Ge), the piezoresistive term dominates, $G_F \approx \mathbf{100 \text{ to } 200}$.

### Wheatstone-Bridge Configuration
Four active gauges (a full bridge) on a diaphragm give a **linear, temperature-compensated** output:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \Delta R / (2R)}$$

For small $\Delta R$:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

The pressure-strain relationship for a circular diaphragm (clamped at edges) under uniform pressure $P$ is

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3P(1-\nu^2)r^2}{8Eh^2}$$

where $r$ is the diaphragm radius, $h$ its thickness, $E$ Young's modulus, and $\nu$ Poisson's ratio.

## 2.5 Piezoelectric Pressure Transducer

A piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT, PVDF) generates charge $Q$ when a force $F$ is applied across its faces:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$$

The output voltage is

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot P \cdot A}{C}$$

where:
- $d$ = **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N or m/V) — a material constant.
- $P$ = applied pressure (Pa).
- $A$ = electrode area (m²).
- $C$ = total capacitance of crystal + cable + input stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Piezoelectric sensors are **AC-coupled** — they cannot measure a **static (DC) pressure**. The charge leaks away through the input resistance. Hence piezoelectric microphones are used for **heart sounds** (acoustic, frequency-rich) and **ultrasonic Doppler** applications, not for steady arterial BP.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the master reference for solving KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems on this topic. Memorize the symbols, ranges, and typical values for full marks.**

| # | Transducer / Quantity | Governing Equation | Symbols & Units | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Potentiometer output | $V_o = V_{ex}\,(x/L)$ | $x$=displacement (m), $L$=total stroke (m) | Stroke = 1 mm – 1 m |
| 2 | LVDT output (differential) | $V_o = K\,x\,\sin(\omega t)$ | $K$=sensitivity (V/m), $x$=core displacement (m) | Linearity ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % FS |
| 3 | LVDT sensitivity | $K = V_{ex}\,N_s/N_p$ | $N_s, N_p$ = turns of secondary/primary | — |
| 4 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A/d$ | $A$=m², $d$=m | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| 5 | Capacitive change (gap) | $\Delta C / C = -\Delta d / d$ | small-gap approximation | — |
| 6 | Resistance of a wire | $R = \rho L / A$ | $\rho$ in $\Omega\cdot$m | Cu: $1.72\times 10^{-8}$ |
| 7 | Gauge factor | $G_F = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Metal ≈ 2; Si ≈ 100–200 |
| 8 | Strain–pressure (diaphragm) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{3P(1-\nu^2)r^2}{8Eh^2}$ | $P$=Pa, $r$,$h$=m, $E$=Pa, $\nu$ = 0.3 | — |
| 9 | Full-bridge output | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\,G_F\,\varepsilon$ | small-signal | — |
| 10 | Quarter-bridge output | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\,G_F\,\varepsilon$ | one active gauge | — |
| 11 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d\,P\,A$ | $d$ in C/N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² | Quartz $d_{11} = 2.3$ pC/N |
| 12 | Piezoelectric voltage | $V_o = d\,P\,A/C$ | $C$ in F | PZT $d_{33}\approx 250$ pC/N |
| 13 | LVDT null condition | $V_o = 0 \iff x = 0$ | core at geometric centre | — |
| 14 | Pressure conversion | $1$ mmHg $= 133.322$ Pa | $1$ atm $= 101.325$ kPa | — |
| 15 | Sensitivity of capacitive gap sensor | $S_d = \vert \Delta C/\Delta d \vert = C/d$ | F/m | Non-linear in $d$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Invasive arterial BP monitoring** in ICU uses **strain-gauge / semiconductor piezoresistive** catheter-tip transducers (e.g., Millar Mikro-Tip).
- **Non-invasive BP** (sphygmomanometer) historically used **aneroid LVDT-style** sensors.
- **Heart-sound phonocardiography (PCG)** and **fetal-heart detection** rely on **piezoelectric** microphones (active, no DC bias needed).
- **Intrauterine pressure** during labour is measured with **capacitive** tip transducers (Dräger/Utah Medical).
- **Respiratory plethysmography** uses **potentiometric chest-belt** sensors to record chest-wall displacement.
- **Joint-angle goniometers** in rehabilitation engineering use **rotary potentiometers**.
- **LVDT** is the gold standard for **cardiac-catheter tip pressure** (Swan-Ganz) and **ventricular pressure** waveforms.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LVDT Differential Output Voltage

**Given:** Primary excited by $V_p = V_{ex}\sin(\omega t)$. Two identical secondaries wound in series-opposition, with mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ to the primary.

**Find:** Expression for differential output $V_o$ in terms of core displacement $x$.

### Step 1 — Write the secondary EMFs by Faraday's Law

The induced EMF in secondary 1 is

$$E_1 = -M_1\,\frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

The induced EMF in secondary 2 is

$$E_2 = -M_2\,\frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Apply primary current

The primary coil of inductance $L_p$ draws current

$$I_p = \frac{V_p}{j\omega L_p} = \frac{V_{ex}\sin(\omega t)}{j\omega L_p}$$

### Step 3 — Express the EMFs

$$\frac{dI_p}{dt} = \frac{V_{ex}\omega\cos(\omega t)}{j\omega L_p} = \frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{j L_p}$$

Substituting,

$$E_1 = -M_1\,\frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{j L_p} = jM_1\,\frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{L_p}$$

$$E_2 = -M_2\,\frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{j L_p} = jM_2\,\frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{L_p}$$

### Step 4 — Connect secondaries in series-opposition

The output voltage is the **differential** of the two:

$$V_o = E_1 - E_2 = j(M_1 - M_2)\,\frac{V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{L_p}$$

### Step 5 — Linearize the mutual inductances

For a well-designed LVDT over the linear range, the mutual inductances vary linearly with core displacement $x$:

$$M_1 = M_0 + K\,x$$
$$M_2 = M_0 - K\,x$$

where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at null, and $K$ is a geometric constant.

### Step 6 — Subtract

$$M_1 - M_2 = (M_0 + Kx) - (M_0 - Kx) = 2Kx$$

### Step 7 — Final output

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{j\,2K\,x\,V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{L_p} = K'\,x\sin(\omega t + 90^\circ)\,}$$

where $K' = 2K V_{ex}/L_p$ is the LVDT sensitivity (V/m).

**Interpretation:** Magnitude is **proportional to displacement** $x$; **phase** indicates **direction** of core motion (towards $S_1$ vs. towards $S_2$). This dual nature is why a **phase-sensitive demodulator** is used at the output.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain-Gauge Pressure Transducer Sensitivity

**Given:** A circular steel diaphragm ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$) of radius $r = 8$ mm, thickness $h = 0.2$ mm, instrumented with a full-bridge of four strain gauges ($G_F = 2.0$). Excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Input pressure $P = 100$ mmHg.

**Find:** Bridge output voltage $V_o$ at $P = 100$ mmHg.

### Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI

$$P = 100\;\text{mmHg} \times 133.322\,\text{Pa/mmHg} = 13\,332.2\;\text{Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum strain on the diaphragm

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3P(1-\nu^2)r^2}{8Eh^2}$$

Compute numerator:

$$3 \times 13\,332.2 \times (1 - 0.3^2) \times (0.008)^2 = 3 \times 13\,332.2 \times 0.91 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$= 3 \times 13\,332.2 \times 5.824 \times 10^{-5} = 2.329\;\text{(Pa·m²)}$$

Compute denominator:

$$8 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times (0.0002)^2 = 8 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 64\,000\;\text{(Pa·m²)}$$

Therefore,

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{2.329}{64\,000} = 3.639 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 3 — Relative resistance change

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 3.639 \times 10^{-5} = 7.278 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 4 — Full-bridge small-signal output

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.0 \times 3.639 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_o = 1.25 \times 7.278 \times 10^{-5} = 9.097 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{V} \approx 91\;\mu\text{V}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity

$$S = \frac{V_o}{P} = \frac{9.097 \times 10^{-5}}{13\,332.2} = 6.82 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{V/Pa} = 6.82\;\mu\text{V/mmHg}$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 mmHg pressure change on this diaphragm produces only ~91 µV of bridge output — explaining why biomedical strain-gauge pressure transducers **require** a high-gain low-noise instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) at the front end.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoelectric Pressure Transducer Output

**Given:** A circular PZT disk of diameter $D = 10$ mm, thickness $t = 1$ mm, with $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N. The applied pressure is $P = 1$ kPa. The electrode capacitance is $C = 1$ nF. Cable and input capacitance are neglected.

**Find:** Generated charge $Q$, output voltage $V_o$, and peak voltage for $P = 1$ kPa pulsed.

### Step 1 — Electrode area

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Force on the disk

$$F = P \cdot A = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} = 7.854 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{N} = 78.54\;\text{mN}$$

### Step 3 — Generated charge

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 250 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{C} \approx 19.6\;\text{pC}$$

### Step 4 — Output voltage

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-11}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.963 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{V} \approx 19.6\;\text{mV}$$

### Step 5 — Verification using direct formula

$$V_o = \frac{d_{33} \cdot P \cdot A}{C} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-12} \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-9}} = 0.0196\;\text{V} \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Even 1 kPa produces a measurable 19.6 mV — piezoelectric sensors are highly sensitive, but require **charge amplifiers** (e.g., Analog Devices AD745) to prevent the charge from leaking through cable capacitance and input impedance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Transducer Selection for Biomedical Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board often asks: "Which transducer would you use for ___? Justify." The following matrix is your full-mark answer:**

| Biomedical Application | Recommended Transducer | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous arterial BP** (ICU) | **Strain-gauge / semiconductor piezoresistive** (full bridge on diaphragm) | Static (DC) response, fast, linear, sterilizable catheter |
| **Heart-sounds (PCG) microphone** | **Piezoelectric** | Self-generating, high sensitivity at audio (20 Hz–2 kHz), tiny |
| **Respiratory chest expansion** | **Potentiometric** belt or **LVDT** belt | Simple, large displacement (cm), low frequency |
| **Intrauterine pressure** during labour | **Capacitive catheter tip** | Small, soft, drift-free, isolated from electrical noise |
| **Fetal heart rate (Doppler probe)** | **Piezoelectric (PZT)** | Ultrasonic frequency (2–5 MHz), self-generating |
| **Joint angle (goniometer)** | **Rotary potentiometer** | Angular displacement, large signal, low cost |
| **Intracranial pressure (ICP) in MRI** | **Fiber-optic pressure** | Immune to electromagnetic interference, MRI-safe |
| **Sphygmomanometer (aneroid)** | **LVDT / bourdon + strain gauge** | Static pressure, calibrated, linear |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Pattern

**Question (14-mark pattern):** A strain-gauge pressure transducer uses a steel diaphragm of radius $r = 10$ mm, thickness $h = 0.25$ mm, with $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Two active gauges (each $G_F = 2.0$) are mounted on the diaphragm, and two dummy gauges complete the bridge. Excitation is 10 V. Calculate (a) the strain at the diaphragm edge for $P = 50$ mmHg, and (b) the bridge output voltage.

**Solution:**

(a) Convert: $P = 50 \times 133.322 = 6666.1$ Pa.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \times 6666.1 \times (1 - 0.09) \times (0.01)^2}{8 \times 200\times 10^9 \times (0.00025)^2}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 6666.1 \times 0.91 \times 10^{-4} = 1.819$

Denominator: $8 \times 200\times 10^9 \times 6.25\times 10^{-8} = 100\,000$

$$\varepsilon = 1.819 / 100\,000 = 1.819 \times 10^{-5}$$

(b) For a **half-bridge** with two active gauges in opposite arms:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}\,G_F\,\varepsilon = \frac{10}{2} \times 2.0 \times 1.819\times 10^{-5} = 1.819 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{V} \approx 182\;\mu\text{V}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LVDT — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete signal-flow of an LVDT-based displacement measurement channel — the kind of diagram a KTU examiner awards full marks for.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Oscillator\n1 kHz to 20 kHz\nSinusoidal AC] --> priA[Primary Winding P\nCenter tapped on bobbin]
    priA --> fluxA{Magnetic Flux\nCoupling}
    fluxA --> sec1A[Secondary S1\nLeft coil]
    fluxA --> sec2A[Secondary S2\nRight coil]
    sec1A --> diffA[Differential\nSubtractor Stage]
    sec2A --> diffA
    diffA --> demodA[Phase-Sensitive\nDemodulator]
    demodA --> lpA[Low-Pass Filter\nCutoff approx 100 Hz]
    lpA --> ampA[Instrumentation\nAmplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    ampA --> outA[DC Output Vout\nProportional to x\nPolarity gives direction]
    coreA((Movable\nIron Core\nInput x)) -.-> fluxA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The core (lower-left) is the input. The oscillator excites the primary. The magnetic flux couples *unequally* to $S_1$ vs. $S_2$ depending on core position. The differential output retains a *phase* signature (0° vs. 180°) which the phase-sensitive demodulator resolves into a bipolar DC signal — positive voltage = upward motion, negative = downward.

---

## 4.2 Strain-Gauge Pressure Transducer — Bridge Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pressA[Input Pressure P\nfrom blood vessel or balloon] --> diapA[Silicon or Steel\nDiaphragm\nradius r, thickness h]
    diapA --> strainA[Strain Development\nepsilon proportional to P]
    strainA --> gaug1A[Gauge 1\nTop of diaphragm R plus deltaR]
    strainA --> gaug2A[Gauge 2\nBottom of diaphragm R plus deltaR]
    strainA --> gaug3A[Gauge 3\nRight edge R minus deltaR]
    strainA --> gaug4A[Gauge 4\nLeft edge R minus deltaR]
    gaug1A --> bridA[Wheatstone Full Bridge]
    gaug2A --> bridA
    gaug3A --> bridA
    gaug4A --> bridA
    exciA[Excitation Vex\n5 V to 10 V stable DC] --> bridA
    bridA --> ampA[Instrumentation\nAmplifier]
    ampA --> filtA[Active Low-Pass Filter\nfc approx 40 Hz for BP]
    filtA --> dispA[Digital Display or\nPatient Monitor]
```

---

## 4.3 Piezoelectric Pressure Transducer — Signal-Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA[Pressure Pulse\nAcoustic or Impact] --> cryA[Quartz or PZT\nCrystal]
    cryA --> chA[Generated Charge Q\nCoulombs]
    chA --> cabA{Cable Capacitance Cc}
    cabA --> inA[Input of Charge Amplifier\nOp-Amp with Cf in feedback]
    inA --> vA[Output Voltage Vout\nVout = minus Q divided by Cf]
    inA --> resetA[Reset FET Switch\nDrain leakage every second]
    resetA --> inA
    vA --> lpA[Low-Pass Filter\nfc approx 2 kHz for PCG]
    lpA --> outA[Amplified PCG Signal\nto ECG monitor or tape]
```

---

## 4.4 Capacitive Displacement Transducer — Differential Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tgtA((Conductive\nTarget\nInput x)) --> cap1A[Capacitor C1\nbetween target and plate A]
    tgtA --> cap2A[Capacitor C2\nbetween target and plate B]
    cap1A --> oscA[AC Excitation\n100 kHz to 1 MHz]
    cap2A --> oscA
    cap1A --> difA[Differential\nC-to-V Converter]
    cap2A --> difA
    difA --> outA[DC Output Vout\nproportional to x]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Differential capacitive design** cancels common-mode drift (temperature, humidity) and doubles the sensitivity compared with a single-ended design — a feature universally used in biomedical-grade capacitive sensors.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Displacement and Pressure Transducers] --> br1A[Resistive\nPotentiometric]
    rootA --> br2A[Inductive\nLVDT RVDT]

```
