# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **The following questions replicate the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for PEERT413. Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice. Marks are split equally between (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). Why is its output said to be phase-sensitive?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An LVDT is an **inductive displacement transducer** consisting of one primary winding and two identical secondary windings wound on a common cylindrical bobbin, with a movable high-permeability iron core sliding inside.

It is called **phase-sensitive** because the **phase** of the differential output voltage changes by **180°** when the core crosses the null position and moves from the $S_1$ side to the $S_2$ side. Hence, a **phase-sensitive demodulator** can extract both **magnitude** (displacement) and **polarity** (direction) information from a single AC signal.

*(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – construction, 1 mark – phase sensitivity with 180° justification.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is a piezoelectric transducer unsuitable for measuring static (DC) blood pressure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A piezoelectric crystal generates a **charge** $Q = d \cdot P \cdot A$ only when the applied force **changes with time**. Under a steady DC pressure, the charge generated leaks away through the **finite input resistance** of the connected amplifier and the **finite resistance of the cable**, causing the output voltage to **decay exponentially** with a time-constant $\tau = RC$.

For arterial blood pressure, the static (diastolic) component is a true DC signal, and the piezoelectric sensor would show a continuously drifting output. Hence piezoelectric sensors are used only for **dynamic, AC-coupled** signals like **heart sounds (PCG)** and **ultrasonic echoes**.

*(1 mark – charge-generation principle, 1 mark – leakage explanation, 1 mark – DC drift conclusion with example.)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "LVDT is a displacement transducer" with "LVDT is a pressure transducer" — the LVDT itself measures *position*; pressure is measured *via* a diaphragm that converts pressure to displacement.
> 2. **For piezo transducers**, you must explicitly state that they are **active / self-generating** — many students lose a mark by calling them "passive."
> 3. **Numerical problems on strain gauges** often test unit conversion (mmHg → Pa). A mark is deducted for leaving pressure in mmHg.
> 4. **Phase-sensitive demodulation** is the heart of the LVDT signal-conditioning chain — writing only "AC amplifier" is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. For the **gauge factor formula** $G_F = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$, do not omit the geometric term $(1 + 2\nu)$ in the definition.

---

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of an LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Discuss why its output is **phase-sensitive** and how this property is exploited in a **phase-sensitive demodulator**.

**(b) [7 marks]** An LVDT is excited by a 5 V, 5 kHz sinusoidal source. At a core displacement of 0.5 mm, the secondary-induced EMFs are 1.2 V and 0.8 V (in series-opposition). Calculate (i) the **differential output voltage**, (ii) the **sensitivity** in V/m, and (iii) the **null voltage** at $x = 0$.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** Construction (3 marks):
- One primary $P$ centred on bobbin, two identical secondaries $S_1, S_2$ wound on either side, connected in **series-opposition** (anti-phase).
- Movable high-permeability **ferromagnetic core** slides through hollow bore.
- AC excitation $V_p = V_{ex}\sin(\omega t)$ to primary.

Working (2 marks):
- At null: $E_1 = E_2$ → $V_o = E_1 - E_2 = 0$.
- Core moved towards $S_1$ → $E_1 > E_2$ → $V_o$ positive-phase.
- Core moved towards $S_2$ → $E_2 > E_1$ → $V_o$ negative-phase (180° shift).
- $V_o = K' x \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ with magnitude proportional to $x$ and phase giving direction.

Derivation (1 mark) — see Section 3.1:
$$V_o = \frac{2K x V_{ex}\cos(\omega t)}{L_p}$$

Phase-sensitive demodulator (1 mark):
- A reference signal (same frequency, in-phase with primary) is multiplied with the output.
- Result is low-pass filtered → DC voltage whose **magnitude is proportional to $x$** and **polarity** (positive/negative) **indicates direction**.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) Differential output: $V_o = E_1 - E_2 = 1.2 - 0.8 = 0.4$ V (rms).

(ii) Sensitivity: $K' = V_o / x = 0.4 / 0.0005 = \mathbf{800\;\text{V/m}}$.

(iii) Null voltage: by definition, $V_o = 0$ at $x = 0$, so $V_{\text{null}} = \mathbf{0\;\text{V}}$.

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` *(Internal-Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **piezoresistive effect**. Derive the expression for the **gauge factor** of a strain gauge. Why is a **Wheatstone bridge** used for strain-gauge pressure measurement, and what is the advantage of a **full-bridge** configuration over a **quarter-bridge**?

**(b) [7 marks]** A semiconductor strain gauge of gauge factor 120 is bonded to a steel cantilever beam ($E = 200$ GPa, length 50 mm, thickness 2 mm, width 10 mm). A force of 5 N is applied at the free end. The gauge is wired in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with excitation 10 V. Calculate (i) the **strain** at the gauge location, (ii) the **change in resistance** (assuming $R = 120\;\Omega$), and (iii) the **bridge output voltage** (small-signal approximation).

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** Piezoresistive effect (2 marks):
- Change in resistivity $\rho$ of a material under mechanical strain, in addition to the geometric change in length and area.
- First observed by Lord Kelvin in metals; dominant in semiconductors.

Gauge-factor derivation (3 marks):
$$R = \rho L / A$$
$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$
Differentiate:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$
With $dA/A = -2\nu dL/L$:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$
$$\boxed{\,G_F = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

Why Wheatstone bridge (1 mark): converts small $\Delta R$ to a voltage; provides **null-balance**, **temperature compensation**, and **common-mode rejection**.

Full-bridge vs. quarter-bridge (1 mark): full bridge gives **4× the sensitivity** and **automatic temperature compensation** since all four arms change resistance together with temperature → temperature-induced $\Delta R$ cancels.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) For a cantilever, strain at the surface is
$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 F L}{E b h^2} = \frac{6 \times 5 \times 0.05}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.01 \times (0.002)^2}$$
$$= \frac{1.5}{200 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.5}{8000} = 1.875 \times 10^{-4}$$

(ii) $\Delta R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R = 120 \times 1.875 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 = 2.7\;\Omega$.

(iii) Quarter-bridge small-signal output:
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\,G_F\,\varepsilon = \frac{10}{4} \times 120 \times 1.875 \times 10^{-4} = 5.625 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{V} \approx \mathbf{56.25\;\text{mV}}$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ✅ **LVDT** = primary + 2 secondaries in series-opposition + movable core; **null at centre**; **infinite resolution**; **phase-sensitive** output.
- ✅ **LVDT output** $V_o = K' x \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ — magnitude ∝ $|x|$, phase gives direction.
- ✅ **Potentiometric** = simple, low-cost, but has **sliding-contact wear and finite resolution** (wire-wound).
- ✅ **Capacitive transducer** — variable $d$ (gap) gives **inverse non-linear** output; variable $A$ (area) gives **linear** output.
- ✅ **Gauge factor** $G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ — for metals ≈ 2, for Si semiconductors ≈ 100–200.
- ✅ **Full-bridge small-signal output** $V_o \approx (V_{ex}/4)\,G_F\,\varepsilon$ — gives **automatic temperature compensation**.
- ✅ **Strain on a clamped circular diaphragm** $\varepsilon = 3P(1-\nu^2)r^2 / (8Eh^2)$ — quadruples if you **halve the thickness $h$**.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric** = **ACTIVE / self-generating**; **no DC response**; use for **PCG and ultrasound**, not for steady BP.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric output** $V_o = dPA/C$ — higher $C$ (cable + input) **reduces** voltage (charge amplifiers used).
- ✅ **1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa** and **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg** — must convert for KTU numericals.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications:** BP catheter = strain-gauge / semiconductor; heart sounds = piezo; intrauterine = capacitive; joint angle = rotary pot; ICP in MRI = fiber-optic.
- ✅ **Phase-sensitive demodulator** is the signal conditioner of choice for LVDT — it extracts magnitude and direction.
- ✅ **LVDT excitation frequency** = 1 kHz to 20 kHz; **piezo charge amplifiers** use op-amps with FET input for low leakage.
- ✅ **Quarter-bridge** = 1 active gauge, sensitivity = 1×; **half-bridge** = 2 active, 2×; **full-bridge** = 4 active, 4×, with temperature compensation.
- ✅ **PZT** has $d_{33} \approx 250$ pC/N; **quartz** $d_{11} \approx 2.3$ pC/N — PZT is **~100× more sensitive** but quartz is more temperature-stable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric Effect (Definition per KTU Syllabus):** The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystalline materials to generate an electric charge (or voltage) on their surfaces when subjected to a mechanical stress (force or pressure). Conversely, when an electric field is applied across these materials, they undergo a measurable mechanical deformation. This dual, reversible coupling between mechanical strain and electrical polarization in non-centrosymmetric crystals is the foundational principle of all piezoelectric transducers used in biomedical instrumentation.

The word *piezo* originates from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning **"to press"** or **"to squeeze"**, precisely describing the nature of the mechanical input required to elicit the electrical response. The phenomenon was first discovered by **Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880** while studying the behavior of crystals such as quartz, tourmaline, and Rochelle salt under mechanical load.

### 1.2 Classification of the Piezoelectric Effect

The piezoelectric effect is fundamentally bidirectional, and the classification is critical for both KTU theory questions and transducer design problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Generator/Sensor Mode):**  
> Mechanical Force $\rightarrow$ Electrical Charge. This is the operating principle of piezoelectric *sensors* (e.g., phonocardiograph microphones, ultrasound receivers, pressure transducers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect (Actuator Mode):**  
> Applied Voltage $\rightarrow$ Mechanical Strain. This is the operating principle of piezoelectric *actuators* (e.g., therapeutic ultrasound heads, inkjet printer heads, micro-pumps for drug delivery).

A single piezoelectric crystal can therefore function as **both a sensor and an actuator**, making it a *transducing* element in the truest sense.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **party balloon** (which is non-centrosymmetric) being squeezed between your palms. As you apply pressure, two regions of the balloon's surface bulge outwards and develop a net surface charge distribution. If we attach tiny electrodes to these surfaces and connect them to a meter, we would observe a small voltage. Now, if instead we connect a battery across those electrodes, the balloon would deform — squishing further or expanding. A piezoelectric crystal behaves exactly like this "smart balloon," except that the deformation is at the **microscopic lattice level** (measured in picometers to nanometers) and the generated voltage is highly precise and reproducible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it happen?**  
> In a non-centrosymmetric crystal (one without a center of symmetry), the positive and negative charge centers of the unit cell **do not coincide** naturally. When a mechanical force displaces the ions, the charge centers shift, creating a net **electric dipole moment**. The cumulative effect across billions of unit cells produces a measurable surface charge.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Piezoelectric Charge Constant ($d$)** — Units: **Coulomb/Newton (C/N)** or **meters/Volt (m/V)**. Symbol: $d_{ij}$.  
- **Piezoelectric Voltage Constant ($g$)** — Units: **Volt·meter/Newton (V·m/N)**.  
- **Relative Permittivity ($\epsilon_r$)** — Dimensionless ratio.  
- **Curie Temperature ($T_c$)** — Units: **Kelvin (K)** or **°C**. Above this temperature, the piezoelectric crystal loses its polarization (becomes centrosymmetric, i.e., paraelectric).  
- **Coupling Coefficient ($k$)** — Dimensionless, $0 < k < 1$. Indicates the efficiency of energy conversion between mechanical and electrical domains.  
- **Mechanical Quality Factor ($Q_m$)** — Dimensionless. Bandwidth of resonance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must remember the symmetric matrix nature of $d_{ij}$, where the first index $i$ denotes the **direction of the generated electric field** (1, 2, 3) and the second index $j$ denotes the **direction of the applied mechanical stress** (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Out of 18 possible coefficients, only a few are non-zero depending on crystal symmetry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The operational concept of a piezoelectric transducer is broken down into the following engineering logic steps:

1. **Step 1 — Material Selection & Poling:**  
   A raw piezoelectric ceramic (e.g., PZT) is isotropic (no net polarization). It is heated above its **Curie temperature** and subjected to a strong DC electric field (typically **2–5 kV/mm**). This aligns the randomly oriented ferroelectric domains. The field is then removed after cooling, leaving a **remanent polarization ($P_r$)** permanently locked into the material. This process is called **poling**.

2. **Step 2 — Electrode Deposition:**  
   Thin metallic electrodes (silver, gold, or nickel) are coated on opposite faces perpendicular to the poling axis (axis 3). These electrodes collect the induced surface charge.

3. **Step 3 — Mechanical Input (Sensor Mode):**  
   An external mechanical force ($F$ in Newtons) or pressure ($P$ in N/m²) is applied along a defined axis. The stress $\sigma$ (N/m²) or strain $S$ (dimensionless) creates a proportional **displacement of charge** $Q$ (Coulombs) on the electrodes.

4. **Step 4 — Charge-to-Voltage Conversion:**  
   The generated charge $Q$ appears as an open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = Q / C_p$, where $C_p$ is the self-capacitance of the crystal (typically in picofarads). This high output impedance is why piezoelectric sensors **must** be paired with **charge amplifiers** or **voltage amplifiers with very high input impedance** ($>10^{12}\ \Omega$).

5. **Step 5 — Signal Conditioning & Readout:**  
   The conditioned voltage signal is then digitized or displayed. For dynamic signals, the output is proportional to the rate of change of force; for quasi-static signals, charge amplifiers are mandatory to prevent drift.

6. **Step 6 — Reverse Operation (Actuator Mode):**  
   When a voltage is applied, the converse effect induces strain, displacing the crystal by a few micrometers, used to generate ultrasound waves, vibrate diaphragms, or move micro-mirrors.

### 2.2 Modes of Vibration (Critical for KTU Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three primary modes used in KTU numericals are:**

- **Thickness Mode (33-Mode):** Electric field $E_3$ is parallel to the poling axis (axis 3), and stress $\sigma_3$ is also along axis 3. Coefficient used: $d_{33}$.  
- **Length Mode (31-Mode):** Electric field $E_3$ is along axis 3, but stress $\sigma_1$ is along axis 1 (perpendicular). Coefficient used: $d_{31}$.  
- **Shear Mode (15-Mode):** Electric field $E_1$ is along axis 1, and shear stress $\sigma_5$ is applied. Coefficient used: $d_{15}$.

### 2.3 Constitutive Equations (The Heart of Piezoelectricity)

The two fundamental coupled equations governing the linear piezoelectric effect are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= s^E_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{im}\,E_m \\
D_m &= d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \epsilon^T_{mn}\,E_n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:  
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain (dimensionless)  
- $s^E_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)  
- $\sigma_j$ = mechanical stress (N/m²)  
- $d_{im}$ = piezoelectric charge constant (C/N)  
- $E_m$ = applied electric field (V/m)  
- $D_m$ = electric displacement (C/m²)  
- $\epsilon^T_{mn}$ = permittivity at constant stress (F/m)  

The first equation governs the **converse effect** (electrical input → mechanical output), and the second governs the **direct effect** (mechanical input → electrical output).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Critical Equations for KTU Examinations**

| # | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $Q$ = charge (C), $d$ = charge constant (C/N), $F$ = force (N) | Direct piezoelectric charge generation |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$ | $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage (V), $C_p$ = crystal capacitance (F) | Output voltage for high-impedance readouts |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = g \cdot t \cdot F / A$ | $g$ = voltage constant (V·m/N), $t$ = thickness (m), $A$ = area (m²) | Output voltage per unit force |
| 4 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r}$ | $\epsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity, $\epsilon_r$ = relative permittivity | Fundamental relation between $d$ and $g$ |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{t}$ | $A$ = electrode area, $t$ = crystal thickness | Self-capacitance of the piezoelectric disc |
| 6 | $f_r = \dfrac{v}{2t} = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho s^E_{33}}}$ | $f_r$ = resonant frequency, $v$ = acoustic velocity, $\rho$ = density | Resonant frequency of thickness mode |
| 7 | $k^2 = \dfrac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \epsilon^T}$ | $k$ = electromechanical coupling coefficient | Energy conversion efficiency |
| 8 | $S = d \cdot E$ | $S$ = induced strain, $E$ = applied electric field | Converse piezoelectric effect (actuator) |
| 9 | $\Delta t = d \cdot V$ | $\Delta t$ = thickness change (m), $V$ = applied voltage (V) | Actuator displacement formula |
| 10 | $S_i = s^E_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{im}\,E_m$ | Full constitutive equation | Strain under combined load |
| 11 | $D_m = d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \epsilon^T_{mn}\,E_n$ | Full constitutive equation | Electric displacement under combined load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on coefficients:** For PZT-5A, the typical $d_{33} \approx 374 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, $g_{33} \approx 24 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N, $\epsilon_r \approx 1700$, and $T_c \approx 350°$C. These are exam-standard values.

### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

- **Diagnostic Ultrasound Probes (1–15 MHz):** The piezoelectric array acts as both transmitter (converse effect) and receiver (direct effect) of acoustic waves.  
- **Phonocardiograph (PCG) Microphones:** Detects heart sounds (20–2000 Hz) via diaphragm-coupled piezoelectric crystals.  
- **Fetal Heart Rate Monitors (Doppler):** Continuous-wave Doppler ultrasound at 2 MHz.  
- **Blood Pressure Catheter-tip Transducers:** Milli-sized PZT discs for intra-arterial pressure measurement.  
- **Respiratory Monitors (Chest-Band Type):** Piezoelectric belts measure chest expansion.  
- **Bone Conduction Hearing Aids:** Piezo-driven vibrators transmit sound through the skull.  
- **Accelerometers for Gait Analysis:** Detect patient motion and tremors (Parkinson's, epilepsy).  
- **Lithotripters:** High-intensity focused ultrasound for non-invasive kidney stone disintegration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Charge Output from Applied Force

**Problem Statement:** A PZT-5A piezoelectric crystal has a charge constant $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$ and a cross-sectional area $A = 100\ \text{mm}^2$. A force $F = 5\ \text{N}$ is applied along the poling axis. Find the generated charge $Q$, the capacitance $C_p$, and the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$. Take $\epsilon_r = 1700$, thickness $t = 2\ \text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the generated charge using the direct piezoelectric equation.**  
The fundamental direct effect equation is:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

We substitute the known values (ensure SI units — force in Newtons, $d$ in C/N):

$$
Q = (374 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}) \times (5\ \text{N})
$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$
Q = 1870 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C} = 1.87 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} = 1.87\ \text{nC}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the self-capacitance of the crystal using the parallel-plate formula.**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{t}
$$

Converting to SI units: $A = 100\ \text{mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, and $t = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$. Substituting:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}) \times 1700 \times (1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2)}{2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}}
$$

Multiplying numerator values:

$$
\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1700 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5052 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F·m}
$$

Dividing by thickness:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{1.5052 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.526 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \approx 0.7526\ \text{nF}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the open-circuit output voltage.**

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p}
$$

Substituting computed values:

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{1.87 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C}}{7.526 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F}}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
V_{oc} \approx 2.485\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-verify using the $g$-constant relationship.**

We use the relation $g_{33} = d_{33} / (\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r)$:

$$
g_{33} = \dfrac{374 \times 10^{-12}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1700}
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1700 = 1.5052 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{F/m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
g_{33} = \dfrac{374 \times 10^{-12}}{1.5052 \times 10^{-8}} = 24.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V·m/N}
$$

The voltage can also be written as:

$$
V_{oc} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{F}{A}
$$

Plugging in:

$$
V_{oc} = (24.85 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times \dfrac{5}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

Simplifying the fraction $5 / 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{4}$:

$$
V_{oc} = 24.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{4} = 2.485\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark
$$

The two methods agree, confirming the analytical consistency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonant Frequency of a Thickness-Mode Crystal

**Problem Statement:** A quartz crystal has density $\rho = 2650\ \text{kg/m}^3$, thickness $t = 1\ \text{mm}$, and elastic compliance at constant field $s^E_{33} = 11.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$. Determine the thickness-mode resonant frequency $f_r$.

**Step 1 — Recall the acoustic wave speed in the piezoelectric medium.**  
The longitudinal acoustic velocity is given by:

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot s^E_{33}}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(2650)\,(11.5 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$
\rho \cdot s^E_{33} = 2650 \times 11.5 \times 10^{-12} = 30{,}475 \times 10^{-12} = 3.0475 \times 10^{-8}
$$

Take the reciprocal and the square root:

$$
v = \sqrt{3.281 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5728\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the half-wavelength resonance condition.**  
For a piezoelectric plate vibrating in thickness mode, the fundamental resonance occurs when the plate thickness equals **half an acoustic wavelength**:

$$
t = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2t
$$

The frequency is then $f_r = v / \lambda$:

$$
f_r = \dfrac{v}{2t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
f_r = \dfrac{5728\ \text{m/s}}{2 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m})}
$$

Simplify:

$$
f_r = \dfrac{5728}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5728}{0.002} = 2.864 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz} = 2.864\ \text{MHz}
$$

This resonant frequency is typical of medical ultrasound transducers operating in the diagnostic range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient

**Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric disc of PZT-4 ceramic has $d_{33} = 289 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$, $s^E_{33} = 12.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$, and $\epsilon^T_{33} = 1100 \times \epsilon_0$. Find the electromechanical coupling coefficient $k_{33}$.

**Step 1 — Use the defining formula:**

$$
k^2_{33} = \dfrac{d^2_{33}}{s^E_{33} \cdot \epsilon^T_{33}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values in SI units.**

Numerator:

$$
d^2_{33} = (289 \times 10^{-12})^2 = 83{,}521 \times 10^{-24} = 8.3521 \times 10^{-20}\ (\text{C/N})^2
$$

Denominator:

$$
s^E_{33} \cdot \epsilon^T_{33} = (12.5 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12})
$$

First, $1100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 9.739 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F/m}$. Then:

$$
\text{Denominator} = 12.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 9.739 \times 10^{-9} = 1.2174 \times 10^{-19}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
k^2_{33} = \dfrac{8.3521 \times 10^{-20}}{1.2174 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.686
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain $k_{33}$:**

$$
k_{33} = \sqrt{0.686} \approx 0.828
$$

This means approximately **82.8%** of the input electrical energy is converted to mechanical energy (or vice versa), demonstrating the high efficiency of PZT ceramics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Piezoelectric Materials

| Material | Type | $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | $\epsilon_r$ | $T_c$ (°C) | $Q_m$ | Biomedical Suitability |
|----------|------|----------------|--------------|-----------|-------|------------------------|
| **Quartz (SiO₂)** | Natural single crystal | 2.3 | 4.5 | 573 | High (>$10^4$) | Precision force/pressure sensors, very stable |
| **Rochelle Salt** | Natural single crystal | 480 | 500 | 45 (low!) | Low | Microphones, hearing aids (temperature-limited) |
| **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)** | Synthetic ceramic | 190 | 1200 | 120 | Medium | Hearing aid mics, sonar |
| **PZT-5A** | Synthetic ceramic | 374 | 1700 | 365 | 75 | **Ultrasound probes, accelerometers** |
| **PZT-5H** | Synthetic ceramic | 593 | 3400 | 195 | 65 | High-sensitivity acoustic sensors |
| **PVDF (Polymer Film)** | Synthetic polymer | 33 | 10 | 80 | 10 | Flexible body-surface sensors, hydrophones |
| **Lead Metaniobate** | Synthetic ceramic | 80 | 225 | 570 | Very low | High-temperature ultrasound, NDE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PZT dominates biomedical applications:** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) offers the optimal balance of high $d_{33}$ (sensitivity), high $T_c$ (thermal stability during autoclaving), moderate $\epsilon_r$ (low impedance matching challenges), and tunable composition via the Zr:Ti ratio.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Static Sensitivity and Charge Amplifier Matching

**Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric accelerometer has sensitivity $S_a = 50\ \text{pC/g}$ (where $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$). It is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_f = 1\ \text{nF}$. Determine the voltage sensitivity and output voltage for a 5g acceleration input.

**Step 1 — Convert charge sensitivity to voltage sensitivity (in the charge-amplifier configuration).**  
The output voltage of a charge amplifier is:

$$
V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}
$$

The voltage sensitivity $S_v$ is therefore:

$$
S_v = \dfrac{S_a}{C_f} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/g}}{1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}} = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/g} = 50\ \text{mV/g}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate output for 5g input.**

$$
V_{out} = S_v \times a = 50\ \text{mV/g} \times 5\ \text{g} = 250\ \text{mV}
$$

This explains why charge amplifiers are mandatory: they decouple the output from the cable capacitance, ensuring the calibration remains accurate regardless of cable length.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Piezoelectric Effect — Direct vs. Converse (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Mechanical Force F applied to crystal"]:::input
    A2["Lattice deformation in non-centrosymmetric unit cell"]:::process
    A3["Net dipole moment created"]:::process
    A4["Surface charge Q accumulates on electrodes"]:::process
    A5["Output voltage V open-circuit measured"]:::output
    A6["Direct Effect SENSOR mode"]:::label

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A6 -.- A1

    B1["Voltage V applied across electrodes"]:::input
    B2["Electric field E established in crystal"]:::process
    B3["Ions displaced within unit cell"]:::process
    B4["Mechanical strain S produced"]:::process
    B5["Crystal physically deforms by delta t"]:::output
    B6["Converse Effect ACTUATOR mode"]:::label

    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
    B6 -.- B1

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef label fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Mechanical Input Force or Pressure"]:::phys
    s2["Piezoelectric Crystal with Electrodes"]:::transducer
    s3["Generated Charge Q on high-impedance electrodes"]:::signal
    s4["Charge Amplifier with Feedback Capacitance Cf"]:::amp

    s7["ADC Analog to Digital Converter"]:::adc
    s8["Digital Signal Processing and Display"]:::out

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8

```

### 4.3 Poling Process — Ferroelectric Domain Alignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Unpoled Piezoelectric Ceramic with random domains"]:::state
    p2["Heat above Curie Temperature Tc"]:::process
    p3["Apply Strong DC Poling Field Ep about 2 to 5 kV per mm"]:::process
    p4["Domains align parallel to Ep field"]:::state
    p5["Cool crystal to room temperature while keeping Ep applied"]:::process
    p6["Remove field - remanent polarization Pr remains locked in"]:::state
    p7["Crystal is now permanently polarized - ready for use"]:::output

    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6
    p6 --> p7

```

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit — Mason Model of a Piezoelectric Resonator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["Mechanical Port F velocity"]:::port
    m2["Mass M inertance"]:::elem
    m3["Compliance Cm series spring"]:::elem
    m4["Mechanical damper Rm losses"]:::elem
    m5["Ideal electromechanical transformer ratio phi"]:::xformer
    m6["Electrical Port with dielectric capacitance C0"]:::port
    m7["Load impedance ZL at electrical port"]:::port

    m1 --> m2
    m2 --> m3
    m3 --> m4
    m4 --> m5
    m5 --> m6
    m6 --> m7

    classDef port fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A237E
    classDef elem fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
```
