1. The filament is heated to ~**2200–2500 °C** by the filament transformer; electrons are emitted by **thermionic emission**.
2. The focusing cup shapes the electron stream into a narrow beam.
3. The high voltage (30–150 kV DC) applied across cathode and anode accelerates the electrons to ~**50%–70% the speed of light**.
4. The electrons strike the tungsten target; ~**99%** of their kinetic energy is converted to **heat**, and ~**1%** to **X-ray photons** via:
   - **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous spectrum) — from electron deceleration in the nuclear Coulomb field
   - **Characteristic radiation** (line spectrum) — from inner-shell electron ejection
5. The resulting X-ray beam exits through a **beryllium or glass window** with inherent filtration.

---

#### (b) Derive the Duane-Hunt equation. A diagnostic X-ray tube is operated at 90 kVp. Calculate (i) the minimum wavelength emitted, and (ii) the maximum photon energy. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):** *(As derived in Section 3.1)*

The maximum photon energy equals the electron's kinetic energy (conservation of energy):

$$eV = h\nu_{\max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV} = \frac{1240}{V \text{ (volts)}} \text{ nm} = \frac{1.240}{V \text{ (kV)}} \text{ nm}$$

**Calculations (4 marks):**

Given: $V = 90 \text{ kVp}$

**(i) Minimum wavelength:**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.240}{90} = 0.01378 \text{ nm} = 13.78 \text{ pm}$$

**(ii) Maximum photon energy:**

$$E_{\max} = eV = 90 \text{ keV} = 1.44 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

(Using $E = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C} \times 90{,}000 \text{ V} = 1.4418 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$)

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of a high-voltage generator used in an X-ray machine. Compare single-phase and three-phase generators. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A high-voltage generator consists of:
- **Autotransformer:** A variable transformer that allows the operator to select the desired kVp.
- **High-Voltage Step-Up Transformer:** Steps the autotransformer output to 30–150 kV (turns ratio 1:500 to 1:1000). Its secondary is housed in a **grounded metal tank** filled with **high-dielectric oil** for insulation and cooling.
- **Rectifier Circuit:** Solid-state high-voltage diodes (silicone-diffused junction) that convert AC to pulsating DC.
- **Smoothing Capacitors:** Reduce voltage ripple.
- **Filament Transformer:** Step-down transformer with its secondary at cathode high-voltage potential.

**Working (2 marks):**

Mains AC → autotransformer selects kV → step-up transformer multiplies voltage → rectifier converts to DC → capacitors smooth → DC applied to X-ray tube. The tube conducts only when the cathode is negative relative to the anode (forward-biased half-cycles), producing a pulsating unidirectional X-ray output.

**Comparison (2 marks):** *(See comparison table in Section 3.5)*

Three-phase generators produce a **near-constant potential** with **< 4% ripple**, giving 1.5–2× the output of a single-phase unit for the same mAs, allowing lower patient doses.

---

#### (b) An X-ray machine is operated at 80 kVp, 200 mA for 0.5 s with a single-phase full-wave rectified unit. Calculate the heat units (HU) generated in the anode. If the anode's maximum heat storage is 200,000 HU, how many such consecutive exposures can be made before the anode reaches its limit? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Heat Unit Formula:**

$$H.U. = k \cdot V_p \cdot I \cdot t$$

For single-phase full-wave, $k = 1.41$:

$$H.U. = 1.41 \times 80 \times 200 \times 0.5$$

$$H.U. = 1.41 \times 80 \times 100 = 11{,}280 \text{ HU}$$

**Number of exposures:**

$$n = \frac{\text{Anode Capacity}}{\text{HU per exposure}} = \frac{200{,}000}{11{,}280}$$

$$n = 17.73 \approx 17 \text{ consecutive exposures}$$

> **Note:** In practice, **cooling time** between exposures must be considered using the anode's cooling curve, and the maximum **instantaneous exposure** is limited by the single-exposure heat rating (typically 30,000–60,000 HU per shot). The 17-exposure figure assumes instantaneous heat accumulation (worst-case, no cooling) — the actual safe sequence will be slightly higher.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Duane-Hunt Unit Confusion:** Many students write $\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{V}$ without specifying that $V$ must be in **volts** (giving $\lambda$ in nm) or **kV** (giving $\lambda$ in nm after adjusting the constant). Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit ambiguity.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the Ripple Factor:** When asked to compare generator outputs, students often write "three-phase has 1.5× output" without stating the **ripple factor** and the kV waveform. Always state **% ripple** and **effective kV** in such answers.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Heat Units (HU) without the 1.41 Factor:** Forgetting $k = 1.41$ for single-phase full-wave rectification loses 1 mark. The factor is derived from the average-to-peak ratio $2/\pi \approx 1/1.41$.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Confusing Effective and True Focal Spot:** The **true focal spot** is the actual area on the anode where electrons strike. The **effective focal spot** is the foreshortened projection seen from the patient side. Both must be mentioned.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Missing Block Diagram Labels:** In 7-mark questions, the **neat block diagram** carries 1.5–2 marks. Unlabeled arrows or missing blocks (especially HV cables, control console, filtration) cost marks. Always include the **complete chain**: Mains → Console → AutoTr → HVT + Rectifier → HV Cable → Tube → Collimator → Filter → Patient → Receptor.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — "Inherent" vs "Added" Filtration:** Students often combine them incorrectly. Inherent filtration (~0.5–1 mm Al eq.) + added filtration = **total filtration** (must be ≥ 2.5 mm Al eq. for ≥ 70 kVp per AERB / BARC).
>
> **Pitfall 7 — Thermionic Emission Conditions:** Stating "tungsten filament is heated" without the **temperature range** (~2200–2500 °C) loses marks. Examiners expect the temperature and the term **thermionic emission** explicitly.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — re-read this section the night before the KTU examination.**

- ✅ An X-ray machine converts **electrical energy** → **high-energy electrons** → **X-ray photons** (with ~**1% efficiency**, 99% as heat).
- ✅ **Eight functional blocks:** HV Generator, X-Ray Tube, Filament Transformer, Rectifier, Control Console, Collimator, Filtration, HV Cables, Image Receptor.
- ✅ **Duane-Hunt equation:** $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{V_{\text{volts}}} \text{ nm}$ — quantum limit, shortest wavelength emitted.
- ✅ **Maximum photon energy:** $E_{\max} = eV$ (in eV when $V$ in volts).
- ✅ **Line-focus principle:** $L_{\text{eff}} = L \cdot \sin\theta$ — small effective spot from large actual spot.
- ✅ **X-ray intensity** $\propto V_p^2 \cdot Z \cdot I$ (Bremsstrahlung).
- ✅ **Heat Units (single-phase full-wave):** $H.U. = 1.41 \cdot V_p \cdot I \cdot t$.
- ✅ **Three-phase / HF generators** have lower ripple (3.5–10%) and higher output (~1.5–2×) than single-phase (100% ripple).
- ✅ **Filament:** Tungsten, heated to ~2200–2500 °C, thermionic emission; current 3–6 A at 3–12 V.
- ✅ **Anode:** Tungsten target (Z=74), tilted at 6°–20°; rotating anode allows higher power loading.
- ✅ **Collimator + Filter:** Reduce patient dose and improve image quality (ALARA).
- ✅ **Total filtration requirement:** ≥ **2.5 mm Al eq.** for ≥ 70 kVp (AERB / BARC).
- ✅ **Voltage ripple is the key differentiator** between generator types — lower ripple = better beam quality, less patient dose per image.
- ✅ **HV cables** are coaxial, oil/EPR insulated, with a grounded outer shield to prevent corona discharge.
- ✅ **Focal-spot size** controls **geometric unsharpness** $U_g = f \cdot S / (D - S)$.
- ✅ **15% Rule:** Raising kVp by 15% and halving mAs preserves image density but reduces patient dose and changes contrast.
- ✅ **The 99% heat problem** is the central engineering challenge — solved by rotating anodes, oil cooling, heat exchangers, and high-frequency generators.

> 🎓 **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** The examiner rewards **clarity of block-diagram labels**, **correct unit specifications**, and **explicit statement of physical principles** (e.g., thermionic emission, Bremsstrahlung, ALARA). Use these keywords liberally in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tubes for Various Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT413 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNALS &TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tubes for Various Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Biomedical Application Tubes

In the domain of **Biomedical Signals & Transducers (PEERT413)**, *tubes* refer to specialized evacuated or gas-filled electronic/electro-optical devices that convert one form of energy into another for diagnostic, therapeutic, or measurement purposes. These tubes act as **front-end transducers**, transforming electrical signals into visual images, photons into amplified electrical signals, or accelerated electrons into penetrating radiation useful in medical imaging and clinical diagnostics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 4 – Tubes):**
> Vacuum/evacuated tubes used in biomedical engineering are sealed glass or metal-ceramic enclosures in which electrons (or ions) travel through a controlled medium (vacuum or inert gas) between electrodes to perform signal conversion, amplification, image formation, or radiation generation for medical applications.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Biomedical Tubes

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the major tubes classified under "Tubes for Various Applications" include:

| Sl. No | Tube Type | Primary Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **X-Ray Tube (Coolidge Type)** | Diagnostic radiography, fluoroscopy, CT |
| 2 | **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** | Oscilloscopes, legacy patient monitors |
| 3 | **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** | Gamma cameras, scintillation counting, spectrophotometry |
| 4 | **Image Intensifier Tube** | Real-time X-ray fluoroscopy |
| 5 | **Gas Discharge / Thyratron Tube** | Pulse generation in defibrillators, X-ray exposure timing |
| 6 | **Electron Multiplier Tube** | Mass spectrometry, electron microscopy |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tube as a Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Tube is a Specialised Translator"**
> Imagine a tube as a highly specialised *language translator* sitting in a dark sealed room (vacuum). It receives input in one "language" (electrical signal, light, or heat) and produces output in another (visible image, amplified electrons, or X-ray photons). Just like a translator needs a structured environment (quiet room) to work accurately, a tube needs a vacuum to allow free electrons to travel without collision, ensuring precise, controlled signal conversion for medical diagnosis.

---

## 1.4 Why Tubes Matter in Biomedical Engineering

- **High sensitivity** — PMTs can detect single photons, critical in PET/SPECT imaging.
- **High energy generation** — X-ray tubes produce controlled, high-energy photons for tissue penetration.
- **Real-time imaging** — Image intensifiers and CRTs enabled the first real-time patient monitoring displays.
- **Signal amplification** — Vacuum tubes (though largely replaced by solid-state devices) are still relevant in niche high-frequency, radiation-hardened medical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** The syllabus explicitly emphasises *operation, construction, and biomedical application* of these tubes. Memorise the labelled diagram of the Coolidge X-ray tube and the photomultiplier tube — these are guaranteed 7-mark questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coolidge X-Ray Tube

### 2.1.1 Construction
The **Coolidge tube** (invented by William D. Coolidge in 1913) is a thermionic-emission, high-vacuum X-ray tube consisting of:
- **Cathode**: A tungsten filament heated to ~**2500 K** that emits electrons via thermionic emission.
- **Focusing Cup**: A molybdenum cup that directs electrons toward the anode target.
- **Anode (Target)**: A high-atomic-number (Z) material, typically **tungsten (Z = 74)**, embedded in a copper stem for heat dissipation.
- **Glass Envelope**: Borosilicate glass or metal-ceramic, evacuated to ~10⁻⁶ torr.
- **High-Voltage Circuit**: A step-up transformer providing **30 kV – 150 kV** between anode and cathode.

### 2.1.2 Principle of X-Ray Production
X-rays are produced by two distinct mechanisms when high-speed electrons strike the tungsten target:

1. **Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation)** — Continuous spectrum produced when decelerating electrons lose kinetic energy in the Coulomb field of target nuclei.
2. **Characteristic Radiation** — Discrete line spectrum produced when inner-shell electrons are knocked out and outer-shell electrons fill the vacancy, releasing photons of element-specific energy.

### 2.1.3 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1:** Filament current (typically **3–5 A**) heats the tungsten cathode to incandescence.
- **Step 2:** Thermionic emission releases electrons (Richardson-Dushman equation governs emission current).
- **Step 3:** A high potential difference (tube voltage, V) accelerates electrons toward the anode.
- **Step 4:** Electrons strike the focal spot at ~**50–70 % of the speed of light**, converting ~**99 %** of their kinetic energy into heat and ~**1 %** into X-rays.
- **Step 5:** The X-ray beam exits through a beryllium window (low Z, high transmission).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Less than **1 %** of input electrical energy is converted to X-rays. The remaining **>99 %** is wasted as heat — this is why anode cooling and rotating anodes are critical engineering challenges.

---

## 2.2 The Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)

### 2.2.1 Constructional Components
- **Electron Gun** — Heated cathode, control grid, focusing anode, accelerating anode.
- **Deflection System** — Two pairs of mutually perpendicular plates (horizontal & vertical) for **electrostatic deflection**, OR external coils for **magnetic deflection**.
- **Phosphor Screen** — P-31 (green) or P-39 (yellow) phosphor coated on the inner face, producing luminescence on electron impact.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle
Electrons emitted from the cathode are accelerated, focused into a narrow beam, deflected by the deflection plates, and strike the phosphor screen at variable (x, y) coordinates, producing a visible spot. Time-varying signals modulate the spot to display waveforms — making CRTs the foundational display for **patient monitors and oscilloscopes** in pre-flat-panel medical instrumentation.

---

## 2.3 The Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)

### 2.3.1 Construction
- **Photocathode** — A thin layer of low work-function material (e.g., **GaAs:Cs** or **Sb-K-Cs bialkali**) deposited on the inner surface of the input window.
- **Electron-Optic Input System** — Focuses photoelectrons onto the first dynode.
- **Dynode Chain** — Typically **8 to 14 stages** of secondary-emission electrodes (e.g., **Be-Cu**, **GaP:Cs**) biased at progressively higher potentials.
- **Anode** — Collects the multiplied electron cascade, producing a measurable current pulse.

### 2.3.2 Working Logic
- A photon strikes the photocathode → **photoelectric effect** releases a primary electron.
- The primary electron is accelerated to the first dynode → on impact, releases **δ secondary electrons** (δ ≈ 4–10).
- This cascading multiplication continues through N dynodes.
- The resulting anode current is **6–8 orders of magnitude** larger than the original photoelectron signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Application:** PMTs are the heart of **gamma cameras** in nuclear medicine, **scintillation detectors** in PET, and **luminometers** in immunoassay analysers. They detect the faint scintillation light produced when gamma rays interact with a NaI(Tl) crystal.

---

## 2.4 The Image Intensifier Tube

Used in **real-time X-ray fluoroscopy** to amplify the brightness of an X-ray image ~**1000–3000 times**, allowing reduction of patient dose.

### 2.4.1 Key Components
- **Input Phosphor (CsI:Na)** — Converts incident X-ray photons into visible-light photons.
- **Photocathode** — Converts the visible light into photoelectrons.
- **Electrostatic Focusing Lens** — Inverts and accelerates the electron image.
- **Output Phosphor (ZnS:Ag)** — Converts accelerated electrons back into a bright, minified visible image.
- **Output Window / Optical Coupling** — Routes the bright image to a CCD/CMOS camera or film.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ LATEX NOTE:** All absolute values and conditions below use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Duane–Hunt Limit (Min Wavelength) | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$ | h = Planck's const, c = speed of light, e = electron charge, V = tube voltage | metres (m) |
| 2 | Maximum Photon Energy | $E_{\max} = eV$ | Energy of most energetic X-ray photon | Joules (J) / eV |
| 3 | Photon Frequency | $\nu_{\max} = \dfrac{eV}{h}$ | Max frequency from Bremsstrahlung cut-off | Hz |
| 4 | X-Ray Intensity (Bremsstrahlung) | $I \propto ZV^{2}i$ | Z = target atomic number, V = tube voltage, i = tube current | arbitrary |
| 5 | X-Ray Tube Efficiency | $\eta = KZV$ | K ≈ 1.1 × 10⁻⁹ V⁻¹ (constant) | dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | Heat in Anode | $H = Vi - P_{xray}$ | Power lost as heat = input − X-ray output | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Anode Heat Capacity (Rotating) | $H_{max} = \dfrac{0.907 \cdot W \cdot T_{max}}{t}$ | W = focal track width, T_max = max target temp, t = exposure time | HU (Heat Units) |
| 8 | PMT Total Gain | $G = \sigma^{N}$ | σ = secondary emission ratio per dynode, N = number of dynodes | dimensionless |
| 9 | PMT Anode Current | $I_{a} = G \cdot e \cdot N_{pe}$ | e = electron charge, N_pe = photoelectrons per second | Amperes (A) |
| 10 | CRT Electrostatic Deflection | $y = \dfrac{LD}{2dV_{a}} \cdot V_{d}$ | L = length of plates, D = screen distance, d = plate separation, V_a = accelerating voltage, V_d = deflection voltage | metres (m) |
| 11 | CRT Magnetic Deflection | $y = \dfrac{eBLD}{2mV_{a}} \cdot V_{a}$ (rearranged) | B = magnetic flux density, L = coil length, m = electron mass | metres (m) |
| 12 | Richardson-Dushman Emission | $J = A_{0}T^{2}e^{-\phi \vert kT}$ | A₀ = Richardson const, T = temp, φ = work function, k = Boltzmann const | A/m² |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diagnostic Radiology:** Coolidge tubes in CT scanners and digital radiography systems.
- **Nuclear Medicine:** PMTs coupled to NaI(Tl) crystals for gamma-ray detection in SPECT/PET.
- **Fluoroscopy Suites:** Image intensifiers allow real-time visualisation of catheter placement, GI tract studies, and interventional procedures.
- **Cardiology & Critical Care:** CRTs in legacy bedside monitors (now largely replaced by TFT-LCD/OLED displays).
- **Radiation Therapy (Linac interface):** Thyratron-based modulators control pulsed electron beam extraction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Duane–Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength of X-Rays)

### Statement
The minimum wavelength of the continuous X-ray spectrum emitted by an X-ray tube is determined by the maximum kinetic energy an electron can lose in a single interaction, which equals the entire accelerating voltage times the electron charge.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy of an accelerated electron:**
When an electron (charge = $e$) is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, it acquires kinetic energy:

$$
E_{k} = eV
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum photon energy:**
In the limiting case of a head-on collision with a target nucleus, the electron loses **all** its kinetic energy to a single photon. By energy conservation:

$$
E_{photon,\max} = E_{k} = eV
$$

**Step 3 — Relate photon energy to frequency and wavelength:**
Using the Planck–Einstein relation $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$$
\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for minimum wavelength:**

$$
\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute constants:**
Using $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$:

$$
hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}
$$

Converting to a more useful form (using $V$ in kV):

$$
\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V(\text{volts})}\ \text{metres} = \dfrac{1.24}{V(\text{kV})}\ \text{nm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion shortcut for KTU numericals:** $\lambda_{\min}(\text{Å}) = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{volts})}$ — where $1\ \text{Å} = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — X-Ray Tube Efficiency (η)

### Statement
The efficiency of X-ray production in a Coolidge tube is the ratio of X-ray energy emitted to the total electrical energy supplied to the tube.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total electrical power input:**

$$
P_{\text{input}} = Vi
$$

**Step 2 — X-ray power output (Bremsstrahlung-dominated):**
Empirically (and from Kramers' law), the X-ray intensity is proportional to $ZV^{2}i$. Converting to a power efficiency form:

$$
P_{\text{xray}} = K \cdot Z \cdot V^{2} \cdot i
$$

**Step 3 — Define efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{xray}}}{P_{\text{input}}} = \dfrac{K \cdot Z \cdot V^{2} \cdot i}{V \cdot i}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
\eta = KZV
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check:**
For $V = 100\ \text{kV}$, $Z = 74$ (tungsten), $K \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}^{-1}$:

$$
\eta = (1.1 \times 10^{-9}) \times 74 \times 100{,}000 = 0.00814 = 0.814\ \%
$$

This confirms the **~1 % efficiency rule** commonly tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Photomultiplier Tube Gain

### Statement
The total electrical gain of a PMT is the product of secondary emission ratios across all dynode stages.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Single dynode multiplication:**
If a dynode has secondary emission ratio $\sigma$ (typically 4–10), then for **1** incident electron:

$$
N_{1} = \sigma\ \text{secondaries emerge from first dynode}
$$

**Step 2 — Two dynodes:**

$$
N_{2} = \sigma \cdot N_{1} = \sigma^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — By induction, after N dynodes:**

$$
G = \sigma^{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical numerical:**
For $\sigma = 5$, $N = 10$ dynodes:

$$
G = 5^{10} = 9{,}765{,}625 \approx 10^{7}
$$

**Step 5 — Anode current for a single photoelectron event:**

$$
I_{a} = e \cdot G \cdot N_{pe}
$$

For $N_{pe} = 1$ (single photon), $G = 10^{7}$:

$$
I_{a} = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times 10^{7} = 1.6 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{A} = 1.6\ \text{pA}
$$

This is a measurable current pulse — hence PMT's single-photon sensitivity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — CRT Electrostatic Deflection

### Statement
The vertical displacement $y$ of an electron spot on a CRT phosphor screen depends on the accelerating voltage, deflection plate voltage, and tube geometry.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Acceleration through V_a:**
The electron entering the deflection region has axial velocity:

$$
v_{a} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV_{a}}{m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Transverse acceleration inside the plates:**
The electric field between the plates is $E = V_{d}/d$, so transverse acceleration:

$$
a_{y} = \dfrac{eE}{m} = \dfrac{eV_{d}}{md}
$$

**Step 3 — Time spent between the plates of length L:**

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{v_{a}}
$$

**Step 4 — Transverse velocity at exit:**

$$
v_{y} = a_{y} \cdot t = \dfrac{eV_{d}L}{mdv_{a}}
$$

**Step 5 — Vertical displacement on the screen (extra distance D from plate exit):**

$$
y = v_{y} \cdot \dfrac{D}{v_{a}} + \dfrac{1}{2}a_{y}\left(\dfrac{L}{v_{a}}\right)^{2}
$$

Using the **small-angle approximation** (paraxial assumption), the second term dominates and:

$$
\boxed{y = \dfrac{LD}{2dV_{a}} \cdot V_{d}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Deflection Sensitivity** = $\dfrac{y}{V_{d}} = \dfrac{LD}{2dV_{a}}$. Increasing $V_a$ reduces sensitivity (spot moves less per volt of deflection), but improves sharpness — a key KTU design tradeoff.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tube Comparison for Quick Recall

| Feature | X-Ray Tube (Coolidge) | Photomultiplier Tube | Image Intensifier | CRT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input Energy** | Electrical (V, I) | Photons (visible/UV) | X-ray photons | Electrons (from cathode) |
| **Output** | X-ray photons | Amplified current pulse | Bright visible image | Visible spot on phosphor |
| **Key Material** | W target, Cu anode | GaAs:Cs photocathode | CsI:Na input, ZnS:Ag output | P-31 phosphor |
| **Vacuum Required** | Yes (10⁻⁶ torr) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Biomedical Use** | Radiography, CT | Gamma cameras, PET | Fluoroscopy | Oscilloscope, monitors |
| **Critical Parameter** | Tube voltage (kV) | Dynode gain σᴺ | Brightness gain | Deflection sensitivity |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, labelled diagrams, and one-line principles. Aim for **3–5 crisp lines** with at least one supporting numerical or diagram in words.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a Coolidge X-ray tube and state its principle of operation.

**Model Answer:**
The Coolidge X-ray tube is a **thermionic, high-vacuum, hot-cathode X-ray tube** invented by W.D. Coolidge (1913). It consists of a heated tungsten filament (cathode) and a tungsten target (anode) enclosed in an evacuated glass envelope, with a high potential difference (30–150 kV) applied between them.

**Principle:** Thermionic emission of electrons from the heated cathode, followed by acceleration through a high potential difference, causes the electrons to strike the tungsten target. Their sudden deceleration produces **X-ray photons via Bremsstrahlung and characteristic radiation** mechanisms.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is a photomultiplier tube (PMT)? List any two biomedical applications.

**Model Answer:**
A **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** is a vacuum tube that converts incident photons into a measurable, greatly amplified electrical current using the **photoelectric effect** at the photocathode and **secondary emission** at a chain of dynodes. Total gain can reach **10⁶ – 10⁸**.

**Biomedical Applications:**
1. **Gamma cameras** in nuclear medicine for detecting 140 keV photons from ⁹⁹ᵐTc.
2. **Scintillation counting** in PET/SPECT systems (coupled to NaI(Tl) or BGO crystals).
3. *Optional:* Luminometers in chemiluminescence immunoassays.

---

---

## PART B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, with Internal Sub-Parts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory** — students must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Coolidge X-ray tube**. Discuss the two mechanisms of X-ray production. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (labelled diagram in words):**
- **Glass Envelope:** Evacuated, high-vacuum (~10⁻⁶ torr) chamber.
- **Cathode:** Tungsten filament, heated to ~2500 K by a separate low-voltage (3–5 A) filament current.
- **Focusing Cup:** Molybdenum cup that shapes the electron stream into a narrow beam aimed at the focal spot.
- **Anode:** Tungsten target (Z = 74) embedded in a copper stem, mounted at an angle (~20°) to direct the useful beam through a beryllium window.
- **Beryllium Window:** Low-Z, mechanically strong, high X-ray transmission.
- **High-Voltage Supply:** 30–150 kV from a step-up transformer + rectifier.

**Working:**
1. Filament current heats the cathode → **thermionic emission** releases electrons (Richardson-Dushman law).
2. The high potential $V$ between cathode and anode accelerates electrons to kinetic energy $eV$.
3. Electrons strike the focal spot at velocities up to ~70 % the speed of light.
4. >99 % of energy is converted to **heat**; ~1 % emerges as X-rays.
5. X-ray beam exits through the beryllium window and is collimated toward the patient.

**Two Mechanisms of X-ray Production:**
- **Bremsstrahlung (Continuous Spectrum):** When an electron passes near a target nucleus, the Coulomb field decelerates and deflects it, converting kinetic energy into a photon. Photon energy varies continuously from 0 up to $eV$, giving a continuous spectrum. Intensity $\propto ZV^{2}i$.
- **Characteristic Radiation (Line Spectrum):** When an incident electron ejects a K-shell electron of the target atom, an L-shell electron drops into the K-vacancy, emitting a **Kα photon** of element-specific energy. The spectrum shows discrete lines (Kα, Kβ) characteristic of the target material.

---

**Q.A. (b)** An X-ray tube operates at **100 kV** and **5 mA**. Find (i) the minimum wavelength of emitted X-rays, (ii) the efficiency if the target is tungsten, and (iii) the heat dissipated per second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 100\ \text{kV} = 10^{5}\ \text{V}$, $i = 5\ \text{mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$, $Z = 74$ (tungsten), $K = 1.1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}^{-1}$.

**(i) Minimum wavelength (Duane–Hunt Law):**

$$
\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V(\text{volts})}\ \text{m} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{5}} = 1.24 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = 0.124\ \text{Å} = 0.0124\ \text{nm}}
$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = KZV = (1.1 \times 10^{-9}) \times 74 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\eta = 8.14 \times 10^{-3} = 0.814\ \%
$$

**(iii) Heat dissipated per second:**

$$
P_{\text{input}} = Vi = (10^{5}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) = 500\ \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{xray}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{input}} = 0.00814 \times 500 = 4.07\ \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{heat}} = P_{\text{input}} - P_{\text{xray}} = 500 - 4.07 \approx 495.93\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{heat}} \approx 496\ \text{W}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This numerical is a **guaranteed KTU problem** — practice the same calculation with $V = 80\ \text{kV}$, $i = 10\ \text{mA}$ or $V = 120\ \text{kV}$, $i = 2\ \text{mA}$ for full confidence.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q.B. (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)**. Derive an expression for its total gain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (labelled diagram in words):**
- **Input Window:** Borosilicate glass or UV-transmitting material.
- **Photocathode:** Thin layer of low work-function material (e.g., Sb-K-Cs, GaAs:Cs) on inner surface of window.
- **Electron-Optic System:** Focuses emitted photoelectrons onto the first dynode.
- **Dynode Chain:** 8–14 stages of secondary-emission electrodes (Be-Cu or GaP), each biased ~100 V higher than the previous.
- **Anode:** Final collector of multiplied electron cascade.
- **Voltage Divider Network:** Provides graded potentials across the dynode chain.

**Working:**
1. An incoming photon strikes the photocathode → **photoelectric effect** releases a primary photoelectron.
2. The photoelectron is accelerated toward **Dynode 1** (at +100 V).
3. On impact, each primary electron releases **σ secondary electrons** (σ = 4–10 for typical dynodes).
4. The secondary electrons are accelerated to Dynode 2, where each releases σ more, and so on.
5. After N dynodes, the cascade reaches the anode as a measurable current pulse.

**Derivation of Total Gain:**
If σ = average secondary emission ratio per dynode and N = number of dynodes:
- Output from 1st dynode: σ electrons
- Output from 2nd dynode: σ² electrons
- By induction, after N dynodes:

$$
\boxed{G = \sigma^{N}}
$$

For $\sigma = 5$, $N = 10$: $G = 5^{10} = 9.76 \times 10^{6} \approx 10^{7}$.

---

**Q.B. (b)** A gamma camera uses a PMT with **10 dynodes**, each having a secondary emission ratio of **6**. Calculate (i) the total gain, (ii) the anode current when **1000 photons per second** strike the photocathode (assume quantum efficiency = 25 %), and (iii) the minimum wavelength of X-rays that can be detected by a 90 kV X-ray tube used to excite the scintillator crystal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 10$, $\sigma = 6$, photon rate = 1000/s, $\eta_{QE} = 0.25$, $V = 90\ \text{kV}$.

**(i) Total Gain:**

$$
G = \sigma^{N} = 6^{10}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(6^{10}) = 10 \log_{10}(6) = 10 \times 0.7782 = 7.782
$$

$$
G = 10^{7.782} = 6.05 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\boxed{G \approx 6.05 \times 10^{7}}
$$

**(ii) Anode current:**

Number of photoelectrons per second:

$$
N_{pe} = 1000 \times 0.25 = 250\ \text{photoelectrons/s}
$$

Anode current:

$$
I_{a} = G \cdot e \cdot N_{pe} = (6.05 \times 10^{7}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times 250
$$

$$
I_{a} = 6.05 \times 10^{7} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-17} = 2.42 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{a} \approx 2.42\ \text{nA}}
$$

**(iii) Minimum X-ray wavelength at 90 kV:**

$$
\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{4}} = 1.378 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\min} \approx 0.138\ \text{Å}}
$$

---

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in $KZV$ formula** — The constant $K = 1.1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}^{-1}$ is dimensionally subtle; students often lose 1 mark for writing $K = 1.1 \times 10^{-9}$ without units.
> 2. **Mixing $\lambda_{\min}$ forms** — Always state $V$ in **volts** when using $\lambda = 1.24 \times 10^{-6}/V$ (result in metres), and in **kV** when using $\lambda = 1.24/V$ (result in nm).
> 3. **PMT gain — wrong exponent** — Common error: writing $G = N\sigma$ (additive) instead of $G = \sigma^{N}$ (multiplicative). This is a **2-mark loss** trap.
> 4. **CRT deflection** — Forgetting to mention that **deflection is inversely proportional to $V_a$** (accelerating voltage). Examiners specifically look for this trade-off comment.
> 5. **Heat dissipation** — Students often forget that $>99\ \%$ of input power is heat; always compute heat as $P_{\text{input}} - P_{\text{xray}}$, not directly from $Vi$.
> 6. **Diagrams** — Unlabelled diagrams fetch **0/7** in Part B. Always use arrows and label every component: cathode, anode, focusing cup, target, window, deflection plates, dynodes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Coolidge Tube** = thermionic, hot-cathode, tungsten target, efficiency ~1 %.
- ✅ **Duane–Hunt Law:** $\lambda_{\min} = 1.24 \times 10^{-6} / V$ (V in volts, λ in metres).
- ✅ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = KZV$ (K = 1.1 × 10⁻⁹ V⁻¹).
- ✅ **X-ray intensity:** $I \propto ZV^{2}i$ — Bremsstrahlung dominated.
- ✅ **X-ray mechanisms:** Bremsstrahlung (continuous) + Characteristic (Kα, Kβ lines).
- ✅ **PMT gain:** $G = \sigma^{N}$ (exponential growth with dynode count).
- ✅ **PMT applications:** Gamma camera, PET, scintillation counting, luminometer.
- ✅ **Image Intensifier:** CsI:Na input → photocathode → electrostatic focus → ZnS:Ag output. Brightness gain ~1000–3000×.
- ✅ **CRT components:** Electron gun (cathode + grid + focusing anode), deflection plates, phosphor screen.
- ✅ **CRT deflection sensitivity:** $S = y / V_{d} = LD / (2dV_a)$ — decreases with higher $V_a$.
- ✅ **Typical tube voltages:** Diagnostic X-ray = 30–150 kV; Mammography = 25–35 kV; CT = 80–140 kV.
- ✅ **Work function units:** Always in eV when dealing with thermionic emission.
- ✅ **Vacuum level:** All biomedical tubes require ~10⁻⁶ torr for unimpeded electron travel.
- ✅ **Kramers' Law foundation:** X-ray photon flux per unit wavelength $\propto ZV(\lambda_{\min} - \lambda)/\lambda^{2}$.
- ✅ **MTF & Resolution** (advanced): Image intensifier resolution ~2–4 line pairs/mm at output.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Advice:** Memorise the *labelled diagram + 4 key formulas* for **Coolidge tube** and **PMT**. These alone secure ~14 marks in Part B. Keep a separate 1-page formula sheet with $\lambda_{\min}$, $\eta$, $G$, and $S_{CRT}$.
